leads.why: reclaimSquatter has multiple exit points (line ~298/1610 return false with no closure when a doomed sibling lease is funded, later returns true after CloseLease loop, and a third return true for the victim==nil branch) — a new caller of this function, or a future edit reordering the fail-closed check after some closures already ran, is exactly the shape the playbook's class-1 tell names; worth confirming no partial-closure path exists between the fail-closed scan and the closing loop.

releaseRun is the sole place that closes a terminal run's leases and drops its pods, but nothing inside it checks run.Status.Phase — its safety depends entirely on every caller setting the terminal phase either just before (failRun, applyResolution default, HandleNodeFailure's post-loop sweep) or just after (completeRun) calling it, which is asserted only by the doc comment 'CALL IT ONLY ON A TERMINAL RUN.'

completeRun calls c.releaseRun(run, "Completed", now) BEFORE assigning run.Status.Phase = RunPhaseComplete, so releaseRun executes while the run's stored phase is still whatever it was (e.g. Running) — inconsistent ordering versus failRun/applyResolution which set the terminal phase first; benign today only because nothing reads Phase in between, which is exactly the kind of ordering fragility class 1/2 flags.

the comment 'CALL IT ONLY ON A TERMINAL RUN' is the entire enforcement mechanism for releaseRun's precondition; no assertion, panic, or invariant check inside releaseRun verifies the run is actually terminal before it deletes every pod the run has.

the long comment above 'ev := c.evaluate(now)' argues at length that taking the funding evaluation once (rather than per-iteration) is safe and one-directional, calling the staleness 'known low-severity' and 'tracked as task #54' — this is a design decision defended entirely in prose, not enforced by any check, and the playbook explicitly warns that comments defending a known-stale derivation are the highest-yield thing to re-derive with a test rather than accept.

the package doc lists four invariants 'considered and REJECTED... because each is false in a state this engine legally produces' — these are prose claims about the engine's legal state space (e.g. 'a lease naming a node absent from the cluster is an orphan' being false because cordoned nodes are dropped from the snapshot); nothing runs to confirm these four are still false under the current bridge/reconciler code, only the comment asserts it.

'for runKey, phase := range phases' iterates a map (runPhaseTracker) and calls c.releaseRun(run, ...) in the body for every run whose final tracked phase is Failed — order-dependent by construction of Go map iteration; appears safe because each run's cleanup is independent, but it is exactly the syntactic shape (map range + body writing observable state) the class-3 tell asks to be checked, and it is new code introduced by this branch (post-loop sweep after reclaimSquatter's phases.apply calls).

fenced() reads Node deletion/out-of-service-taint via the APIReader and is called twice — once outside the bridge lock (a 'cheap filter... decides nothing') and once inside WithWorld's closure as 'THE VERDICT' — confirm the outside-lock call cannot itself be treated as authoritative anywhere else in the reconcile path, since a second, differently-scoped read of the same signal is the exact shape that produced task #36.

removeRunPodsOnNodes and reclaimSquatter's nodesOfSlots deliberately coarsen node#ordinal SLOT identity down to MACHINE identity for pod eviction ('the pod plane cannot address a slot') — this is an acknowledged, documented coarsening (not a bug by itself) but is precisely the identity-coarsening shape the playbook's class 5 flags, and it means any FUNDED lease of the SAME run sharing that machine must be excluded by the fail-closed loop in reclaimSquatter (lines ~1498-1540) or it would be silently evicted alongside the unfunded squatter.

the pass-2 spare-conflict loop 'for j := range c.State.Leases' has a role/index-skip guard (j == spareIdx || j == i) and an occupancy-match predicate (leaseOccupiesSlots) applied in sequence, then branches on otherKey == runKey vs funding class — worth confirming the skip guards never suppress a same-run stale lease that should have been closed, since 'handled'/'crossRunConflict' boolean flags downstream (line 1285) gate whether failGroupWithoutSpare runs at all.

openRunLeasesOnNodes filters leases by (closed, run-key, then node-membership) in that order inside a for loop building the 'doomed' list that reclaimSquatter's fail-closed funding check iterates — if a lease of the victim run is Closed already it is silently excluded before the node-match test runs, which is fine only if closed leases can never legitimately need to be re-examined for the fail-closed guard; confirm no legal state has a lease that should count toward 'this run still has funded work on these nodes' but got filtered out here for being already-closed by an earlier pass in the same reconcile.

groupIndexForPodIndex is an admitted second implementation of pack.deriveGroups' grouping rule ('duplicated here rather than exported from pkg/pack... The two are pinned together by a test') — pinning by test reduces but does not eliminate drift risk if pack.deriveGroups' bucketing algorithm changes in a way the pin test's fixed set of {total, groupGPUs, gpusPerPod} tuples does not exercise.

leaseGroupIndex is now the single accessor for a lease's group label, but it was introduced to replace at least three call sites that previously each defaulted a missing group to "0" independently (collectElasticGroups, findSpareLease, the emit message in pass 1) — worth checking every non-test call site was actually migrated to leaseGroupIndex rather than a lingering direct label read that still defaults silently.

TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode changed the run's declared TotalGPUs from 4 to 2 ('so the widths agree' with the new WidthAssembled invariant) — this changes what scenario the test exercises; confirm the original 4-GPU-run-with-2-GPU-leases scenario (a run under its declared width holding only a spare-only failed node) is still covered somewhere, since narrowing a fixture to make the invariant happy can silently drop coverage of the state the test used to represent.

mirrorPods synthesizes exactly ONE pod per open lease using only lease.Spec.Slice.Nodes[0] as the pod's NodeName, even for a multi-node/multi-slot lease — inserted into twelve existing fixtures that pin pod-deleting code paths (swap, reclaim, terminal release); if any of those paths' correctness depends on per-node pod counts or on a lease's other nodes also having represented pods, mirrorPods' single-pod-per-lease simplification could make the test pass while not exercising the multi-pod deletion logic it claims to pin.

every eviction/terminal test in this new file explicitly checks len(open) == len(pods) or similar two-plane parity assertions per the playbook's class-9 rail, which is good practice, but the file's own header cites the specific adjudication record (2026-07-09-r27-invariant-oracle-98b602d) it was written to close — confirm each test's mutation-tested (delete the CloseLease/removeRunPodsOnNodes call and re-run) rather than merely reading as plausible.

applyResolution's terminal 'default:' branch now calls c.releaseRun(run, "RunFailed", now) after setting RunPhaseFailed — a new call site of releaseRun; confirm the resolver's Scope-limited view of leases (pkg/resolver only sees leases inside the reservation's scope) can't cause this branch to be reached for a run whose leases the resolver already partially closed via its own action loop earlier in the same function, which would double-count or mis-order closures.

result.summary: I read docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md in full, then ran `git diff main...HEAD --stat` (47 files, ~8059 insertions/593 deletions across run_controller.go, invariants.go, settle.go, bridge.go, reconcilers.go, pkg/invariant, and ~10 new/expanded test files) and read the full diffs of controllers/run_controller.go, controllers/invariants.go, controllers/settle.go, controllers/kube/bridge.go, controllers/kube/reconcilers.go, pkg/invariant/invariant.go, pkg/admission/admission.go, cmd/scheduler/plugin/plugin.go, controllers/two_plane_test.go, controllers/ledger_mirror_test.go, controllers/group_identity_test.go, and the smaller test-fixture diffs (completion_test.go, cutover_test.go, eta_test.go, gang_width_test.go, node_failure_test.go, run_controller_test.go, scenario_test.go, gang_test.go). The diff is definitely non-empty and substantial. I mechanically scanned the changed lines and their containing functions against each of the eight named tell classes: reclaimSquatter and releaseRun for multi-return/immortal-lease shapes; the heavy prose comments defending the once-per-call funding evaluation, the four rejected invariants, and releaseRun's terminal-only precondition for comment-as-enforcement; the new `for runKey, phase := range phases` map-range loop for last-writer-wins; NodeReconciler.fenced's double-call structure for signal-vs-reality; nodesOfSlots/removeRunPodsOnNodes for identity coarsening from slot to machine granularity; the guard-then-predicate ordering in the pass-2 spare-conflict loop and in openRunLeasesOnNodes for guard-before-predicate; leaseGroupIndex and groupIndexForPodIndex as newly-centralized versus newly-duplicated obligations for cloned-obligation; and the modified/added test fixtures (especially the GPU-count edit in node_failure_test.go and the single-pod-per-lease synthesis in ledger_mirror_test.go's mirrorPods) for test-asserts-the-bug. I did not find any hits for class 7's narrow tell ('.Status.Closed' write outside func CloseLease in a non-test file) beyond the already-migrated call sites, which appear correctly routed through CloseLease/controllers.CloseLease everywhere I checked (applyResolution, cleanupDeletedRun, shrinkRun, settle.go). All findings below are leads for the reviewing panel to adjudicate, not confirmed defects — several (the once-per-call evaluation staleness, the coarse machine-granularity eviction, the four rejected invariants) are explicitly flagged and defended in the diff's own comments as deliberate, documented tradeoffs, and are included here per the task's instruction to over-report rather than filter based on my own judgment of correctness.

result:

I have completed a thorough class-4/5/6 review with executed mutation confirmation on every load-bearing guard. All four protections in my lens are real and pinned by tests; I found no new defect. Here is my report.

What is genuinely new is the Pods field itself; both invariants that read it were added in the same commit (4e832a7), so there is no 'previous projection' that tolerated a state these now reject. Within the new design, counting spare pods cuts both ways: for INV-LEASE-HAS-POD it can only SATISFY the check, never trigger it — which MASKS the exact defect class the invariant was written for: a victim whose whole ACTIVE pod set is deleted passes as long as one spare pod survives anywhere (executed: TestASurvivingSparePodMasksAWholeActivePodSetDeletion, no panic on an open active lease with zero active pods). For INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS, spare pods widen the trigger, but the only legal state I found where that fires is the same async-deletion window that any role's pod produces; engine paths (releaseRun/removeAllRunPods) drop spares with everything else. One footnote-grade in-state half-plane: HandleNodeFailure pass 1 (run_controller.go:1194-1212) closes a spare lease on the failed node without removing its spare pod from state.Pods — no invariant fires (no open lease remains) and reality Pod-GCs the fenced node's pods, but the quiescence driver's model retains such pods forever. Also note the projection counts Succeeded/Failed pods as 'holding a GPU', which is factually wrong — a terminal-phase pod's resources are freed — and that error is a load-bearing half of the Q2 refutation.",
    "4. AwaitingMint = activePods[key] > openActiveLeases[key] gates INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED. mirrorPods() adds exactly one pod per open lease. Does that make AwaitingMint FALSE in fixtures where it was previously true — and if so, has mirrorPods silently ENABLED a width check that those twelve tests were never written to satisfy, or silently DISABLED one? Which twelve tests, and what changed for each?": "Neither enabled nor disabled — AwaitingMint was FALSE before mirrorPods and stays FALSE after. With zero pods, activePods=0 and 0 > N is false for any N; with exactly one pod per open lease, mirrored pods carry the lease's ROLE, so spare-lease mirrors are excluded from activePods exactly as spare leases are from openActiveLeases, leaving the two counts equal — still false. The width check was therefore armed on all twelve fixtures from the moment the oracle landed (7a681c0), via KnownToLedger=anyLease; that is precisely why fixtures were narrowed in that commit (e.g. node_failure_test.go:83-86 comment on TotalGPUs 4→2). Empirically confirmed both directions (TestOnePodPerLeaseKeepsTheWidthInvariantArmed): one pod per lease → INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED fires on an under-width Running run; a second unminted pod → AwaitingMint disarms it. The twelve call sites/tests: gang_width_test.go:181 TestReconcileDoesNotAdoptSpareOnlyLeases, :214 TestReconcileDoesNotAdoptOnGrowLeasesAlone, :239 TestSwapLeasesCountTowardGangWidth, :267 TestMalleableRunAdoptsAtMinWidth; run_controller_test.go:974 TestReconcileAdoptsHalfAppliedAdmission, :1049 TestActivateReservationAdoptsHalfAppliedActivation; node_failure_test.go:92 TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode, :123 TestNodeFailureReturnsTypedSentinelWhenNoLeaseNamesTheNode, :154 TestSwapLeavesACoLocatedRunOnOtherSlotsAlone, :190 TestSwapDeclinesRatherThanEvictAFundedRunOnTheSpareSlots, :297 TestDecliningTheSwapNeverStrandsTheRunsOwnSpare, :351 TestDecliningTheSwapReleasesTheSpareEvenWhenTheRunParksInCheckpointGrace. What changed for each: the fixture became INV-LEASE-HAS-POD-legal, and the pod-deleting halves of the paths under test (removeSparePodOnNodes in the swap tests, releaseRun's cull in the failure tests, adoption against a populated pod plane) now run against non-empty slices — though most of the twelve still assert only on leases; the pod-plane assertions live in two_plane_test.go. Residual weakness: mirrorPods places the pod on Slice.Nodes[0] only; harmless for today's single-node-lease fixtures, but a future multi-node-lease fixture would under-represent its pod set and could pass a per-node deletion path it does not exercise.",
    "5. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is checked for terminal runs too (the loop runs it before the `if r.Terminal { continue }`? read the code and say exactly). Is a terminal run with open leases and no pods reported once or twice, and does the ordering of the checks in CheckSteady change which violation an author sees first?": "It is NOT checked for terminal runs. In CheckSteady (pkg/invariant/invariant.go:248-283) the terminal block runs first — TerminalPresent (:250), then TerminalNoPods (:261), then `continue` (:271) — and the LeaseHasPod check sits after it (:273), so a terminal run never reaches it. Executed: a Failed run with one open lease and zero pods yields exactly [INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT] — reported ONCE (TerminalNoPods cannot also fire because Pods==0 is its pass condition). Within one run the append order is fixed: TerminalPresent before TerminalNoPods before (never) LeaseHasPod. Across runs, snapshotWorld ranges the c.State.Runs MAP (controllers/invariants.go:87), so the order of Runs in the World — and hence which run's violation an author sees FIRST in the banner — is randomized per process; cosmetic rather than semantic, because Panic prints all violations, but a class-3-shaped wobble worth knowing when diffing failure output.",
    "6. Take the single best refutation you found. Write the Go test, run it, and paste the real output. If you cannot refute either invariant, say so plainly and name the strongest legal state you tried.": "Best refutation: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD versus a drained node (TestLeaseHasPodFiresOnADrainedNode, scratchpad module invrefute, run against the real engine via a replace directive). Real output: '================ INVARIANT VIOLATION ================ | site: RunController.Reconcile | INV-LEASE-HAS-POD: run default/train: holds 1 open lease(s) (train-active-0-lease) and not one pod — the ledger is billing a budget and reserving GPUs for containers that do not exist | This state is illegal. If a test asserts it, the TEST is wrong: see docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md, class 8.' followed by 'after 20 simulated hours: the violation fired on 20 of 20 reconciles; the lease is still open, the run still Running' — --- PASS: TestLeaseHasPodFiresOnADrainedNode. The stimulus is a cordon+drain, the exact event R21 ruled must produce NO engine action, and the panic banner instructs the next author that any test modelling it is wrong. The companion refutation for INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS also ran red as predicted: 'INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS: run default/train: terminal (phase=Completed) but the workload plane still holds 1 pod(s)...' at site RunController.Reconcile on the pass immediately after a completion the engine performed correctly in both planes — and TestTerminalNoPodsCondemnsAnUnrelatedRunsReconcile shows the same terminating pod turning an unrelated healthy run's reconcile red."
  },

result.summary: Investigator examined the LAST-WRITER-WINS (playbook class 3) lens on the changed engine code, hunting for outcomes that depend on the order of state.Leases or on Go's randomized map iteration. The conclusion was that every run.Status.* write inside a loop in the diff falls into one of three provably order-independent shapes: a commutative severity-lattice fold (runPhaseTracker.apply, Running<Pending<Failed) used by HandleNodeFailure's pass-2 lease loop, failGroupWithoutSpare, and reclaimSquatter; a once-per-run map visit (applyResolution's affectedRuns loop and the post-loop phases sweep) where each run's verdict is computed purely from its own state; and a build-then-sort pattern (lender-share loop appends in map order then sort.Slice by Owner). The investigator ran the repo's exhaustive 24-ordering permutation test (TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder) and a 64-repetition map-iteration test (TestApplyResolutionOutcomeIsInvariantUnderMapIteration), both passing 20 consecutive times (go test -count=20), and reported the outcome as byte-invariant. To validate the rail is load-bearing rather than decorative, the investigator archived the tree to scratch and mutated reclaimSquatter's `phases.apply` fold into a bare `victim.Status.Phase = RunPhasePending` assignment; the mutation test then went RED, with the filler run reporting Pending in some lease orderings and Failed in others, matching the class-3 divergence described in the codebase's history docs. The investigator concluded this confirms both that the reclaimSquatter fix (tied to previously-open task #50) is real and that the invariant test genuinely detects class-3 defects. No class-3 (last-writer-wins) defect was found in the branch, and the findings list is empty.

answers.question: 1. Find every assignment to run.Status.* (Phase, Message, Width, CheckpointDeadline) that happens INSIDE a loop in the changed code. For each, is the write guarded by a comparison against what is already there, or does the last iteration simply win?
answers.conclusion: Three loops in the diff write run.Status.* per iteration, all guarded/independent. (a) HandleNodeFailure pass-2 loop (run_controller.go:1248) writes phase ONLY via runPhaseTracker.apply, which compares runPhaseSeverity[seen] >= runPhaseSeverity[phase] and keeps the WORST (line 1410) — a commutative fold, not last-writer; failGroupWithoutSpare (1450/1458) and reclaimSquatter (1563) both write through that same tracker. (b) applyResolution's `for runKey := range affectedRuns` (1833) writes Phase/Message/Width once per run, each verdict derived from that run's own allocated/expected/reclaimedOnly — no cross-run last-writer effect. (c) the post-loop sweep `for runKey, phase := range phases` (1368) calls releaseRun per Failed run, independent per run. All other Status writes (Reconcile, completeRun, failRun, activateReservation, reconcileElasticRun 2620-2657) are single-run-per-call ending in return, not loops. No unguarded in-loop write survives.

answers.question: 2. Find every `for k := range someMap` in the changed code. Go randomizes map iteration order per process. Does any observable outcome depend on that order?
answers.conclusion: The map-range loops in changed engine code are: run_controller.go:1368 (`for runKey, phase := range phases`), 1833 (`for runKey := range affectedRuns`), 2816/2819/2822 (lender owners), settle.go:130 (`for key, run := range state.Runs` building the doomed map), invariants.go:87 (read-only snapshot). None produces an order-dependent observable: 1368 and 1833 visit each distinct run once and compute per-run-independent results; the lender loop (2822) appends in map order but is immediately followed by sort.Slice on Owner (2829-2831), making output deterministic; settle.go's terminal-vs-orphan rules are mutually exclusive per key (a terminal run is known+non-nil so the orphan loops never touch it) so the doomed map has no last-writer conflict; invariants.go only reads. The resolver's leaseGroupIndex change is a grouping-KEY fix (R28b), not an ordering change. No observable outcome depends on map order.

answers.question: 3. Apply the metamorphic test: take a scenario the change exercises, SHUFFLE the order of state.Leases, replay, and compare the resulting run statuses and lease closure reasons byte-for-byte. Report whether the outcome is invariant. Run it several times. This is the definitive experiment — actually run it.
answers.conclusion: Executed. The repo's TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder replays ALL 24 orderings of a 5-lease node-failure (stronger than a random shuffle) and byte-compares the full fingerprint (every run phase+message, every lease closure reason, every pod); TestApplyResolutionOutcomeIsInvariantUnderMapIteration repeats 64x to sample map randomization. Both PASS 20 consecutive times (go test -count=20). Outcome is byte-invariant. To prove the rail is load-bearing, the investigator copied the tree to scratch and mutated reclaimSquatter's `phases.apply` fold to a bare `victim.Status.Phase = RunPhasePending`: the test immediately FAILED, with filler=Pending in order [1 2 3 4 0] but Failed in the canonical order — the exact class-3 nondeterminism. The fix is real and the test detects the class.

answers.question: 4. If an ordering dependence exists, is the correct fix a deterministic sort, or a COMMUTATIVE fold (a severity lattice, a post-loop sweep)? Argue for one. A deterministic order that happens to give the right answer is still a coincidence.
answers.conclusion: No ordering dependence remains, but the branch already applies the correct shape, and it is the COMMUTATIVE fold, not a sort. run_controller.go uses runPhaseSeverity (Running<Pending<Failed) so multiple writers to one run's phase JOIN to the worst verdict regardless of order (1410), and the terminal-run cleanup is a post-loop sweep (1368) explicitly moved out of the lease loop so 'the outcome does not depend on the order of c.State.Leases' (1367). This is right: a lattice join encodes the actual specification (a dead uncovered rank makes the run Failed no matter what a sibling group did), whereas sorting c.State.Leases would only make the last-visited lease deterministic — it would give a stable answer for a reason unrelated to correctness, i.e. a coincidence, exactly the failure mode the playbook and history-run-phase-writers.md warn against. The lender loop's build-then-sort (2829) is also correct there because that output is a set being serialized, not a fold over competing verdicts.

result.summary: I hunted for immortal leases (class 1) and cloned obligations (class 7) across the whole branch (git diff main...HEAD), reading the playbook, history-run-phase-writers.md, settle.go, invariants.go, the full run_controller.go diff, resolver.go, bridge.go, and the new test files. I traced every exit path of every lease-touching function the diff adds or modifies (completeRun, failRun, applyResolution, HandleNodeFailure's two passes and post-loop sweep, failGroupWithoutSpare, reclaimSquatter, releaseRun, removeAllRunPods, removeRunPodsOnNodes, openRunLeasesOnNodes, SettleLeases, CloseLease). Every terminal-phase assignment is paired with a whole-run closure (releaseRun or the post-loop sweep keyed on the runPhaseTracker), and the sole-closer rail (CloseLease + hack/antifake/soleclose.go, zero allowlist) holds: CloseLease is the ONLY non-test writer of Lease.Status.{Closed,Ended,ClosureReason} in the tree under review. I built an external test module in the scratchpad that imports the package, confirmed the invariant oracle is genuinely live there (a Completed run holding an open lease panics INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT/NO-PODS), then drove the most suspicious surviving path -- reclaimSquatter demoting an unfunded malleable victim to Pending while it retains a lease on an untouched node -- through HandleNodeFailure plus 20 Reconciles over 20 simulated hours: no lease leaked, no invariant fired, the leftover lease stayed backed by its pod. I found NO immortal lease introduced by this branch. I report one low-severity class-7 durability observation: groupIndexForPodIndex/groupSizesFor is a newly-introduced second implementation of pack.deriveGroups whose only drift detector is an example-based pin test (five tuples), not a property/exhaustive check.

answers.question: 1. Enumerate EVERY exit path (return, continue, break, panic, early error) of every function this change adds or modifies. For each, list which leases are open at that moment and which are closed. Name any path that exits with a lease open that should have been closed.
answers.conclusion: reclaimSquatter has four exits: (a) run_controller.go:1535 `return false` fires from the fail-closed loop BEFORE the closing loop at 1539, so NO victim lease is closed -- correct, the caller then declines the swap; there is provably no partial-closure path between the scan and the closes; (b) 1547 `return true` (victim Run gone) closes the on-node doomed leases and leaves off-node leases for SettleLeases' orphan-run rule; (c) 1559 `return true` (still >= min) and (d) 1573 `return true` (demoted Pending) both close on-node doomed leases AND remove on-node pods, leaving only off-node leases that still back live containers. releaseRun (single loop, then removeAllRunPods) always exits with every run lease closed and every pod dropped. failGroupWithoutSpare closes lease (+spare) then either parks Pending in checkpoint grace (leases of surviving groups deliberately open with running pods) or writes Failed via the tracker, whose run is swept by the post-loop loop at 1368-1380. completeRun/failRun/applyResolution-default each call releaseRun. No exit leaves a lease open that should be closed.

answers.question: 2. Does the change add a new CALLER to an existing function whose lease-closing behaviour was only correct for its old callers? failRun once carried the comment "It never holds leases at this point". Check every function the diff newly calls.
answers.conclusion: releaseRun (the renamed/expanded closeRunLeases) gains three new call sites: completeRun (534), applyResolution's Failed default (1888), and it replaces closeRunLeases in failRun (694) and the HandleNodeFailure post-loop sweep (1376). releaseRun now ALSO removes every pod (removeAllRunPods, 1668), so its correctness depends on the caller having a TERMINAL run. I verified all five callers are terminal: completeRun sets Complete, failRun sets Failed, applyResolution-default sets Failed, the post-loop sweep only fires for tracker phase==Failed. failRun's four callers (checkpoint-grace expiry 168, follow-cycle 566/587/597) are all hard-fail transitions where dropping all pods is correct; none is the checkpoint-grace-keep path (that goes through failGroupWithoutSpare, which never calls releaseRun). No new caller inherits a stale precondition.

answers.question: 3. Does the change set a run to a terminal phase (Failed / Completed) anywhere? For each such site, prove that every lease referencing that run is closed on the same path or by a sweep that provably runs. Cite the sweep.
answers.conclusion: Four terminal-phase sites, each with proven closure on the same call: completeRun (535 Complete) calls releaseRun at 534 first; failRun (688 Failed) calls releaseRun at 694; applyResolution default (1876 Failed) calls releaseRun at 1888; failGroupWithoutSpare (1458, Failed via phases.apply) is NOT closed inline -- deliberately -- but the run is closed by HandleNodeFailure's post-loop sweep `for runKey, phase := range phases { if phase != RunPhaseFailed { continue } ... c.releaseRun(run,...) }` (1368-1380), which runs unconditionally after the lease loop; every Failed writer in HandleNodeFailure goes through phases.apply so every Failed run is in the tracker. releaseRun scans ALL of c.State.Leases for the run key (not a scoped subset), so out-of-scope spares are caught. The deferred checkInvariants at each entry point asserts INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT on return; my positive-control test confirmed it panics on a terminal run with an open lease. Production adds a second backstop: Bridge.WithWorld calls SettleLeases (settle.go:127) which closes any open lease whose run is terminal or gone.

answers.question: 4. Does the change write Lease.Status.Closed anywhere other than closeLease()? Every clone of the closing obligation drifts. Name each site and say what closeLease does that the clone does not (events, metrics, ClosureReason).
answers.conclusion: No. A repo-wide grep for `Status.Closed =`/`Status.Ended =`/`Status.ClosureReason =` outside _test files finds exactly one write site in the tree under review: the body of CloseLease itself (run_controller.go:3032-3035). The previous clone -- applyResolution's inline `lease.Status.Closed = true; lease.Status.Ended = &ended; lease.Status.ClosureReason = action.Reason` -- was migrated to `CloseLease(lease, action.Reason, now)` at 1795 in this diff. hack/antifake/soleclose.go enforces this with a zero allowlist ("The permitted count is zero, forever"). CloseLease also carries the idempotency guard (`if lease.Status.Closed { return }`) so first-closure wins; settle.go and applyResolution reach it, so ClosureReason/Ended stamping is uniform. (The only other `Status.Closed`/`ClosureReason` occurrences in controllers are READS at invariants.go:106 and run_controller.go:2115, not writes. Stale copies under .claude/worktrees/ are other worktrees, not part of this module's sources.)

answers.question: 5. Take the single most suspicious path you found. Write a Go test that reaches it, drive Reconcile ~20 times over ~20 simulated hours, and assert the lease is closed. Report the actual test output. If it stays open you have a critical finding; if it closes, say what closed it and cite that code.
answers.conclusion: Most suspicious path: reclaimSquatter demoting an unfunded malleable victim to Pending (run_controller.go:1563) while it keeps an open lease on a node the swap never touched -- does that leftover lease leak across reconciles? I built an external module (scratchpad/leasehunt) so os.Args[0] ends in `.test` and the oracle is live (confirmed: a Completed run holding an open lease PANICS INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT/NO-PODS). Fixture: run `filler` (unfunded, Malleable MinTotalGPUs=4) holds `squat` on the spare's slots (node-b) and `keep` on node-c. After HandleNodeFailure(node-a): output `filler.phase="Pending" squat=true(ReclaimedBySpare) keep=false open=[keep] pods=[filler-keep]` -- squat closed in both planes, keep still backed by its pod. After 20 Reconciles over 20h: `filler.phase="Pending" open=[keep] pods=[filler-keep,filler-active-0..3]` -- NO panic, no leak; `keep` stays open because it backs a live container, and the extra pods are the legal awaiting-mint window (pods ahead of leases, which INV-LEASE-HAS-POD exempts). Nothing closed `keep` because nothing should: it is not an immortal lease. Conclusion: the path is sound. reclaimSquatter closes exactly the on-node doomed leases (1539-1541) and removes exactly their pods (1549), keeping the two planes consistent.

answers.question: Are the two new invariants (INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS, INV-LEASE-HAS-POD) reapers, and is the cloned-obligation surface guarded? (self-posed, within assigned lens)
answers.conclusion: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS is safe: it only fires for terminal runs, a state the swap window and checkpoint-grace never leave a run in. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is deliberately COARSE (open lease + zero pods for the whole run) and self-documents that per-lease correspondence is deferred to R26's auditor; it is exempted only where a run has any pod, so it is not a reaper but has a known blind spot (a run holding several leases where only one loses its container). The class-7 surface: CloseLease is unified (sole-closer rail); leaseGroupIndex exists in both controllers and pkg/resolver and both now return "" (no "0" default), kept in sync manually and backstopped by INV-GROUP-STAMPED; groupIndexForPodIndex/groupSizesFor is a NEW clone of pack.deriveGroups whose only drift detector is the five-tuple pin test -- adequate today, thin against a future bucketing change.

findings.title: groupIndexForPodIndex/groupSizesFor is a second implementation of pack.deriveGroups guarded only by an example-based pin test
findings.failure_scenario: If pack.deriveGroups' group layout is ever changed for a shape not in the pin's set {8/0, 8/4, 9/4, 12/4/2, 64/32} -- e.g. a change affecting total sizes divisible in a way none of those tuples exercise -- the spec-only clone used by topUpActiveGang re-emits a lost rank with a group index the packer never chose. INV-GROUP-STAMPED still passes (the label is present, just wrong), so the resolver's per-group lottery, the elastic shrink loop, and the node-failure spare pairing all silently address the wrong ranks, with nothing red.
findings.taxonomyClass: 7 CLONED OBLIGATION

result.summary: The investigator conducted a class-4/5/6 adversarial review (SIGNAL != REALITY, IDENTITY COARSENING, GUARD BEFORE PREDICATE) of the branch diff (git diff main...HEAD), reading run_controller.go (HandleNodeFailure pass 1/2, reclaimSquatter, openRunLeasesOnNodes, removeRunPodsOnNodes, nodesOfSlots, leaseGroupIndex), kube/reconcilers.go (fenced/nodeFailed/nodeReady, the double fenced call, the ErrNoLeaseOnNode swallow), kube/bridge.go (WithWorld + SettleLeases + load ordering), settle.go, admission.go, resolver.go, plugin.go, and the shipped RBAC. The investigator states they copied the repo to a scratch dir and ran four mutation tests, each reverting one load-bearing line and going RED: (1) reclaimSquatter's fail-closed return false pinned by TestSwapDeclinesWhenTheSquattersFundedWorkSharesTheMachine; (2) pass-1's close-the-spare-on-the-failed-node guard (R25) pinned by TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode and TestSwapNeverTargetsASpareOnTheFailedNode; (3) the R22 slot-vs-node granularity in the pass-2 conflict loop pinned by TestSwapLeavesACoLocatedRunOnOtherSlotsAlone; (4) the #36 fencing verdict re-read under the bridge lock pinned by TestStaleFencingVerdictDoesNotCloseARecreatedNodesLeases. The investigator concludes no new class-4/5/6 defect was found: signals are read only as fencing assertions with no clock, identity comparisons use the finest key (run key + node#ordinal slot for conflicts, run key + group for spares), coarsening to machine granularity is always guarded by a same-run fail-closed funding scan, guard-before-predicate ordering sets `handled` in every pass, and every swallow site uses a typed sentinel via errors.Is with no error-text coupling in production control flow. The findings array is empty and no new defects are reported.

answers.question: 1. Does the change read any Kubernetes condition, taint, timestamp, or object-absence and treat it as a fact about what is PHYSICALLY running on a machine? For each: if that inference is wrong, WHAT RUNS TWICE?
answers.conclusion: The only physical-reality inference the change makes is nodeFailed()/fenced() and it is deliberately narrowed to a FENCING assertion: the Node object is absent (IsNotFound => fenced) OR carries node.kubernetes.io/out-of-service. It reads NEITHER conditions, NotReady, cordon (Unschedulable), NOR any timestamp — both triggers force Pod GC to grade-0 delete, so the rank is provably stopped before a swap starts a second copy. NotReady/cordon explicitly return not-fenced (a cordoned pod keeps running; NotReady means the control plane cannot hear the kubelet). So nothing runs twice: the swap that would create two live training ranks only fires on a real fence, and the previously-racy verdict is re-taken under the bridge lock so a node recreated while the reconcile waited on the mutex is not treated as failed. Mutation-confirmed: removing the inside-lock re-read reds TestStaleFencingVerdictDoesNotCloseARecreatedNodesLeases. No new signal-as-reality defect.

answers.question: 2. For each such signal: WHO can write that field, and can they lie? Check the shipped RBAC in deploy/helm/gpu-fleet/templates/rbac.yaml.
answers.conclusion: nodeFailed takes NO clock/timestamp, which removes the class's classic lie (a compromised kubelet backdating LastTransitionTime to manufacture a failure) — there is no time field to forge. The two inputs are node deletion and the out-of-service taint, applied by a human/fencing agent or the cloud-controller-manager, and both cause force-deletion so the assertion is self-validating. The shipped RBAC (rbac.yaml) grants the controller only nodes:[get,list,watch] and NO node delete/patch/update, so jobtree itself cannot manufacture a fence; it can only observe one. The controller's own reads use the UNCACHED APIReader (Bridge doc), so it reads etcd truth, not a possibly-stale informer. This is the shipped R21/#36 design, not new, and the investigator found no new trust hole introduced by the branch.

answers.question: 3. Does the change compare a COARSER key than the one that matters? Find every equality test on an identity and name the finest key that should have been used.
answers.conclusion: Every conflict/equality test uses the finest key that matters. Cross-run swap conflict is decided at node#ordinal SLOT granularity via leaseOccupiesSlots(other, buildSlotSet(spare.Nodes)) — NOT machine — so run B sharing a machine on different GPUs is left alone (mutation to node granularity reds TestSwapLeavesACoLocatedRunOnOtherSlotsAlone). Run identity everywhere is keys.NamespacedKey(namespace,name), never bare name. Spare<->active pairing uses run key + group index (findSpareLease filters runKey then leaseGroupIndex), and group index is only ever compared within a run, never across runs. The ONE deliberate coarsening — reclaimSquatter/removeRunPodsOnNodes evict at MACHINE granularity via nodesOfSlots, because a PodManifest names a node not a slot — is fully guarded: openRunLeasesOnNodes returns ALL the victim run's open leases on those machines and the fail-closed loop declines the swap if ANY is not Unfunded, so no funded co-located lease of the same run is silently evicted (mutation to fail-open reds TestSwapDeclinesWhenTheSquattersFundedWorkSharesTheMachine). No coarser-than-needed comparison found.

answers.question: 4. Does the change contain a loop with BOTH a type/role filter (if ... continue) AND a match predicate? Check their ORDER.
answers.conclusion: Yes, and the ordering is correct in every case — this is exactly the R25 shape and it has been fixed. HandleNodeFailure is now split into pass 1 (role==Spare, then node match, closes the spare, sets handled=true) and pass 2 (role!=Spare, then node match, sets handled=true), so a node holding ONLY a spare is handled in pass 1 and no longer falls through to a false 'no lease found'. handled is set on every path that touched a lease, so the only route to ErrNoLeaseOnNode is a genuinely lease-free node. The inner pass-2 conflict loop's index-skip guard (j==spareIdx||j==i) precedes the occupancy predicate but only excludes the two leases already being processed (the swap target and the failed active), never a same-run stale lease that must be closed (that is handled by the otherKey==runKey branch). Mutation disabling pass 1 reds TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode and TestSwapNeverTargetsASpareOnTheFailedNode. No new guard-before-predicate defect.

answers.question: 5. Grep the changed code and its callers for strings.Contains(err.Error(), ...) or any error compared by TEXT.
answers.conclusion: No production control-flow decision in the changed code compares an error by text. The R25 swallow that used to string-match 'no active lease found' is now errors.Is(err, controllers.ErrNoLeaseOnNode) against a typed sentinel (var ErrNoLeaseOnNode = errors.New(...), returned via fmt.Errorf("%w: ...")). The new plugin path adds another typed sentinel, ErrNoPlacementGroup, wrapped with %w and turned into a fail-closed PreBind Error (never swallowed). The only strings.Contains(err.Error(),...) hits are in _test.go files (webhook_test, binder_regression_test, reservation_semantics_test) asserting messages, not swallowing; the only production .Error() uses set run.Status.Message / a status string for surfacing, not branching. So every swallow site swallows exactly one typed sentinel. Clean.

result.summary: I reviewed the whole branch (git diff main...HEAD, 47 files) through the class-8 lens: do the tests assert the bug? I read every new and modified test (two_plane, settle, settle_bridge, order_independence, resolver_settlement, group_identity, quiescence, node_recreated, ledger_mirror, invariant, mint_group, admission, soleclose) plus the binding domain docs (quota-semantics.md, leases.md) and the invariant oracle. Because the engine is pure I did not speculate: I copied the working tree into a scratch dir (never editing the repo) and ran SIX mutations, each reverting a load-bearing line the change adds. All six went red: (A) dropping reclaimSquatter's removeRunPodsOnNodes fails 4 two-plane/resolver tests on explicit pod assertions; (B) dropping releaseRun's removeAllRunPods PANICS on the new INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS; (C) reverting reclaimSquatter's phases.apply to the pre-#50 direct phase write makes TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder show filler=Failed vs Pending across orderings; (D) making SettleLeases skip pod-dropping fails the settle tests on both planes; (E) removing reclaimSquatter's fail-closed guard evicts FUNDED work and fails TestSwapDeclinesWhenTheSquattersFundedWorkSharesTheMachine; (F) removing applyResolution's terminal releaseRun leaks the out-of-scope spare. I also proved the one suspicious fixture change is CORRECT, not a narrowing-to-hide-a-bug: restoring TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode's TotalGPUs from 2 back to 4 makes the oracle PANIC on INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED (2 of 4 min GPUs), so the old fixture encoded an illegal state and the R25 spare-only behaviour is fully preserved by the legal 2-GPU version. I confirmed the quiescence generator is non-vacuous (40 short seeds: 174 mints, 79 node-failures handled, 107 node-deletes, green under the oracle in panic mode), which is strong evidence the two new, unreviewed invariants are not reapers within the modelled universe. mirrorPods is legitimate (it models the plugin's per-pod mint and, per mutation B, makes the node-failure tests STRONGER, not decorative). Every stimulus is legitimate: node failure is provoked by a DELETED Node (apierrors NotFound / absent object / removal from state.Nodes), never by a cordon — the R21 trap is avoided. Net: no test asserts the bug and no fixture asserts an illegal end-state. The single observation worth surfacing is a defense-in-depth gap: the isolated unit tests that call c.applyResolution directly bypass the invariant oracle, because applyResolution is not one of the three entry points (Reconcile/ActivateReservations/HandleNodeFailure) that install the deferred check — so those tests rely solely on their own assertions (which are load-bearing, per mutation F). The production path and the quiescence integration cover applyResolution via ActivateReservations, so this is low severity.

answers.question: For each test the change adds or modifies: what is the STIMULUS? How does it provoke the behaviour under test? Is that stimulus legitimate?
answers.conclusion: Every stimulus is legitimate and correctly interpreted. Node-failure tests provoke via a genuinely fenced node: node_recreated_test uses apierrors.NewNotFound (deleted) / an absent Node object; the pure engine tests call HandleNodeFailure(name) after the node is gone; the quiescence driver's deleteNode removes the Node from state.Nodes and explicitly notes 'A cordon is NOT a failure and cannot be modelled here.' This is exactly the R21-correct signal — deletion/out-of-service, never cordon or NotReady. Reclaim tests provoke a squatter by minting an Unfunded lease on the spare's exact node#ordinal slots (funding derived, not stored — matches quota-semantics Decision 3). Sweep tests provoke by setting a run terminal or deleting its Run object (real races cleanupDeletedRun handles). R28b tests provoke via emitIntentPods/mintGroupIndex, the real committer path. Budget tightening in quiescence models an admin shrinking an envelope, the exact way Owned→Unfunded happens with nothing else moving. No R21-style invalid-stimulus (cordon-as-failure) test exists.

answers.question: Read each new assertion as a claim about the WORLD against docs/project/quota-semantics.md and docs/concepts/leases.md. Is the asserted state actually DESIRABLE?
answers.conclusion: The asserted end-states are desirable per the binding docs. TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanes asserts filler→Pending (demote-not-kill, quota-semantics R14: unfunded work is 'the first cut... reclaimed only on demand' and requeues, not killed). TestReclaimingASquatterNeverLeavesARunningRunWithNoContainers asserts a malleable run above its MinTotalGPUs stays Running (R14 exhaustion-demotes/keeps-running). TestSwapDeclines... asserts a FUNDED sibling is never evicted by a swap ('choosing between funded runs belongs to pkg/resolver') and the uncovered covered-run Fails — matching leases.md's SwapDeclined and playbook class 9's legal-exception rules. The closure reasons asserted (NodeFailure, Swap, SwapDeclined, ReclaimedBySpare, RunFailed, SweptTerminalRun/SweptOrphanRun) all match leases.md section 1a. Crucially, TestSwapDeclinesRatherThanEvictAFundedRunOnTheSpareSlots INVERTS the canonical class-8 specimen: it asserts the declined spare must be RELEASED, quoting that an earlier version asserted the opposite ('the spare must not be consumed') — the corruption is now the negative control, not the spec.

answers.question: MUTATE THE FIX. Delete the load-bearing line the change adds in a scratch copy, and re-run the tests that supposedly cover it. Report exact commands and output.
answers.conclusion: Six mutations in a scratch tree (cp of the working tree; the repo was never edited), each reverting one load-bearing line and running the covering tests. ALL went red. (A) removed c.removeRunPodsOnNodes(victimKey, nodes) in reclaimSquatter → 4 fails (two_plane_test.go:126/:180, resolver_settlement_test.go:345/:397). (B) removed c.removeAllRunPods(run) in releaseRun → PANIC INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS at HandleNodeFailure. (C) replaced reclaimSquatter's phases.apply(victim,victimKey,RunPhasePending,msg) with a direct victim.Status.Phase=RunPhasePending → TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder failed showing filler=Failed for one ordering and Pending for another (the task-#50 defect). (D) neutered SettleLeases' pod drop → settle_test.go:52/:78 failed ('the container outlived the lease'). (E) removed reclaimSquatter's fail-closed 'if !classified || class != funding.ClassUnfunded { return false }' → two_plane_test.go:233 failed ('the swap evicted FUNDED work... 0 pods survive'). (F) removed applyResolution's terminal c.releaseRun(run,"RunFailed",now) → resolver_settlement_test.go:111 failed ('the spare of a run the resolver ENDED is still open'). No test passed against a reverted fix, so none is decorative on the lines tested.

answers.question: Does any test assert on a state that the invariant oracle (pkg/invariant) would reject as illegal?
answers.conclusion: No test asserts an illegal end-state. The full controllers package passes with -count=1 while the oracle is in PANIC mode and installed on Reconcile/ActivateReservations/HandleNodeFailure, so any illegal state at those entry-point returns would have panicked. The pkg/invariant unit tests deliberately build illegal Worlds, but only to confirm CheckSteady/CheckTransition CATCH them (e.g. TestTerminalRunKeepingItsPodsIsAViolation). settle_test builds terminal-run-with-open-lease fixtures and calls SettleLeases directly (not an entry point) — but the assertion is that SettleLeases REPAIRS them, and the post-sweep state is legal. The one caveat: tests that call c.applyResolution directly (resolver_settlement, order_independence's map test) bypass the oracle because applyResolution is not an installed entry point — see the finding. Separately, I proved a MODIFIED fixture WOULD have been illegal before the change: restoring TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode to TotalGPUs=4 panics on INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED ('holding 2 of 4 minimum runnable GPUs').

answers.question: Did the change MODIFY or DELETE an existing test? For each: was the old test wrong, or is the new test reshaped to accommodate a defect?
answers.conclusion: The modifications are corrections, not corruptions. node_failure_test.go: TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode changed Runs from nfRun(...,4,...) to nfRun(...,2,...) and TestNodeFailureReturnsTypedSentinel... from 4 to 1; I proved the OLD width was an illegal state (INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED panics: a 4-GPU run holding a 2-GPU active + 2-GPU spare reports Running below its minimum), and the R25 spare-only-node-closes behaviour is unchanged — so no legitimate coverage was dropped (the old scenario was not a real state). mirrorPods was inserted into 12 lease-only fixtures across gang_width_test/run_controller_test/node_failure_test; mutation B proved this makes the node-failure tests STRONGER (they now catch the terminal-pod bug via INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS), and mirrorPods models the sole committer (one pod per open lease, activePods==openActiveLeases so it does NOT trip the AwaitingMint width bypass). The R28b label additions (completion/cutover/eta/gang_width/scenario tests) and the closeLease→CloseLease rename (node_failure_test.go:390) conform to the sole-closer refactor. The two golden JSONs changed only group ('0'→'1', the R28b correctness fix) and nodeName; both were regenerated via UPDATE_GOLDEN from commit e681a96 (R28b), which stopped flattening the packer's groups — a re-derivation after an intended behaviour change, on the placement (not lease/funding) plane.

findings.title: applyResolution unit tests are not backstopped by the invariant oracle
findings.failure_scenario: In mutation F I removed applyResolution's terminal c.releaseRun(run,"RunFailed",now); the resulting state (a Failed run holding an open out-of-scope spare) is exactly INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT, yet TestResolverEndingARunReleasesTheLeasesItStillHolds did NOT panic inside the call — it was caught only by its own line-111 assertion. If a future edit both reintroduces an illegal-state bug in applyResolution AND weakens one of these isolated assertions, the unit test would stay green; only the ActivateReservations entry point and the quiescence integration (both of which DO run the oracle) would catch it. This is a partial hole in the 'green means asserted-and-legal' guarantee for precisely the resolver/Scope path where class-1's 'third door' lived. Mitigation: add a deferred checkInvariants to applyResolution, or drive these tests through ActivateReservations. Not a code defect — the shipped code is correct and the assertions are load-bearing — but a review-worthy gap in the safety net.

result.summary: This is a formatted mapping of an investigator's adversarial review of branch fix/r27-invariant-oracle (f243eb8..HEAD, 47 files) under a half-plane/reaper lens, examining lease-closure and pod-deletion pairing across controllers/run_controller.go, settle.go, invariants.go, pkg/invariant, kube/bridge.go and kube/reconcilers.go. The investigator read the playbook, AGENTS.md and history-run-phase-writers.md, enumerated every CloseLease site and every state.Pods mutation paired plane-by-plane, and compiled/ran three reproductions in a scratchpad module against the real tree (never editing the repo). Five findings were reported: F1 (high) — external pod loss leaves an open lease with no closer anywhere, confirmed by a 20-reconcile/20-hour repro with zero self-heal; F2 (medium) — a same-run stale-lease branch closes a lease while leaving the active pod on the node a swap pod hard-targets; F3 (medium) — the new INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS invariant fires on the routine graceful-termination window because bridge load() does not filter DeletionTimestamp; F4 (medium) — the checkpoint-grace declined-swap path strands a spare holder pod, blocking future spare re-provisioning; F5 (low) — a mint-after-terminal race is swept and reported as 'Shirked' though nothing shirked a duty. The investigator also mutation-tested two of the branch's defenses (releaseRun's pod cull, reclaimSquatter's fail-closed funding guard), confirming both are load-bearing, and adjudicated eight scout leads, refuting six and confirming two (the two mutation tests, and a bounded single-pod-per-multi-node-lease test gap with no defect found behind it). The checkpoint-grace window itself was found NOT reached by the new sweep, with exemption keyed safely on positive terminal-phase enumeration rather than a phase name.

answers.question: For every site the change closes a Lease: whose container is still running? For every site it deletes a Pod: whose lease is still open? Name each pairing. Bridge.apply deletes exactly the pods absent from state.Pods, which is why forgetting is silent.
answers.conclusion: Closure sites and their pod half: settle.go:172 (sweep) — pods of the same doomed run dropped in the same function, same bridge pass, PAIRED. run_controller.go:1202 (pass-1 spare on failed node) and 1428 (dead group's active) — the container is on a FENCED node (deleted or out-of-service), provably stopped and force-deleted by Pod GC; paired by fencing semantics. 1261 (run==nil) — pods swept by cleanupDeletedRun and, since this branch, by SettleLeases' orphan-run rule in the same WithWorld pass, PAIRED. 1297 (same-run 'stale' lease on the spare's slots) — UNPAIRED: no pod removal exists on this branch (removeSparePodOnNodes filters RoleSpare only); compiled repro shows the stale lease closed, its active pod alive on node-b, and a swap pod hard-targeted at the same node (finding F2). 1346/1347 (swap) — spare pod removed via removeSparePodOnNodes, failed active's pod on the fenced node, PAIRED. 1437 (SwapDeclined spare): Failed branch paired via the post-loop releaseRun; checkpoint-GRACE branch UNPAIRED — the spare holder pod survives its closed lease indefinitely (finding F4). 1540 (reclaimSquatter) — removeRunPodsOnNodes over the same node set, guarded fail-closed; mutation-verified PAIRED. 1664 (releaseRun) — removeAllRunPods inside the same function; mutation-verified PAIRED. 1795 (applyResolution actions) — pods dropped via closedGroups keyed run::group; paired given INV-GROUP-STAMPED (an unstamped legacy lease would yield GroupIndex "" and skip the pod drop, but the clean-break upgrade policy excludes live unstamped leases). 2740/2743 (shrink) — removePodsForGroups(removed), PAIRED. reconcilers.go:105 (cleanupDeletedRun) — drops the run's pods below, PAIRED. Pod-deletion sites in the reverse direction all close their leases first or in the same call (removeSparePodOnNodes/removeRunPodsOnNodes/removeAllRunPods/removePodsForGroups/SettleLeases). Pod-creates-before-lease (emitSwapPod, emitCohortPods, emitSparePods) are the legal mint window and the oracle's AwaitingMint gate models it. The one pairing NOBODY owns: a pod deleted by the WORLD (drain, eviction, kubectl delete) leaves its lease open with no closer anywhere — Reconcile's Running branch returns without looking at pods, the pod watch DeleteFunc returns false, and SettleLeases skips non-terminal existing runs (finding F1, compiled repro: open after 20 simulated hours, swept=0).

answers.question: The checkpoint-grace window is a DELIBERATE, bounded half-plane state: the run parks Pending with a deadline and its containers keep running so they can write a checkpoint. Does the change reach it? Any sweep or cull that does is a reaper. Check the exemption keys on the deadline or on non-terminality, not on a phase name that could change.
answers.conclusion: The change does not reach the grace window, and the keying is in the safe direction. SettleLeases builds its doomed set POSITIVELY from run.Status.Phase == RunPhaseFailed || RunPhaseComplete (settle.go:131) plus Run-object absence — it enumerates the states it destroys rather than exempting the ones it spares, so a grace run (Pending + CheckpointDeadline) is untouched whatever the grace phase is named, and a hypothetical new terminal phase would be MISSED (fail-safe: sweeps nothing) rather than reaped. TestSweepLeavesARunInCheckpointGraceAlone pins it. Both new invariants gate on the same positive terminal pair (invariant projection: Terminal := phase==Failed||Complete), and INV-LEASE-HAS-POD cannot fire during grace because the grace containers ARE the pods (fires only on Pods==0). Grace termination is deadline-keyed, not phase-keyed: Reconcile line 167 tests run.Status.CheckpointDeadline against the clock before failRun→releaseRun culls the containers. The quiescence driver deliberately walks through grace (a third of generated runs carry spec.runtime.checkpoint). One adjacent defect: the grace branch of failGroupWithoutSpare itself creates an UNMANAGED half-plane remnant — it closes the spare's lease (SwapDeclined) and removes no spare pod; the Failed branch's releaseRun would cull it, but the grace branch culls nothing, and on re-admission emitSparePods counts the stale holder pod as an existing spare (run_controller.go:2062-2069) so the spare is never re-minted (finding F4, pre-existing, half-fixed by this branch).

answers.question: Take each fix in this change. State, concretely, the legal state its repair would destroy if applied one step more aggressively. If you cannot name one, say so — but look first at pkg/invariant's four rejected invariants.
answers.conclusion: Per fix: (1) SettleLeases — one step further is Fable's rule 2 ('open Spare with no open Active of its group is an orphan'), which reaps healthy standby during every ordinary admission because the plugin mints per pod and a spare legally precedes its actives; kept refuted-executable in TestSweepLeavesASpareOnlyRunAlone. Keying doomed on 'not Running' instead of the terminal pair would reap the checkpoint-grace run. (2) releaseRun (F4/F5/F7) — calling it on the nearest non-terminal state, the grace-parked Pending run, deletes the containers mid-checkpoint-write; that is exactly why its precondition is terminality, currently enforced only by the doc comment ('CALL IT ONLY ON A TERMINAL RUN'), and an in-function terminality assert is blocked by completeRun calling releaseRun BEFORE setting Phase=Complete — reorder completeRun first if hardening. (3) reclaimSquatter — closing all the victim's leases or its whole pod set evicts the victim's Owned lease, because class is per-LEASE; mutation removing the fail-closed guard turned TestSwapDeclinesWhenTheSquattersFundedWorkSharesTheMachine red ('the swap evicted FUNDED work'). Killing (Failed) instead of demoting (Pending) destroys legally reclaimable work (R14). (4) the ev-taken-once choice (#54) — 're-fixing' it by re-evaluating per iteration reintroduces class-3 order dependence, and a judge's mutation showed sparing the artifact-funded squatter fails a genuinely funded victim instead. (5) #36 refix — one step further is treating NotReady/cordon as the verdict, which is R21: two live copies of one rank. (6) R28b no-default leaseGroupIndex — one step further is closing or refusing unstamped leases at load, which reaps every pre-upgrade lease; the shipped shape only refuses to invent '0'. (7) The oracle itself — the four documented rejected invariants each reap a named legal state (tolerated slot oversubscription; cordoned-node leases = R21 rebuilt; spare-before-active mint; the swap window's zero-open-active Running run), and WidthAssembled measured on base-gang width was a shipped reaper this branch itself had to fix (R27g/h): the resolver legally cuts a malleable run's base group while grow ranks cover the minimum.

answers.question: Does the change add or strengthen an INVARIANT? If so, find a legal state this engine really produces that would trip it. An invariant that is wrong is not a weaker safety net; it is a reaper that panics CI on healthy code and argues for a fix that rebuilds a shipped corruption.
answers.conclusion: Yes — INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS, INV-LEASE-HAS-POD, and the sweep's Shirked-panics-under-test rail. All three are trippable by states a real cluster routinely produces. (a) INV-LEASE-HAS-POD: any external pod deletion (node drain, eviction, kubectl delete) of a Running run's pod. Compiled repro: the lease stays open forever (no engine handler; the pod watch's DeleteFunc returns false; the Running branch of Reconcile returns without reading pods; SettleLeases sweeps 0), and the invariant fires on EVERY subsequent reconcile. In production that is a permanently-nonzero jobtree_invariant_violations_total for a state the engine cannot repair; under test it panics any future test that models eviction — and the quiescence generator has NO pod-delete event ('Nothing in this file … deletes a pod'), so 800 green seeds never challenge it. The violation is arguably a true detection of a real pre-existing gap (the R9 9A-3 failure edge), so the right fix is an engine path that closes leases for externally-lost workload, not deleting the invariant — but as shipped the invariant asserts a property nothing maintains. (b) INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS: the graceful-termination window. Bridge.apply deletes pods with default grace; load() lists pods with no DeletionTimestamp filter; for up to terminationGracePeriodSeconds after every failure-path releaseRun (forever in envtest, which has no kubelet), the world contains a terminal run with a Terminating pod. Compiled repro: reconciling a DIFFERENT healthy run panics on it. Production: violation counter increments after every failed run; envtest: a latent panic on healthy code the first time someone writes the failure-path envtest. (c) Shirked: a lease the plugin mints at PreBind after releaseRun's snapshot was loaded lands on an already-terminal run; the next pass sweeps it as 'terminal-run' and, under test, panics with 'some path shirked a duty' — the identical two-process race shape the orphan-run rule was explicitly excused for, but not this rule.

answers.question: Assume every finding you raised is real. For each: which budget is charged, how many GPUs are held, and for how long? Quantify it.
answers.conclusion: F1 (external pod loss, lease immortal): the lease's PaidByBudget/PaidByEnvelope is charged for len(Slice.Nodes) GPUs — 1 GPU in the repro, all 8 of an 8-GPU node in a routine drain — for UNBOUNDED time: 20 simulated hours confirmed no self-heal, and the only exits are run completion (impossible: runGangComplete needs the pods that are gone), run deletion by a human, or node fencing. The owner's envelope concurrency is consumed, blocking their future admissions, and the ledger marks the slots occupied, shrinking the schedulable cluster. Post-branch it is at least VISIBLE (violations counter) but never healed. F2 (same-run ledger-only eviction): the budget is UNDER-charged — billing stops while the container computes free — and 2 GPUs (repro) are physically held on the node the swap pod is hard-pinned to; if they exhaust the node the swap pod never binds and the gang wedges: a 96-GPU run then charges ~94 GPUs' worth of open leases for zero progress until a human intervenes. F3 (TerminalNoPods on Terminating pods): zero GPUs and zero dollars; the cost is alarm integrity — jobtree_invariant_violations_total, positioned as evidence of illegal state, becomes routinely nonzero for ≥30s after every failed run (minutes for checkpoint-on-SIGTERM grace periods), plus a latent envtest panic. F4 (stranded spare holder pod in grace): groupGPUs GPUs (typically 2) held UNBILLED (lease closed) for the run's remaining lifetime — days to weeks for training jobs — and the run silently loses the swap coverage it paid for, so the next node failure parks or kills a run that owned a spare. F5 (mint-after-terminal race swept as Shirked): seconds-to-minutes of one lease's charge, self-healed by the sweep; the cost is jobtree_swept_leases_total{rule="terminal-run"} — documented alert-on-any-nonzero — firing on a benign race, and a flaky panic in any test running the plugin concurrently.

findings.title: External pod loss (drain/eviction) leaves an open lease with no closer anywhere, forever
findings.failure_scenario: A Running run's pod is deleted by the world (drain/eviction/kubectl) leaving an open lease with no closer anywhere: Reconcile's Running branch returns without reading pods, the pod watch DeleteFunc returns false, SettleLeases skips non-terminal existing runs, and INV-LEASE-HAS-POD fires on every reconcile forever. Compiled repro (scratchpad module): 20 reconciles over 20 simulated hours — lease closed=false, phase=Running, INV-LEASE-HAS-POD panic on every call, SettleLeases swept=0.
findings.taxonomyClass: 1 immortal-lease / 9 half-plane (plus a new-invariant false-fire); pre-existing gap, materially interacted-with by this branch: the sweep whose stated purpose is closing immortal leases by derivation does not cover this door, and the new INV-LEASE-HAS-POD alarms on it forever without healing; the generator omits the one event that reaches it

findings.title: Same-run stale-lease branch closes lease but leaves active pod on the exact node a swap pod hard-targets
findings.failure_scenario: Pass 2's same-run 'stale lease on the spare's slots' branch closes the lease ReclaimedBySpare and removes no pod (removeSparePodOnNodes filters RoleSpare only), then emitSwapPod hard-targets the same node — a ledger-only eviction of exactly the shape the branch's cross-run fix exists to prevent. Compiled repro: stale lease closed=true reason=ReclaimedBySpare, its active pod still present on node-b, 1 swap pod emitted with required affinity to node-b.
findings.taxonomyClass: 9 half-plane; pre-existing (branch only renamed closeLease→CloseLease here), but it is the un-fixed sibling of the branch's own reclaimSquatter two-plane fix, one branch above it

findings.title: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS fires on the routine graceful-termination window because load() does not filter DeletionTimestamp
findings.failure_scenario: load() does not filter pods with a DeletionTimestamp, so for the graceful-termination window after every failure-path releaseRun (forever in envtest, which has no kubelet) the world contains a terminal run with a Terminating pod; INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS then fires from any engine entry point. Compiled repro: reconciling a different, healthy run panics INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS on the dead run's lingering pod — production alert noise on routine failures, latent panic for any future failure-path envtest.
findings.taxonomyClass: reaper-invariant (new class candidate: invariant blind to API deletion latency); new in this branch (INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS, 4e832a7/7a681c0)

findings.title: Declined-swap checkpoint-grace path strands the spare holder pod, blocking future re-provisioning
findings.failure_scenario: On the declined-swap checkpoint-grace path the spare's lease is closed (SwapDeclined) but the spare HOLDER pod (sleep-forever, GPU-holding) is never removed; on re-admission emitSparePods counts it as an existing spare (lines 2062-2069) and a bound pod is never re-scheduled, so no spare lease is ever minted again — one GPU-group held unbilled for the run's lifetime plus silently lost swap coverage. TestDecliningTheSwapReleasesTheSpareEvenWhenTheRunParksInCheckpointGrace asserts only the lease plane for the spare.
findings.taxonomyClass: 9 half-plane; pre-existing; the branch's F4/F5/F7 fix paired the Failed branch (releaseRun culls the spare pod) and left the grace branch unpaired

findings.title: Mint-after-terminal race is swept and reported as 'Shirked' though nothing shirked a duty
findings.failure_scenario: A lease the plugin mints at PreBind after releaseRun's world snapshot was loaded lands on an already-terminal run and is swept next pass as 'terminal-run' — Shirked panics under test and trips the alert-on-nonzero terminal-run metric, though no code path shirked anything; the identical two-process race shape was explicitly excused for the orphan-run rule and not for this one. Not reproduced (spans two processes).
findings.taxonomyClass: signal-not-reality (a race read as a shirked duty); new in this branch (c74e0ef reportSweep asymmetry)
