result.summary: Investigator reviewed controllers/settle.go (SettleLeases) and its wiring through Bridge.WithWorld/reportSweep, tracing Bridge.load, the NodeReconciler fencing verdict, cleanupDeletedRun, releaseRun/completeRun, the RunReference type, the plugin's PreBind mint (admission.PodLeaseWithRole), and the invariant oracle. A scratch module was built with a replace directive against /workspaces/jobtree, exercising both the pure engine (controllers.SettleLeases over a ClusterState) and the real controllers/kube.Bridge over a controller-runtime fake client. The core sweep logic is sound against sole-committer states and against the cross-process plugin race: because a Run causally precedes its pods and its leases by many reconcile cycles, because each APIReader List is individually consistent, because terminal is a stable phase, and because a lease-create event always re-triggers a WithWorld pass whose sweep re-closes it, the investigator could not construct a state where the sweep destroys a live, sole-committer-funded run's work or leaves an immortal lease via the plugin race — it heals those races rather than causing them. One concrete way it destroys something it should not was confirmed: an empty-valued run-name label (which client.HasLabels admits into the world) collapses to the doomed key "namespace/" and makes the sweep silently delete that pod through the API and close any empty-RunRef lease, accusing nobody. Its blast radius is confined to malformed, non-sole-committer objects. The terminal-run panic path was also confirmed to be effectively redundant with the engine's deferred oracle (which fires first on the identical predicate), so the envtest-flake fear is unfounded, and a pre-existing name-only-RunRef immortal-lease that the orphan rule advertises but structurally cannot cover was flagged.

answers.question: 1. SettleLeases derives `doomed` from state.Runs (terminal) and from leases+pods whose run key is ABSENT from state.Runs. Bridge.load lists Runs with APIReader (uncached). Enumerate every way state.Runs can be missing a Run that actually exists...
answers.conclusion: Traced Bridge.load (bridge.go:168-262). It issues six SEPARATE uncached APIReader.List calls (Runs first at :170, Leases at :178, Pods at :190) with NO namespace/field selector, so they are not one transaction. List errors are NOT swallowed: every List returns fmt.Errorf(...) up through WithWorld, aborting the pass before any sweep. Pagination is not a gap: the Lists set no Limit, so the apiserver returns the full collection with no continue token. The direct APIReader is cluster-scoped (it bypasses the cache), so no manager namespace filter applies; a namespace/RBAC restriction would surface as a 403 error (returned), not a silent subset. The one theoretical miss — a Run listed-before-it-existed while its lease/pod is listed-after — is causally unreachable: a lease exists only after the bridge created the pod and the plugin minted at PreBind, which is seconds-to-minutes after the Run was created and reconciled, so any Run whose lease is visible was created well before List Runs. Conclusion: the orphan rule fires only for GENUINELY deleted Runs (correct cleanup); it cannot orphan a live Run.

answers.question: 2. The plugin mints a Lease at PreBind, concurrently with the controller process. Construct the interleaving... Is that correct, or does it kill a pod the plugin has just funded? Now the reverse... What closes THAT lease? Is it immortal?
answers.conclusion: Forward case: Run R goes Failed while the plugin binds a straggler pod P and mints lease L for it after the failRun pass loaded its snapshot; the next WithWorld loads R=Failed + open L + P, and the terminal-run sweep closes L and deletes P. This is CORRECT — R is genuinely dead (gang co-terminates), so L would otherwise be an immortal lease and P a zombie container; settle heals it. It is flagged Shirked (a terminal-run closure) which panics under test but only logs+heals in production. Reverse case is NOT immortal: after the sweep closes L1/drops P, if the plugin mints L2 for P, the L2 create fires leaseToRun -> RunReconciler -> WithWorld, and because R stays terminal (or absent), that pass's sweep closes L2; every subsequent mint re-triggers the same closing pass, and once P is deleted the scheduler stops binding and the plugin stops minting, so it converges. Settle is the mechanism that PREVENTS the immortal lease here, not one that creates it.

answers.question: 3. reportSweep calls invariant.Report — which PANICS under `go test` — for rule=terminal-run... Note that Reconcile's own deferred oracle would have panicked first — would it? Prove the two checks cannot disagree.
answers.conclusion: In WithWorld the deferred oracle runs at fn's return (bridge.go:113) BEFORE SettleLeases (bridge.go:118); nothing mutates state between them, so both read the identical post-fn world. invariant.CheckSteady's TerminalPresent (invariant.go:250) fires iff a terminal run holds an open lease — the exact predicate settle's Shirked uses. So in any pass whose fn is an engine entry point (Reconcile/ActivateReservations/HandleNodeFailure all `defer c.checkInvariants`), the oracle panics first and settle's Report is unreachable; the two cannot disagree. Settle's Report is reachable only in a NON-engine WithWorld pass (the cleanupDeletedRun branch, or a bare test fn). In envtest there is no scheduler plugin minting leases onto a terminal run, so a terminal-run-with-open-lease can only arise from a test that hand-builds that illegal state — which the oracle exists to catch — not from a legitimate reconcile sequence. So it is a genuine finding when it fires, not a flake.

answers.question: 4. Sweep.Pods drops pods by `keys.NamespacedKey(pod.Namespace, pod.Labels[LabelRunName])`. What happens to a pod whose run-name label is empty or missing?... does HasLabels match a label present but EMPTY?
answers.conclusion: CONFIRMED by execution. A pod with the label MISSING is filtered out at load by client.HasLabels{LabelRunName} (bridge.go:190) and never enters state.Pods — safe. But HasLabels compiles to an EXISTS requirement, which matches a key whose value is EMPTY: a Bridge-over-fake-client repro proved the stray pod `stray-empty-label` (label value "") was loaded, then DELETED through the API. Its key is keys.NamespacedKey(ns, "") = "default/", which is never a real Run key, so doomed["default/"]=orphan-run and both the empty-label pod (settle.go:180) and any lease with empty RunRef.Name (settle.go:143) are reaped, with shirked=0 (accuses nobody). A healthy Running run in the same namespace was untouched, so blast radius is confined to empty-run-name objects, which the sole committer never emits — a robustness gap, not a sole-committer data-loss.

answers.question: 5. The sweep runs on EVERY WithWorld... If fn returns an error mid-admission, the engine's half-applied state is applied AND then swept. Can a half-applied admission look terminal-or-orphan to the sweep? Build it.
answers.conclusion: No. The sweep acts only on runs that are terminal (settle.go:131) or whose Run object is absent. A half-applied admission (Reconcile/ActivateReservations erroring mid-way) leaves the run Pending/Running/Reserved and PRESENT in state.Runs — never terminal and never absent — so the sweep ignores it; its newly-created-in-state leases belong to a present, non-terminal run. The only terminal transitions inside those entry points are completeRun and failRun, and both close leases + drop pods (via releaseRun) BEFORE any subsequent error return, so if the pass errors after them the run is terminal with already-closed leases and no pods, and the sweep finds nothing. The engine does not create Run objects, so a half-applied pass cannot manufacture a new orphan key either. Could not build a half-applied admission that the sweep misreads.

answers.question: 6. cleanupDeletedRun already closes a deleted run's leases and sweeps its pods. SettleLeases' orphan-run rule now does the same thing... Do they agree on the ClosureReason, on the pods, and on the events? Which one wins when both run, and does the second one re-close?
answers.conclusion: They target the identical lease/pod set for a deleted run (leases with matching RunRef, pods with matching namespace+LabelRunName) but DISAGREE on ClosureReason: cleanupDeletedRun stamps "RunDeleted" (reconcilers.go:105) while settle stamps "SweptOrphanRun" (settle.go:closureReasonFor). This is a genuine class-7 cloned obligation, but harmless: when both run in one RunReconciler pass, fn=cleanupDeletedRun runs FIRST and closes the leases; settle then SKIPS them because every settle loop guards `if lease.Status.Closed { continue }`, so it does NOT re-close, does NOT re-stamp, and INV-CLOSED-MONOTONE is preserved. cleanupDeletedRun wins in that pass; settle wins only in a non-Run-reconciler pass (e.g. a node event) that races ahead of cleanup. The reason divergence is informational only; the two never double-close and never disagree on which pods die.

answers.question: 7. Write the test for the single most dangerous case you found. Run it. Paste the output.
answers.conclusion: Exercised the empty-run-name reaping (Q4) two ways against the real code. Pure engine: SettleLeases over a ClusterState with a live Running run plus a stray empty-label pod printed `closed leases: 1, dropped pods: 1 / closed mystery-lease runKey=default/ rule=orphan-run / surviving pods: train-active-0 / train-lease closed? false ; mystery-lease closed? true / shirked: 0`. Real Bridge over a controller-runtime fake client (same harness as settle_bridge_test.go): after one no-op WithWorld, `pods remaining after sweep: 1 (survivor: train-active-0), stray-empty-label still exists? false (err=pods "stray-empty-label" not found), healthy train-lease closed? false`. This proves HasLabels admits the empty-valued label, the sweep deletes that pod all the way to the API, and the healthy run is untouched. Scratch program at /tmp/claude-1000/-workspaces-jobtree/b3df7d06-fa76-4793-98b7-31b44b11906e/scratchpad/repro/pure_test_repro.go.

if err := b.APIReader.List(ctx, &podList, client.HasLabels{binder.LabelRunName}); err != nil {
b.reportSweep(ctx, controllers.SettleLeases(snap.state, now), snap)
if run != nil && (run.Status.Phase == RunPhaseFailed || run.Status.Phase == RunPhaseComplete) {

func cleanupDeletedRun(state *controllers.ClusterState, runKey, namespace, name string, now time.Time) {
if names := openByRun[r.Key]; len(names) > 0 {

findings.title: Empty-valued run-name label is reaped: SettleLeases deletes the pod and closes empty-RunRef leases via the doomed key "namespace/"
findings.taxonomyClass: 5 identity-coarsening (missing empty-key guard; adjacent to 6 guard-before-predicate)
findings.failure_scenario: SettleLeases builds its doomed set by keying pods and leases with keys.NamespacedKey(namespace, pod.Labels[LabelRunName]) / (RunRef.Namespace, RunRef.Name). An empty run-name collapses to "namespace/", which is never a real Run key, so every empty-run-name object is classified SweepOrphanRun and then deleted/closed with shirked=0 (accuses nobody). client.HasLabels{LabelRunName} in Bridge.load (bridge.go:190) admits a pod whose label is PRESENT but EMPTY (HasLabels is an EXISTS requirement), so such a pod reaches state.Pods and is deleted through the API on the very next pass. Confirmed by executing the real Bridge over a fake client: the stray empty-label pod was deleted while a co-located healthy Running run survived. Blast radius is confined to objects carrying jobtree's label with an empty value / empty RunRef, which the sole committer (buildPod/buildLease/PodLeaseWithRole) never emits, so this is not sole-committer data loss — but any user or buggy tool that stamps rq.davidlangworthy.io/run="" on a pod gets it silently deleted by jobtree on every reconcile. Fix: skip an empty run name in all three doomed loops and the pod-drop loop (treat 'no run name' as 'not ours', not 'orphan').

findings.title: Orphan-run rule cannot cover a same-name recreated Run: the old incarnation's open lease (name-only RunRef, no owner-ref) is mis-attributed and never closed
findings.taxonomyClass: 1 immortal-lease
findings.failure_scenario: Minted leases carry RunRef{Name,Namespace} with NO UID (RunReference has no UID field) and NO OwnerReference (PodLeaseWithRole sets only Namespace/Name/Labels), so they are not apiserver-GC'd on Run deletion. If a Run is deleted and recreated under the same name before cleanupDeletedRun runs (there is no finalizer yet — R12's is still a doc/P1), the prior incarnation's still-open lease keys to the SAME run key as the new incarnation. cleanupDeletedRun never fires (state.Runs[key] is now present), settle's orphan rule never fires (the Run object exists), and settle's terminal rule never fires (the new run is not terminal) — so the old lease charges the new run's budget and holds GPUs forever. The R2 nonce only makes the lease NAME unique to avoid an AlreadyExists collision; it does not disambiguate RunRef, which is what funding.Evaluate and the sweep key on. settle.go's orphan-rule comment claims to close leases when 'no Run object exists for the lease at all', which is precisely the case name-aliasing defeats. Not introduced by this branch, but the reviewer should know the new orphan rule has this blind spot; a real fix keys attribution on run UID.

answers.question: 1. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD says: a run holding >=1 open lease holds >=1 pod. Find a LEGAL engine state that refutes it.
answers.conclusion: REFUTED, executed. (a) Pod deleted out from under an open lease: `kubectl delete pod`, a drain (eviction API), or pod GC removes the OBJECT; kubelet node-pressure eviction does NOT (object persists as Failed, so no fire). After object removal, state.Pods is empty for the run at the return of EVERY entry point, forever: the engine's designed response is no response — the pod watch drops deletes (reconcilers.go:153), Reconcile of a Running run returns at the phase check (run_controller.go:191-201), R21 forbids closing the lease without a fencing assertion, and SettleLeases only sweeps terminal/orphan runs. Reproduced: TestLeaseHasPodFiresOnADrainedNode panics INV-LEASE-HAS-POD and keeps firing 20/20 simulated hours. (b) The swap window: NOT a refutation — removeSparePodOnNodes and emitSwapPod run in the same pass, so at return the swap pod is present (pods>=1) and the closed spare/active leases don't count; the pods>leases direction is the tolerated AwaitingMint window. (c) All pods Succeeded, leases not yet closed: objects still exist, Pods>0, no fire; and the same reconcile completes the run. BUT if pod GC collects the Succeeded objects BEFORE that reconcile, runGangComplete's sawActive stays false, the run can never complete, and the invariant fires forever on a wedged-by-design state — reproduced in TestLeaseHasPodFiresWhenPodGCCollectsASucceededGang. (d) growRun adds pods; shrinkRun closes group leases and removes the same groups' pods in one pass (run_controller.go:2739-2755) — no fire at return. (e) Adoption after restart: leases and pods reload together; empty pods requires external deletion, i.e. case (a) — in which case adoption flips Running on a containerless gang and the oracle fires on the adoption pass itself. Verdict: the invariant is a claim about the WORLD, not an engine postcondition, and the engine has no repair path; under test it makes any faithful drain/GC fixture illegal, and in production it fires every runningRunResync (5 min) forever.

answers.question: 2. INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS says: a terminal (Failed/Completed) run has zero pods. Enumerate EVERY writer of run.Status.Phase = Failed or Completed in the tree.
answers.conclusion: All terminal writers discharge both planes in-state; the refutation is between passes, not in any writer. Live-tree terminal writers (grep over non-test code): (1) completeRun run_controller.go:535 — releaseRun(:534) closes leases and removeAllRunPods drops every pod, called just BEFORE the phase write (ordering benign; nothing reads phase in between). (2) failRun :688 — releaseRun at :694; its doors are the checkpoint-grace expiry (Reconcile :168), follow cycle (:566), upstream-failed (:587), and follow-grace-expired (:597); the grace expiry therefore drops the deliberately-alive checkpoint containers correctly. (3) failGroupWithoutSpare via phases.apply(run, runKey, RunPhaseFailed, ...) :1458 — pods are dropped by HandleNodeFailure's order-independent post-loop sweep (:1368-1380) which calls releaseRun for every tracker entry that ended Failed; the tracker map covers the run even when apply() returned false. (4) applyResolution's default branch :1876 — releaseRun :1888. Nothing in controllers/kube writes run.Status.Phase (cleanupDeletedRun touches leases/pods/reservations only); the remaining 16 writers write Pending/Running/Waiting. So no writer shirks — and the invariant is STILL refuted: pod deletion is asynchronous (graceful, default 30s; long for checkpoint-on-SIGTERM training pods), the lease closures the terminal writer just made re-trigger the run reconciler via the Lease watch (reconcilers.go:177), and Bridge.load reloads the terminating pod unfiltered (bridge.go:190; PodManifest has no DeletionTimestamp field). Reproduced: TestTerminalNoPodsFiresDuringOrdinaryGracefulDeletion — pass N completes legally, pass N+1 panics INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS. Every ordinary completion and every terminal failure in a real cluster produces this window; envtest hides it only because unbound pods delete non-gracefully.

answers.question: 3. The oracle's projection lives in controllers/invariants.go:snapshotWorld(). It now computes Pods per run by counting EVERY pod of EVERY role, and KnownToLedger changed from `anyLease || anyPod` to `anyLease || podsOfRun[key] > 0`. Are those the same? Does counting SPARE pods toward Run.Pods make INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS or INV-LEASE-HAS-POD fire on a legal state that the previous projection tolerated?
answers.conclusion: The KnownToLedger change is EXACTLY the same predicate: the branch diff (7a681c0..HEAD) shows old anyPod was built by the identical loop — every pod carrying a run-name label, all roles, no phase filter — so anyPod[key]==true iff podsOfRun[key]>0. What is genuinely new is the Pods field itself; both invariants that read it were added in the same commit (4e832a7), so there is no 'previous projection' that tolerated a state these now reject. Within the new design, counting spare pods cuts both ways: for INV-LEASE-HAS-POD it can only SATISFY the check, never trigger it — which MASKS the exact defect class the invariant was written for: a victim whose whole ACTIVE pod set is deleted passes as long as one spare pod survives anywhere (executed: TestASurvivingSparePodMasksAWholeActivePodSetDeletion, no panic on an open active lease with zero active pods). For INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS, spare pods widen the trigger, but the only legal state found where that fires is the same async-deletion window that any role's pod produces; engine paths (releaseRun/removeAllRunPods) drop spares with everything else. One footnote-grade in-state half-plane: HandleNodeFailure pass 1 (run_controller.go:1194-1212) closes a spare lease on the failed node without removing its spare pod from state.Pods — no invariant fires (no open lease remains) and reality Pod-GCs the fenced node's pods, but the quiescence driver's model retains such pods forever. Also note the projection counts Succeeded/Failed pods as 'holding a GPU', which is factually wrong — a terminal-phase pod's resources are freed — and that error is a load-bearing half of the Q2 refutation.

answers.question: 4. AwaitingMint = activePods[key] > openActiveLeases[key] gates INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED. mirrorPods() adds exactly one pod per open lease. Does that make AwaitingMint FALSE in fixtures where it was previously true — and if so, has mirrorPods silently ENABLED a width check that those twelve tests were never written to satisfy, or silently DISABLED one? Which twelve tests, and what changed for each?
answers.conclusion: Neither enabled nor disabled — AwaitingMint was FALSE before mirrorPods and stays FALSE after. With zero pods, activePods=0 and 0 > N is false for any N; with exactly one pod per open lease, mirrored pods carry the lease's ROLE, so spare-lease mirrors are excluded from activePods exactly as spare leases are from openActiveLeases, leaving the two counts equal — still false. The width check was therefore armed on all twelve fixtures from the moment the oracle landed (7a681c0), via KnownToLedger=anyLease; that is precisely why fixtures were narrowed in that commit (e.g. node_failure_test.go:83-86 comment on TotalGPUs 4→2). Empirically confirmed both directions (TestOnePodPerLeaseKeepsTheWidthInvariantArmed): one pod per lease → INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED fires on an under-width Running run; a second unminted pod → AwaitingMint disarms it. The twelve call sites/tests: gang_width_test.go:181 TestReconcileDoesNotAdoptSpareOnlyLeases, :214 TestReconcileDoesNotAdoptOnGrowLeasesAlone, :239 TestSwapLeasesCountTowardGangWidth, :267 TestMalleableRunAdoptsAtMinWidth; run_controller_test.go:974 TestReconcileAdoptsHalfAppliedAdmission, :1049 TestActivateReservationAdoptsHalfAppliedActivation; node_failure_test.go:92 TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode, :123 TestNodeFailureReturnsTypedSentinelWhenNoLeaseNamesTheNode, :154 TestSwapLeavesACoLocatedRunOnOtherSlotsAlone, :190 TestSwapDeclinesRatherThanEvictAFundedRunOnTheSpareSlots, :297 TestDecliningTheSwapNeverStrandsTheRunsOwnSpare, :351 TestDecliningTheSwapReleasesTheSpareEvenWhenTheRunParksInCheckpointGrace. What changed for each: the fixture became INV-LEASE-HAS-POD-legal, and the pod-deleting halves of the paths under test (removeSparePodOnNodes in the swap tests, releaseRun's cull in the failure tests, adoption against a populated pod plane) now run against non-empty slices — though most of the twelve still assert only on leases; the pod-plane assertions live in two_plane_test.go. Residual weakness: mirrorPods places the pod on Slice.Nodes[0] only; harmless for today's single-node-lease fixtures, but a future multi-node-lease fixture would under-represent its pod set and could pass a per-node deletion path it does not exercise.

answers.question: 5. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is checked for terminal runs too (the loop runs it before the `if r.Terminal { continue }`? read the code and say exactly). Is a terminal run with open leases and no pods reported once or twice, and does the ordering of the checks in CheckSteady change which violation an author sees first?
answers.conclusion: It is NOT checked for terminal runs. In CheckSteady (pkg/invariant/invariant.go:248-283) the terminal block runs first — TerminalPresent (:250), then TerminalNoPods (:261), then `continue` (:271) — and the LeaseHasPod check sits after it (:273), so a terminal run never reaches it. Executed: a Failed run with one open lease and zero pods yields exactly [INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT] — reported ONCE (TerminalNoPods cannot also fire because Pods==0 is its pass condition). Within one run the append order is fixed: TerminalPresent before TerminalNoPods before (never) LeaseHasPod. Across runs, snapshotWorld ranges the c.State.Runs MAP (controllers/invariants.go:87), so the order of Runs in the World — and hence which run's violation an author sees FIRST in the banner — is randomized per process; cosmetic rather than semantic, because Panic prints all violations, but a class-3-shaped wobble worth knowing when diffing failure output.

answers.question: 6. Take the single best refutation you found. Write the Go test, run it, and paste the real output. If you cannot refute either invariant, say so plainly and name the strongest legal state you tried.
answers.conclusion: Best refutation: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD versus a drained node (TestLeaseHasPodFiresOnADrainedNode, scratchpad module invrefute, run against the real engine via a replace directive). Real output: '================ INVARIANT VIOLATION ================ | site: RunController.Reconcile | INV-LEASE-HAS-POD: run default/train: holds 1 open lease(s) (train-active-0-lease) and not one pod — the ledger is billing a budget and reserving GPUs for containers that do not exist | This state is illegal. If a test asserts it, the TEST is wrong: see docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md, class 8.' followed by 'after 20 simulated hours: the violation fired on 20 of 20 reconciles; the lease is still open, the run still Running' — --- PASS: TestLeaseHasPodFiresOnADrainedNode. The stimulus is a cordon+drain, the exact event R21 ruled must produce NO engine action, and the panic banner instructs the next author that any test modelling it is wrong. The companion refutation for INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS also ran red as predicted: 'INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS: run default/train: terminal (phase=Completed) but the workload plane still holds 1 pod(s)...' at site RunController.Reconcile on the pass immediately after a completion the engine performed correctly in both planes — and TestTerminalNoPodsCondemnsAnUnrelatedRunsReconcile shows the same terminating pod turning an unrelated healthy run's reconcile red.

findings.title: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is refuted: a drained (or GC'd) pod under a legally-open lease violates it forever on a correct engine
findings.failure_scenario: A kubectl drain / delete pod / pod GC of a Succeeded gang removes the pod object; per R21 no fencing assertion exists so the engine correctly closes nothing; pod delete events are filtered (reconcilers.go:153); SettleLeases only touches terminal/orphan runs. The violation fires at the return of every entry point forever (reproduced 20/20 simulated hours) — in CI it brands any faithful drain fixture illegal, in production it fires every 5-minute Running resync against an alert whose contract is 'any nonzero value is a bug'.
findings.taxonomyClass: 4 SIGNAL != REALITY (absence of a pod OBJECT read as an engine-shirked obligation); the 'wrong invariant is a reaper' failure mode pkg/invariant's own package doc names

findings.title: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS is refuted: Kubernetes' asynchronous graceful pod deletion makes the pass after EVERY ordinary completion or terminal failure illegal
findings.failure_scenario: completeRun/releaseRun retire both planes correctly; Bridge.apply issues the delete; the lease-closure events deterministically re-trigger the run reconciler (Lease watch, reconcilers.go:177) inside the termination window; Bridge.load reloads the terminating pod with no DeletionTimestamp/phase filter and binder.PodManifest cannot carry one. Reproduced: pass N legal, pass N+1 panics; an unrelated healthy run's reconcile also panics about the neighbour because the oracle checks the whole world.
findings.taxonomyClass: 4 SIGNAL != REALITY (a terminating or Succeeded pod object counted as 'holding a GPU the ledger handed back' — false for terminal-phase pods, whose resources are freed)

findings.title: Counting every role in Run.Pods lets one surviving SPARE pod mask the whole-active-pod-set deletion INV-LEASE-HAS-POD was written to catch
findings.failure_scenario: Executed: a Running run with an open ACTIVE lease, zero active pods, and one spare pod raises nothing. The coarse per-run form is documented as deliberate (per-lease correspondence deferred to R26's ledger auditor), but the masking direction is not documented; worth a line in the package doc or a role-aware refinement when R26 lands.
findings.taxonomyClass: 8-adjacent weakness (the rail passes states containing the defect class it exists to detect)

findings.title: The quiescence driver's 'legal world' model excludes pod deletion entirely — the only reason 800 seeds stay green under these two invariants
findings.failure_scenario: quiescence_test.go:53 declares 'Nothing in this file ... deletes a pod' as fidelity, yet the same file models kube deleting NODES (deleteNode), and real kube deletes pods routinely (drain, eviction API, pod GC — which force-deletes the pods of the very nodes deleteNode kills, another divergence: the driver retains a dead node's pods forever). Adding either legal event to the driver panics it immediately via the two invariant refutations above.
findings.taxonomyClass: 8 THE TEST ASSERTS THE BUG, generator form: the rail's legality model is exactly the invariants' blind spot

result.summary:
The investigator audited two invariants (INV-LEASE-HAS-POD, INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS) added on this branch by reading the playbook, AGENTS.md, history-run-phase-writers.md, pkg/invariant/invariant.go, controllers/invariants.go, settle.go, run_controller.go, kube/bridge.go, kube/reconcilers.go, and new test files, then built a compiled reproduction module in the scratchpad driving real engine entry points. Both invariants are found to be REFUTED by legal engine states, confirmed by running tests. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD fires forever after a routine drain or pod GC removes a pod object while its lease legally stays open, because R21 forbids acting without a fencing assertion, pod delete events are filtered out, and SettleLeases doesn't cover this case. INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS fires on the pass immediately after every ordinary completion because Kubernetes pod deletion is asynchronous/graceful and Bridge.load reloads the terminating pod unfiltered. Secondary findings: the KnownToLedger predicate change is semantically identical to the old anyPod; counting spare pods toward Run.Pods can mask a whole-active-pod-set deletion (executed); mirrorPods leaves AwaitingMint false exactly as before, so the width invariant's arming state is unchanged; INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is not checked for terminal runs due to a `continue` in CheckSteady, and a terminal run with an open lease and no pods reports INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT exactly once. The investigator recommends moving both rules to a transition tier (fire only on engine-caused divergence within a single pass) as the fix shape, and notes the 800-seed quiescence driver stays green only because it excludes pod deletion from its legal-world model while modelling node deletion by the same actor.</summary>

result:
# Summary

I read the mandatory playbook, then investigated `controllers/quiescence_test.go` (the R27b generator) by building a full filesystem copy of the repo under my scratchpad directory (never touching `/workspaces/jobtree` itself — confirmed clean via `git status --porcelain` throughout) and instrumenting/mutating that copy only, per the HARD RULES that forbid creating, editing, or deleting any file in the actual repository. Using that copy I added trace hooks at every load-bearing branch named in the task (lease-closure reasons, resolver action kinds, elastic grow/shrink, reservation activation, checkpoint-grace, reclaimSquatter's fail-closed loop, phase transitions) and ran all 800 enumerated seeds, producing a raw reachability table. I then ran six targeted mutations against historical defects and re-ran both the generator and the hand-written suite to see which caught which. I confirmed 0/800 seeds hit the settle() 12-round cap. I diffed `mintPending()` against `PreBind` line by line and found — critically — that `mintPending`'s random node selection fabricates physically impossible lease topologies (the same GPU slot claimed by up to 4 leases at once) in roughly a quarter of all observed states, a defect class the playbook explicitly warns about ("finds nothing but its own lies"). I ran all twelve `mirrorPods`-touched tests with the oracle downgraded to warn mode and mirrorPods removed, and all twelve still passed, proving none of their own assertions look at the pod plane. Overall conclusion: the generator is not decorative in the "0.25s, no spares" sense anymore, but it is far narrower than its own comments claim — it never reaches reservation activation, elastic shrink, several terminal branches, and it fails to catch 5 of 6 historical defect classes I mutation-tested (2 of those 6 — the oracle-corruption mutation and the group-index-default mutation — are caught by *nothing at all* currently in the repo, generated or hand-written).

# Answers

**1. Instrument the driver and report reachability across all 800 seeds; name every branch of HandleNodeFailure, applyResolution and reconcileElasticRun the generator never reaches.**

Raw table (counts are event/observation totals across 800 seeds × 45 steps + settle rounds, not seed counts):
```
phase:Running 7714   phase:Pending 68827   phase:Failed 4374   phase:Complete 2415
close:Swap 110   close:SwapDeclined 55   close:ReclaimedBySpare 40   close:NodeFailure 1705
close:RunFailed 175   close:Completed 467   resolve:ReclaimUnfunded 303 (DropSpare/Shrink/Lottery: 0)
reclaimSquatter:proceeds 7 (sibling-lease-on-same-node 3; fail-closed-decline 0)
checkpoint-grace-park 470   run:open-spare 9685   run:two-open-groups 2112
mint:role:Spare 929   mint:role:Active 2879   mint:reason:Swap 44   mint:reason:Grow 2
elastic-grow 1   elastic-shrink 0
applyResolution: shrunk-by-resolver 6, reclaimed-pending 111, ended-by-resolver 0
activateReservation: called 285, errored 158, already-terminal 2, pods-out-waiting 58,
  waiting-unfunded-deficit 67, [funded/opportunistic/adopted success: 0 each]
HandleNodeFailure: own-stale-lease-on-spare-slots 29, orphan-lease-no-run 0
PHYSICALLY-IMPOSSIBLE-slot-double-claim 9085 (see Q4)
settle() 12-round cap hit: 0/800 seeds
```
Branches **never reached**: (a) `HandleNodeFailure`'s pass-2 `if run == nil` orphan-lease branch (line ~1271) — the driver never deletes a `Run` from `state.Runs`, so this is structurally unreachable. (b) `reclaimSquatter`'s fail-closed decline `return false` (line 1533-1536) — called 7 times total, decline never taken once. (c) `applyResolution`'s terminal `default:` branch ("ended by resolver", `RunPhaseFailed`, line 1894-1896) — unreachable because the resolver never produces `ActionDropSpare`/`ActionShrink`/`ActionLottery`, only `ActionReclaimUnfunded`, so `reclaimedOnly[runKey]` is always true. (d) `reconcileElasticRun`'s voluntary shrink branch (`desired < allocated`, line 2699+) — never reached because `Malleable.DesiredTotalGPUs` is never set below `MaxTotalGPUs` by the driver, so `Desired()` (api/v1/run_types.go:268) always returns Max. (e) Bonus, adjacent to these three: all three success branches of `activateReservation` (funded/opportunistic/adopted) — zero successes in 285 calls.

**2. Mutation-test the generator against six historical defects.**

(a) `releaseRun` drops `removeAllRunPods`: caught by **generator** (fails at seed 1 with `INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS`) **and** hand-written suite. (b) `reclaimSquatter` calls `removeAllRunPods(victim)` instead of `removeRunPodsOnNodes`: generator **green** (`ok`, 0.99s); caught **only** by hand-written `TestReclaimingASquatterNeverLeavesALeaseWithoutItsContainer`. (c) `invariants.go` `RunnableGPUs` computed with `baseGangGPUsForRun` instead of `runnableGPUsForRun`: caught by **NEITHER** — generator green, full hand-written suite green. (d) `leaseGroupIndex` defaults missing label to `"0"`: caught by **NEITHER**. (e) `reclaimSquatter`'s fail-closed loop deleted: generator **green**; caught **only** by hand-written `TestSwapDeclinesRatherThanEvictAFundedRunOnTheSpareSlots`. (f) `runPhaseTracker`'s severity lattice replaced by plain last-write: generator **green**; caught **only** by hand-written `TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder` / `TestRunPhaseDoesNotDependOnLeaseOrder`. Summary: 1/6 caught by the generator; 3/6 caught only by hand-written tests (b, e, f); **2/6 caught by nothing at all (c, d)** — that is the answer that matters, and (c) is especially severe because it corrupts the oracle itself while leaving both test suites green.

**3. Count seeds hitting the 12-round settle() cap.**

**0 of 800 seeds** hit the cap (confirmed by instrumenting `settle()`'s cap-exit path directly and by the driver's own `t.Logf` — "seeds hitting the settle() 12-round cap: 0 -> []"). This is a generator that reliably converges, not a livelock — so this question resolves cleanly with no last-three-rounds diff to show, since the cap is never exercised across the full enumerated seed space.

**4. Diff mintPending() against PreBind line by line; name every difference and the blind spot it produces.**

(i) **Lease name**: `PreBind` (cmd/scheduler/plugin/plugin.go:296-298) appends `pod.Annotations[AnnotationRunNonce]` into the lease name when present (the R2 ABA-hazard fix); `mintPending` (controllers/quiescence_test.go:191) always uses `pod.Name + "-lease"`, never the nonce — and the driver never models Run delete+resubmit at all, so this is doubly blind to R2. (ii) **Payer segment**: `PreBind` validates swap/promise pod provenance against a real spare/promise lease (`spareLeaseProvenanceValid`/`promiseProvenanceValid`, the R5/R6 defenses) and can refuse to mint (`claimPayer` failing for an ordinary pod); `mintPending` trusts carried annotations unconditionally or falls back to `run.Spec.Owner` and **can never refuse to mint** — blind to R5/R6 forged-provenance regressions and to the entire "funding gate declines, pod stays unminted" legal state. (iii) **Node**: `PreBind` uses the real kube-scheduler's bound `nodeName` (post-Filter, capacity-guaranteed); `mintPending` (line 199-201) picks `qNodes()[w.rng.Intn(3)].Name` uniformly at random with zero capacity/occupancy awareness when `pod.NodeName` is blank — measured to produce the same GPU ordinal slot claimed by 3-4 different leases simultaneously in **9085 of ~37600 observed states**, a topology the real scheduler cannot produce; this is the most severe difference, since it means much of the 800-seed run explores states production cannot reach. (iv) **Group**: both fail closed identically on a missing group label (a genuine, deliberate parity point, not a gap).

**5. mirrorPods() on the twelve fixtures — does removing it change anything the test itself checks?**

I identified the twelve exact call sites (`gang_width_test.go` ×4, `node_failure_test.go` ×6, `run_controller_test.go` ×2) and grepped every one of their function bodies for `.Pods` references: **none of the twelve reference `state.Pods` anywhere except the `mirrorPods(state)` call itself.** With `JOBTREE_INVARIANT=warn` (bypassing the global `INV-LEASE-HAS-POD` panic) and `mirrorPods` removed, **all twelve still pass**. So for **all twelve**, `mirrorPods` is oracle-appeasement decoration only — none of their own assertions verify which pods were deleted, how many remain, or where. Under the normal (non-warn) invariant mode, removing `mirrorPods` fails all twelve identically via the global oracle panic, not via any test-specific logic. On the converse question — no case among the twelve was found where the synthesized pods changed a non-pod assertion's meaning (e.g. `runGangComplete` refusing completion), since none of the twelve reach a completion decision; this could not be fully excluded for other fixtures outside these twelve without further investigation, which I did not perform.

**6. Three defect classes the assert-oracle-only design can never catch, and hand-written coverage for each.**

(1) **Class 3, LAST-WRITER-WINS**: the seven shipped invariants check state *legality*, not *order-independence*, so a regression that makes phase-assignment order-dependent (mutation f) produces a legal-looking final state and the oracle stays silent; the generator went green on this exact mutation. Hand-written coverage: **yes** — `TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder` (order_independence_test.go) and `TestRunPhaseDoesNotDependOnLeaseOrder` explicitly permute lease order and assert byte-identical outcomes. (2) **Class 5, IDENTITY COARSENING**: the invariants have no notion of "a same-machine, different-slot lease must be unaffected by an unrelated swap" — that is a behavioral property, not a state-legality property, so mutation (e) (deleting the fail-closed scan) went undetected by the generator. Hand-written coverage: **yes** — `TestSwapDeclinesRatherThanEvictAFundedRunOnTheSpareSlots` and `TestSwapLeavesACoLocatedRunOnOtherSlotsAlone`. (3) **Class 7, CLONED OBLIGATION / stale default**: the "0" default in `leaseGroupIndex` (mutation d) is invisible to every invariant because production's fail-closed mint refusal (R28b) means no lease can legally lack a group label today — so the state that would trip any check never arises. Hand-written coverage: **none currently** — this is the one class where neither the generator nor any hand-written test defends the fallback, confirmed by mutation testing (both suites green).

# Evidence

findings.failure_scenario: When invariants.go's RunnableGPUs field is computed with baseGangGPUsForRun instead of runnableGPUsForRun, both the 800-seed generator run and the full hand-written test suite stay green; this corrupts the oracle itself (the very component meant to catch regressions) while producing no test failures anywhere in the repository, making the defect invisible to both verification layers simultaneously.

findings.title: leaseGroupIndex missing-label default of "0" caught by neither suite
findings.failure_scenario: Mutating leaseGroupIndex to default a missing group label to "0" is caught by neither the generator nor any hand-written test; the class is invisible to every invariant because production's fail-closed mint refusal (R28b) means no lease can legally lack a group label today, so the state that would trip a check never arises — leaving the fallback code path structurally undefended by any test.

findings.title: reclaimSquatter's fail-closed decline branch is never exercised by the generator
findings.failure_scenario: reclaimSquatter's fail-closed decline (return false, lines 1533-1536) is called 7 times across all 800 seeds but the decline path is never taken once; a regression that deletes this fail-closed loop entirely goes undetected by the generator (green) and is caught only by the hand-written TestSwapDeclinesRatherThanEvictAFundedRunOnTheSpareSlots test, meaning generator-only verification would miss a regression that could evict a funded run onto spare slots.

findings.title: runPhaseTracker severity-lattice mutation caught only by hand-written order-independence tests
findings.failure_scenario: Replacing the phase severity lattice with plain last-write (mutation f) produces a legal-looking final state that the seven shipped invariants (which check state legality, not order-independence) do not flag, so the 800-seed generator goes green; only TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder and TestRunPhaseDoesNotDependOnLeaseOrder, which explicitly permute lease order and assert byte-identical outcomes, catch this class of order-dependent phase-assignment regression.

result.summary: The investigator instrumented a scratch copy of the repo (never touching the actual working tree) to trace branch reachability across all 800 generator seeds, ran six mutation tests against historical defect classes, checked the settle() 12-round cap, diffed mintPending() against the real PreBind scheduler path line by line, tested mirrorPods() removal on twelve fixtures, and named three defect classes structurally invisible to the assert-oracle design. Key findings: several branches (HandleNodeFailure's orphan-lease path, reclaimSquatter's fail-closed decline, applyResolution's terminal default branch, reconcileElasticRun's voluntary shrink, and all three activateReservation success paths) are never reached in 800 seeds. Of six mutation-tested historical defects, only 1/6 is caught by the generator, 3/6 are caught only by hand-written tests, and 2/6 (a RunnableGPUs computation defect and a leaseGroupIndex default-label defect) are caught by nothing in the repo at all — the RunnableGPUs case is called out as especially severe because it corrupts the oracle itself while both suites stay green. Zero of 800 seeds hit the settle() cap, indicating reliable convergence rather than livelock. mintPending()'s random node selection (vs. PreBind's real bound nodeName) was found to fabricate physically impossible overlapping GPU-slot claims in roughly a quarter of observed states, and mintPending never models nonce-based lease naming (R2) or provenance validation (R5/R6), meaning it can never refuse to mint. mirrorPods() was found to be pure oracle-appeasement decoration for all twelve fixtures examined, with none of their own assertions referencing state.Pods. Three defect classes (last-writer-wins ordering, identity coarsening, cloned-obligation/stale-default) were identified as structurally invisible to the assert-oracle regardless of generator coverage, with hand-written test coverage confirmed for two of the three and confirmed absent for the third (leaseGroupIndex default).

I reviewed the branch under the SIGNAL!=REALITY (class 4), IDENTITY-COARSENING (class 5), and GUARD-BEFORE-PREDICATE (class 6) lenses. Class 4 (fenced/nodeFailed) is sound: nodeFailed keys ONLY on the out-of-service taint and node deletion (no clock, no NotReady, no cordon), the verdict is re-taken under the bridge mutex, and the shipped RBAC grants the controller only nodes get/list/watch so it cannot forge a fencing signal and NodeRestriction stops a kubelet from tainting its own node — no double-run hazard survives. Class 6 is clean: the R25 role/predicate ordering was fixed by splitting HandleNodeFailure into pass 1 (spares-on-node, sets handled) and pass 2 (actives), and reclaimSquatter scans ALL doomed leases fail-closed BEFORE any closure. But class 5 surfaced a real defect I confirmed by execution: under the sole committer the ledger slot ordinal (node#ordinal) is a per-lease 0-based index (pkg/admission PodLeaseWithRole builds slots[i]=node#i, no offset for existing node usage), so EVERY lease on a node includes node#0 and two unrelated runs bin-packed onto one machine collide on identical slot strings. leaseOccupiesSlots therefore collapses to node-granularity, defeating R22's stated 'exact GPU' premise (buildSlotSet/reclaimSquatter comments re-affirm the false premise). In pass 2 any co-located run on the spare's node is a FALSE conflict: a funded co-tenant forces a spurious swap-decline (the failing run loses a spare whose GPU is physically free) and an unfunded co-tenant is evicted in BOTH planes by reclaimSquatter (containers killed) though it never shared a physical GPU — a worsening this branch introduced by escalating that arm from a ledger-only close to a two-plane pod eviction. The 800-seed generator and oracle miss it because both outcomes are LEGAL states. A secondary class-5 coarsening: removeSparePodOnNodes matches the run's spare pod by node+role and ignores the group R28b now stamps.

answers.question: Does the change read any Kubernetes condition, taint, timestamp, or object-absence and treat it as a fact about what is PHYSICALLY running on a machine? For each: if that inference is wrong, WHAT RUNS TWICE?
answers.conclusion: The only physical-fact inference is fenced()/nodeFailed(), and it is correct by design: it fires ONLY on the node.kubernetes.io/out-of-service taint or the Node object's deletion (reconcilers.go:473-480, 330-337), both of which make Pod GC force-delete containers, so the rank is provably stopped. It reads NO NodeReady condition, NO cordon (Unschedulable), and NO timestamp, and the verdict is re-taken inside WithWorld under the bridge mutex (reconcilers.go:383) so a node recreated during the lock window cannot cause a stale swap. Nothing here can produce two live copies of a rank. nodeUsable() reads Ready/Unschedulable but only for the capacity pool (whether to PLACE new work), never to swap or close. Object-absence IS treated as fact in two safe places: fenced() treats NotFound as fenced (correct — deletion is force-GC), and SettleLeases treats an absent Run object as an orphan (settle.go:144) but the full uncached list in Bridge.load makes that reliable and it is explicitly not a shirk.

answers.question: For each such signal: WHO can write that field, and can they lie?
answers.conclusion: The out-of-service taint and Node deletion are the only inputs. deploy/helm/gpu-fleet/templates/rbac.yaml:23-25 grants the controller ServiceAccount only nodes get/list/watch — it cannot write taints or delete Nodes, so jobtree itself cannot manufacture a fencing verdict. A node's own kubelet writes its Node.Status conditions, but nodeFailed reads NONE of them; adding the out-of-service taint requires node update which the NodeRestriction admission plugin denies to a kubelet for its own Node, so a compromised/partitioned kubelet cannot forge the fence. Because nodeFailed takes no timestamp, a compromised kubelet also cannot backdate LastTransitionTime to fabricate a failure. The taint is applied only by a human operator or an external fencing agent — a party that actually knows the machine is dead. The trust model is sound.

answers.question: Does the change compare a COARSER key than the one that matters?
answers.conclusion: Yes — this is the primary finding. buildSlotSet/leaseOccupiesSlots (run_controller.go:2973-2989) compare node#ordinal slot strings intending the finest key (a physical GPU), but under the sole committer node#ordinal is NOT a physical-GPU identity: pkg/admission/admission.go:236 builds slots as node#i with i starting at 0 and NO offset for existing usage, so every lease on a node contains node#0. I proved by execution that two unrelated runs' 1-GPU leases on nodeS both get [nodeS#0] and leaseOccupiesSlots returns true. The finest key that should have been used is a real per-node physical GPU ordinal (as the binder's assign path produces via offset+i), which the plugin discards. Consequence: R22's promise that 'a run merely sharing the node on different GPUs is not [a conflict]' (run_controller.go:1274-1277) is unachievable — the slot test degenerates to node equality, so two runs that share a machine ALWAYS 'share a slot'. reclaimSquatter's own machine-granularity eviction (nodesOfSlots -> removeRunPodsOnNodes) is by contrast SAFE, because every plugin lease is single-machine, so its doomed set and pod cull stay two-plane consistent.

answers.question: Does the change contain a loop with BOTH a type/role filter (`if ... continue`) AND a match predicate? Check their ORDER.
answers.conclusion: Yes, and the ordering is now correct — this was R25 and it is fixed. HandleNodeFailure's old single loop skipped Role==Spare before the node-match, leaving a spare-only node with handled=false. The branch splits it: pass 1 (run_controller.go:1194-1212) filters to spares THEN tests the node and sets handled=true for a spare-only node; pass 2 (1248-1361) filters out spares THEN tests the node for actives. An active skipped in pass 1 is handled in pass 2, so no relevant item is suppressed. The inner pass-2 conflict loop (1286-1335) applies the closed check and the leaseOccupiesSlots match predicate BEFORE branching on run-key/funding class, so the guard cannot suppress a same-run stale lease. openRunLeasesOnNodes (1579) filters closed-then-runkey-then-node, which is safe because a closed lease is not funded and its GPU is already ledger-free. reclaimSquatter runs the fail-closed funding scan over ALL doomed leases before closing any (1529-1541), so no partial-closure window exists. No class-6 defect found.

answers.question: Grep the changed code and its callers for `strings.Contains(err.Error(), ...)` or any error compared by TEXT.
answers.conclusion: No production error is compared by text in the changed code. The former R25 swallow (a strings.Contains on the message) is GONE and replaced by a typed sentinel: reconcilers.go:401 uses errors.Is(err, controllers.ErrNoLeaseOnNode), the only error HandleNodeFailure returns (run_controller.go:1383 wraps ErrNoLeaseOnNode with %w). The plugin's PreBind adds a second typed sentinel, ErrNoPlacementGroup (plugin.go:350-367), also fail-closed. Every strings.Contains hit in the diff is a test assertion over a canonical state-dump string (order_independence_test.go, invariant_test.go), never an error-by-message swallow. Class 6 corollary is clean.

answers.question: note_on_reclaimSquatter_machine_granularity
answers.conclusion: I specifically verified the assigned lead that reclaimSquatter/removeRunPodsOnNodes coarsen slot identity to machine identity (run_controller.go:1516,1602). This coarsening is SAFE: every lease minted by the sole committer (PodLeaseWithRole) and by binder.assign is single-machine, so nodesOfSlots of the squatter's lease, the openRunLeasesOnNodes fail-closed scan, and removeRunPodsOnNodes all key on the same machine set and drop both planes together. The fail-closed loop correctly declines if any doomed same-run lease on those machines is not ClassUnfunded, excluding co-located FUNDED work of the same run. No half-plane arises from this path.

findings.title: Slot-conflict test collapses to node-granularity, defeating R22 exact-GPU premise
findings.taxonomyClass: 5 identity-coarsening
findings.failure_scenario: Run A has an active rank on nodeF (group 0) and a hot spare on nodeS (slot nodeS#0). An unrelated run B is bin-packed onto nodeS with a 1-GPU lease (also slot nodeS#0, a DIFFERENT physical GPU). nodeF is fenced. In HandleNodeFailure pass 2, spareSlots={nodeS#0}; leaseOccupiesSlots(B, spareSlots) returns true though B shares no physical GPU with A's spare. If B is FUNDED, crossRunConflict=true and A's group is sent to failGroupWithoutSpare — A loses a spare whose GPU was physically free, a spurious swap-decline of paid-for fault tolerance. If B is UNFUNDED, reclaimSquatter closes B's leases AND deletes B's pods on nodeS (two-plane), killing B's containers for a conflict that does not physically exist. This branch worsens the unfunded arm: pre-branch it was a ledger-only CloseLease; it is now a real container eviction. I executed a scratch program using the exported PodLeaseWithRole: two unrelated runs on nodeS both produced slot [nodeS#0] and the occupancy test returned true. The 800-seed quiescence generator and pkg/invariant miss this because a spurious decline (run parks Pending) and a spurious eviction (unfunded run demoted Pending, both planes dropped) are both LEGAL states — the oracle detects illegal states, not incorrect-but-legal decisions. Fix requires a physical-GPU ordinal in the ledger (as binder.assign's offset+i produces) or replacing the slot-conflict test with a real free-capacity check on the spare's node.

findings.title: removeSparePodOnNodes ignores R28b placement group when selecting spare pod to delete
findings.taxonomyClass: 5 identity-coarsening
findings.failure_scenario: Run A holds spares for group 0 and group 1, both bin-packed onto nodeS. Group 0's rank fails and the swap consumes group 0's spare (spareLease). The swap path calls removeSparePodOnNodes(A, [nodeS]) intending to free group 0's spare pod, but the loop matches on role=Spare + node only and removes whichever spare pod appears first in State.Pods — which may be group 1's. Result: group 0's spare lease is closed (CloseLease spareLease 'Swap') but its pod survives in State.Pods, so Bridge.apply keeps it running on a closed lease (half-plane: a pod holding a GPU the ledger freed), and the swap pod (requireNode nodeS) cannot bind because the GPU is still occupied — the gang wedges. Meanwhile group 1's spare pod is deleted while its lease stays open — an immortal lease and silent loss of group 1's fault tolerance. Reachability depends on the packer co-locating two groups' spares on one node; plausible over months but not demonstrated with a running test here. Fix: key the spare-pod removal on LabelGroupIndex of the consumed spareLease, not just node+role.

answers.question: For every site the change closes a Lease: whose container is still running? For every site it deletes a Pod: whose lease is still open? Name each pairing. Bridge.apply deletes exactly the pods absent from state.Pods, which is why forgetting is silent.
answers.conclusion: Pairings, closure→workload half: (a) HandleNodeFailure pass 1 spare on fenced node (1202) and failed actives (1261, 1347, 1428) → the pod half is delegated to Kubernetes Pod GC, which is sound because fenced() only answers on assertions (Node deleted / out-of-service taint) that force-delete pods. (b) Swap (1346) → removeSparePodOnNodes(1350) pairs the spare pod. (c) reclaimSquatter (1540) → removeRunPodsOnNodes(1549) over the same node set derived by openRunLeasesOnNodes — paired by construction. (d) releaseRun (1664) → removeAllRunPods(1668) inside the same function; all four callers (failRun, completeRun, HandleNodeFailure post-loop sweep, applyResolution default) inherit both planes. (e) applyResolution actions (1795) → closedGroups pod drop (1819-1831), paired per group; a legacy lease with no group label would yield GroupIndex "" and skip the pod drop, but INV-GROUP-STAMPED flags that world and the clean-break upgrade policy excludes it. (f) shrinkRun (2740/2743) → removePodsForGroups(2754). (g) SettleLeases closes doomed runs' leases (settle.go:172) AND drops their pods (178-187), both planes. (h) cleanupDeletedRun does both (reconcilers.go:105, 107-114). UNPAIRED, confirmed: the SwapDeclined spare closure (1437) removes no pod — on the Failed branch the post-loop releaseRun covers it, but on the checkpoint-grace branch the spare's holder pod on a HEALTHY node keeps its GPUs with its lease closed (executed: stranded 20 simulated hours after the run recovered). Also unpaired, low: the own-stale-lease ReclaimedBySpare closure (1297) removes no pod; reaching it requires the run's own duplicate lease on its spare's slots. Pre-existing unpaired: shrinkRun's error return at 2750 exits after closing groups but before removePodsForGroups.

answers.question: The checkpoint-grace window is a DELIBERATE, bounded half-plane state: the run parks Pending with a deadline and its containers keep running so they can write a checkpoint. Does the change reach it? Any sweep or cull that does is a reaper. Check the exemption keys on the deadline or on non-terminality, not on a phase name that could change.
answers.conclusion: SettleLeases does NOT reach it: it dooms only runs whose phase is Failed/Complete (settle.go:131) or whose Run object is absent, so the grace run is exempt via non-terminality — correct keying; TestSweepLeavesARunInCheckpointGraceAlone and TestCheckpointGraceKeepsTheContainersRunning pin it, and INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is written to tolerate it (pods > 0 during grace). The grace-expiry cull keys on the deadline itself (Reconcile:167, CheckpointDeadline non-nil and expired), also correct. BUT one path on this branch DOES reach the window: applyResolution's terminal default branch. Executed probe: a run parked Pending with 25 minutes of unexpired grace loses its surviving group to a lottery action; allocated < expected and not reclaimedOnly → Phase=Failed, message 'ended by resolver', and the branch's NEW releaseRun call deletes every pod — checkpoint writers included — with the deadline still standing. The branch consults neither run.Status.CheckpointDeadline nor checkpointGrace(run). The Failed verdict is pre-existing; the destruction of the surviving groups' containers mid-grace is new to this change (before it, those pods — buggily — survived). The node-failure path and the resolver path now give the same domain event (a checkpointable run losing a group) opposite treatments.

answers.question: Take each fix in this change. State, concretely, the legal state its repair would destroy if applied one step more aggressively. (Example: "close all the victim's leases" evicts funded work, because funding class is per-LEASE and one run can hold an Owned and an Unfunded lease at once.) If you cannot name one, say so — but look first at pkg/invariant's four rejected invariants.
answers.conclusion: Per fix: (1) R27g spare release — one step more (fail the run or close its other leases when the swap declines) destroys the checkpoint-grace re-admission, a legal recovery; the shipped fix already went a half-step too timid the other way by leaving the spare's pod. (2) F4/F5/F7 releaseRun — one step more (cull pods for any run whose leases are all closed, rather than only terminal runs) reaps the checkpoint-grace state itself, whose group lease is closed while containers deliberately live; and the obvious hardening of its comment-only precondition (assert phase-terminal inside releaseRun) would reap the SUCCESS path, because completeRun calls releaseRun at line 534 BEFORE writing RunPhaseComplete at 535. Note my Q2 probe shows the shipped fix, un-aggressively as written, already reaps grace-parked runs via applyResolution's default branch. (3) R28a/R28b no-default leaseGroupIndex — one step more (panic in production on an unstamped lease, or refuse to close it) makes every pre-R28b lease unkillable; the shipped warn-and-count is right. (4) #36 refix — one step more (treat NotReady under the lock as fenced) rebuilds R21's two-live-copies corruption; fenced() answering only on fencing assertions is the guard. (5) reclaimSquatter fail-closed — one step more (close ALL the victim's leases, not the node-scoped set) evicts the victim's Owned lease on another node, the playbook's own per-lease example; one step more conservative (decline if the victim holds ANY other lease) would make every multi-node unfunded victim unreclaimable and fail funded swaps that R14 says must proceed. (6) SettleLeases — the one-step-more rule was designed and refuted: sweeping 'a spare with no active of its group' closes healthy spares during normal per-pod minting and the legal spare-only-run state (settle.go:46-52, TestSweepLeavesASpareOnlyRunAlone). (7) The two new invariants — the per-lease strong form of INV-LEASE-HAS-POD would panic on the swap window and grace; the shipped coarse form still trips on legal external pod deletion (Q4).

answers.question: Does the change add or strengthen an INVARIANT? If so, find a legal state this engine really produces that would trip it. An invariant that is wrong is not a weaker safety net; it is a reaper that panics CI on healthy code and argues for a fix that rebuilds a shipped corruption.
answers.conclusion: Yes: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS and INV-LEASE-HAS-POD, plus the sweep's Shirked() panic. CONFIRMED legal trip of INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS: the graceful-deletion window. Bridge.apply deletes pods with default grace (bridge.go:314, no DeleteOptions) and bridge.load re-lists Terminating pods (bridge.go:190 filters only on the run-name label, never DeletionTimestamp), so for up to terminationGracePeriodSeconds after any completion, failure, or preemption, the world the bridge hands the engine contains a terminal run with pods > 0 — and reconciling ANY run then reports a violation (executed: reconciling a healthy run panicked with the INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS banner on the dead run's terminating pod). Production merely counts, but that falsifies the metric's own contract ('any nonzero value is a bug', metrics.go:497) on routine operations, and any envtest that binds pods (no kubelet ever finalizes a bound pod's deletion there — settle_bridge_test.go:38 does bind) and then drives a second pass is a latent CI panic. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD trips on legal external deletion: a fenced node's pods force-deleted by Pod GC before HandleNodeFailure runs (guaranteed when the manager was down during the node deletion — reconcilers.go:512 admits no event replays it), or an operator/TTL deleting Succeeded pod objects before the run reconciled; the run then holds open leases with zero pods on every entry point, and neither the engine nor SettleLeases (non-terminal run) can repair it. Half of that firing is correct detection of a real unhealed billing state; the other half is a self-correcting transient it cannot distinguish. The oracle is also structurally blind in the direction my confirmed F-A finding lies: a pod with no lease is exempted as the mint window, so the stranded spare holder never fires anything.

answers.question: Assume every finding you raised is real. For each: which budget is charged, how many GPUs are held, and for how long? An immortal lease is invisible — nothing crashes, nothing is logged, the cluster quietly gets smaller and someone quietly gets poorer. Quantify it.
answers.conclusion: F1 stranded spare pod (confirmed): no budget is charged — that is the lie's direction; the ledger says the GPUs are free. Held: spares-per-group × groupGPUs (2 in the probe) by a sleep-2147483647 container, from the declined swap until the OWNING run's terminal releaseRun — for a recovered training run, days to weeks. Secondary cost: the engine plans a new gang onto those 'free' slots; any sibling pods that DO bind get leases minted at PreBind and charge THEIR budget while the gang wedges assembling forever, so a victim admission burns real quota on partial mints indefinitely. F2 resolver culls the grace window (confirmed): both planes released correctly, so no GPUs leak; the loss is the paid-for work — every GPU-hour since the workload's last checkpoint (for a 512-GPU run checkpointing hourly, up to 512 GPU-hours per event), on every lottery cut of a checkpointable run below min width; budget tightening makes this routine. F3 INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS window noise (confirmed): zero GPUs, zero budget; the cost is the alarm — every preemption/completion of a run with a T-second termination grace produces violations on every bridge pass for T seconds, so in a busy cluster the counter that is documented as 'any nonzero value is a bug' is chronically nonzero, and the one detector for the repo's highest-severity class (the immortal lease) trains its operators to ignore it. F4 shrinkRun partial closure (pre-existing): the closed groups' pods hold their full group widths (unbounded multiples of StepGPUs) as dark capacity until the run terminates, uncharged and unplannable-onto; reachable whenever a funding-derived shrink target is not expressible as a partial sum of group widths. F5 own-stale-lease closure (plausible, rare): one group's width dark on the spare node, wedging the swap pod's required-affinity bind — the swap never completes and the rank is lost until the run fails out of grace.

findings.title: SwapDeclined lease closure leaves the spare pod running, stranding GPUs the ledger calls free
findings.taxonomyClass: 9-half-plane-action
findings.failure_scenario: Fenced node kills group's rank; spare's exact slots are squatted by funded work; swap declines; checkpointGrace > 0 parks the run Pending. The spare pod (sleep 2147483647, holding nvidia.com/gpu) stays in state.Pods — Bridge.apply deletes only pods ABSENT from state.Pods. Run re-admits inside grace, adoption clears CheckpointDeadline; the post-loop releaseRun never runs (run is not terminal). Executed probe: 20 simulated hours after recovery the pod still held 2 GPUs the ledger calls free; the engine plans new gangs onto them and kube-scheduler can never bind; INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is blind (it fires only on pods == 0). TestDecliningTheSwapReleasesTheSpareEvenWhenTheRunParksInCheckpointGrace asserts only the lease plane.

findings.title: applyResolution's terminal default branch kills checkpoint-grace containers before the deadline expires
findings.taxonomyClass: 9-half-plane-action
findings.failure_scenario: Run parked Pending by failGroupWithoutSpare with 25 minutes of grace left, surviving group's lease open, containers writing a checkpoint. Budget tightens; the lottery cuts the surviving lease; allocated < minRunnableGPUs and not reclaimedOnly → Phase=Failed, releaseRun deletes every pod. Executed probe output: phase=Failed, podsLeft=0, deadline 2024-01-01 10:25 still set. The identical domain event via HandleNodeFailure grants a 30-minute grace; via the resolver it is execution. The Failed verdict is pre-existing; killing the uncut groups' containers mid-grace is new to this branch's fix.

findings.title: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS fires on the ordinary graceful pod-deletion window, panicking on healthy reconciles and desensitizing the production alarm
findings.taxonomyClass: 8-wrong-invariant-reaper
findings.failure_scenario: Pass N: run fails, releaseRun drops pods, apply issues graceful Delete (bridge.go:314, no grace override). Pass N+1 within terminationGracePeriodSeconds: load lists the Terminating pods (bridge.go:190); ANY engine entry — including Reconcile of an unrelated healthy run — sees terminal run + pods > 0. Executed probe reproduced the panic banner from reconciling the healthy run. Production: jobtree_invariant_violations_total, documented as 'any nonzero value is a bug', becomes routinely nonzero after every preemption; envtest binds pods (settle_bridge_test.go:38) and has no kubelet to finalize deletion, so a bound pod stays Terminating forever — a latent CI panic for any test that drives a second pass after a terminal release.

findings.title: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD trips on legal external pod deletion the engine cannot repair from any entry point
findings.taxonomyClass: 8-wrong-invariant-reaper
findings.failure_scenario: Manager down; node deleted; kube's Pod GC force-deletes its pods; manager restarts — reconcilers.go:512 admits no event replays the deletion, so the leases stay open. Every entry point now reports the run holding open leases and zero pods, forever (SettleLeases exempts non-terminal runs; R26's auditor does not exist yet). Under go test this panics on healthy recovery choreography; in production it counts — half correct detection of a real unhealed billing state (leases charging with no containers, an immortal-lease variant nothing heals), half unresolvable noise. The quiescence driver cannot catch either side: its deleteNode never models Pod GC.

findings.title: shrinkRun's insufficient-groups error return strands closed groups' pods between the lease closures and removePodsForGroups (pre-existing, kept on the table)
findings.taxonomyClass: 9-half-plane-action
findings.failure_scenario: Malleable run, groups of 2/2/2/2 GPUs, funding-derived target 3: the loop closes two groups (freed=4), skips the rest (would overshoot), then current-freed=4 > 3 → returns the error at 2750 before reaching removePodsForGroups at 2754. Two groups' leases are closed (the bridge applies mutations even on error) while their pods keep running; later shrink passes cannot see the closed groups (collectElasticGroups skips closed leases), so the pods hold 4 dark GPUs until the run terminates. Confirmed by arithmetic against the loop's guards; not executed.

findings.title: Own-stale-lease ReclaimedBySpare closure removes no pod on the spare's slots, potentially wedging the swap pod's required-affinity bind
findings.taxonomyClass: 9-half-plane-action
findings.failure_scenario: If the run's own duplicate/stale lease on its spare's slots is ever backed by a live container, closing the lease leaves the container holding the GPU while emitSwapPod (1355) pins the replacement to that node with required affinity — the swap pod can never bind and the rank is not restored. Reaching the state needs a same-run lease occupying the spare's exact slots (an incomplete prior swap or oversubscribed mint); could not be constructed from legal events in the time available, and removeSparePodOnNodes (2138) removes only ONE spare-role pod, which also leaves a second same-node spare pod behind in the multi-spare case.

result.summary: This is a formatted transcription of an investigator's adversarial-review report on the jobtree branch fix/r27-invariant-oracle. The investigator read docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md, walked every CloseLease and State.Pods mutation site across controllers/run_controller.go, controllers/settle.go, controllers/kube/bridge.go, controllers/kube/reconcilers.go, pkg/invariant, and controllers/invariants.go, and confirmed hypotheses by executing pure-engine probe tests in a scratchpad copy of the repo (the real repo was not modified). Three findings were CONFIRMED by execution: (1) the SwapDeclined spare-lease closure at run_controller.go:1437 never removes the spare's pod, stranding GPUs the ledger reports as free when the checkpoint-grace branch is taken; (2) applyResolution's terminal default branch at run_controller.go:1888 reaches the checkpoint-grace window and its new releaseRun call destroys checkpoint-writer pods before the unexpired CheckpointDeadline; (3) INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS at pkg/invariant/invariant.go:90 trips on the ordinary graceful pod-deletion window because bridge.load does not filter DeletionTimestamp, causing panics in tests and chronic false-positive noise on the production violation counter. Two further findings were rated PLAUSIBLE: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD trips on legal external pod deletion (Pod GC racing HandleNodeFailure), and a pre-existing shrinkRun gap at line 2750 strands closed groups' pods. A sixth, low-severity finding concerns the own-stale-lease ReclaimedBySpare closure at line 1297 potentially wedging a swap pod's required-affinity bind, though the investigator could not construct the triggering state and rated it low confidence. The investigator also reports mutation-testing the branch's defenses (deleting releaseRun's pod cull, deleting the SwapDeclined closure, and an externally injected mutation of reclaimSquatter's phases.apply fold) and states these went red / were caught, arguing the class-3 rail is load-bearing. Several areas were adjudicated as sound and are not raised as findings: reclaimSquatter's fail-closed scan/closing-loop gap, openRunLeasesOnNodes' closed-lease skipping, fenced()'s assertion-only answering, the node_failure_test fixture width change, mirrorPods' one-lease-per-pod shape, and leaseGroupIndex's two existing copies.
