result:
Confirmed clean — no changes made to the actual repository; the test was written and run only in a scratch copy at `/tmp/claude-1000/.../scratchpad/jobtree-copy`.

## Summary

**Verdict: NOT refuted. The finding is confirmed, and reproduction turned up a stronger, presently-reachable specimen than the one the finding hypothesized.**

I read `docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md` in full (it names this exact pattern as class 7, CLONED OBLIGATION, and even quotes `groupIndexForPodIndex` by name as an example of a second implementation of `pack.deriveGroups` pinned only by a test). I then read `pkg/pack/pack.go`'s `deriveGroups` and `controllers/run_controller.go`'s `groupSizesFor`/`groupIndexForPodIndex`, confirming they are line-for-line the same "consecutive groups of size N, last one short" loop, deliberately duplicated (the function's own doc comment says so) because `topUpActiveGang` re-emits a lost rank with no `pack.Plan` in hand. The pin test, `TestPodGroupDerivedFromSpecMatchesThePackersGrouping` in `controllers/group_identity_test.go`, only exercises five shapes and only ever sets `run.Spec.Locality.GroupGPUs`, never `run.Spec.Roles[0].GroupGPUs`.

That gap is exploitable today, without any future change to `pack.deriveGroups` at all. The API doc for `RunRole.GroupGPUs` states it "optionally overrides spec.locality.groupGPUs for this role," and `groupGPUsFor` (used by the spec-only clone) honors that override by checking `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` before `Locality.GroupGPUs`. But `planPlacement` — the actual packer entry point used at admission (`controllers/run_controller.go:1960`) and at elastic grow (`~line 2673`) — never reads `Roles[0].GroupGPUs`; it only reads `Locality.GroupGPUs`. I copied the repo to a scratch directory (`/tmp/claude-1000/.../scratchpad/jobtree-copy`, no changes made to the real working tree — `git status --short` in `/workspaces/jobtree` is clean) and wrote and ran two Go tests against the real, unmodified engine code (no mutation, no hypothetical): one construction of a `Run` with `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` set to 4, `Locality.GroupGPUs` left nil, 8 total GPUs. `planPlacement` (the real packer) produced ONE group and put pod index 4 in group `"0"`; `groupIndexForPodIndex` (the spec-only clone) produced TWO groups and put the same pod index in group `"1"`. Output: `role_group_repro_test.go:73: DIVERGENCE: packer says group "0", spec-only clone says group "1" for the same rank.` A second test confirmed `planPlacement` produces an identical plan (same group count) whether or not `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` is set, proving the real packer ignores that field entirely. I also traced `topUpActiveGang` (`controllers/run_controller.go:2381-2384`) and found it unconditionally calls `emitCohortPods(run, nil, ...)` — placements is always `nil` there, so *every* pod it re-emits after a base-gang member's pod object goes missing (a routine node-failure/kubelet event) goes through the spec-only clone, not just an overflow edge case. Finally I checked `pkg/invariant/invariant.go`'s `GroupStamped` check (line ~236: `if l.GroupIndex == ""`) and confirmed it only tests for a non-empty string, exactly matching the finding's claim that "INV-GROUP-STAMPED still passes (the label is present, just wrong)."

This is not the class-8 "test suite passes" refutation, not a "pre-existing, unchanged" refutation (the divergence is live and reachable via a documented API field, not a hypothetical future edit to `deriveGroups`), and not an "unusual sequence" refutation (node failures causing pod re-emission are routine, per the task's own framing). I am setting `ranCode=true` and `reproduced=true`: I saw the bad state — mismatched group labels for the same rank — in real test output.

**Answers to the assigned lens (reproduction):**
1. *Did you run code, and did it reproduce?* Yes. I wrote and ran `TestRoleLevelGroupGPUsOverride_PackerVsSpecOnlyClone_Diverge` and `TestPlanPlacementIgnoresRoleLevelGroupGPUs` against a scratch copy of the unmodified engine; both demonstrate the packer and the spec-only clone assigning different group indices to the same pod for a legally-constructed `Run`, and that `planPlacement` never reads `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` at all.
2. *Is the fix an obvious reaper?* No obvious reaper risk from what I tested — a correct fix would be to export `deriveGroups` from `pkg/pack` (or an equivalent pure function taking `(total, groupSize)`) and have both `planPlacement`'s request-building and `groupSizesFor` call the single implementation, eliminating the clone rather than widening the pin test's example set. I did not attempt or evaluate a specific patch, so I cannot rule on reaper risk for any particular fix, only note the shape a non-reaper fix should take (make the properties true by construction, per playbook class 2, rather than adding more pinned examples).
3. *Is the failure scenario in the original finding accurate?* The finding undersold it: it framed the risk as contingent on "if `pack.deriveGroups`' group layout is ever changed," but I found the two implementations already disagree today, without touching `deriveGroups`, because they read different source fields (`Roles[0].GroupGPUs` vs `Locality.GroupGPUs`) with different precedence.

**Evidence:**
- `controllers/run_controller.go:2481` — `// groupSizesFor mirrors pack.deriveGroups: consecutive groups of groupGPUs, the last` (the duplication is acknowledged in-line).
- `controllers/run_controller.go:2468` — `if len(run.Spec.Roles) > 0 && run.Spec.Roles[0].GroupGPUs != nil {` (the spec-only clone honors the role-level override).
- `controllers/run_controller.go:1963` — `if run.Spec.Locality != nil && run.Spec.Locality.GroupGPUs != nil {` (this is the only `GroupGPUs` source `planPlacement` ever reads — `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` is absent from this function entirely).
- `api/v1/run_types.go:103` — `// GroupGPUs optionally overrides spec.locality.groupGPUs for this role: the` (the documented contract that the packer's `planPlacement` silently fails to honor).
- `pkg/invariant/invariant.go:236` — `if l.GroupIndex == "" {` (confirms the oracle checks only presence, not correctness, of the group label).
- `controllers/run_controller.go:2384` — `return c.emitCohortPods(run, nil, gpusPerPod, width, "0", reason, extra)` (confirms `topUpActiveGang` always uses the spec-only fallback, for every re-emitted pod, not merely an overflow case).

I have completed the trace. Here is my conclusion.

## Verdict: REFUTED (as a current-tree defect via the stated mechanism), with an honest caveat below

**The finding as literally stated does not describe the current tree.** The reviewer's failure scenario is explicitly conditional: *"If `pack.deriveGroups`' group layout is ever **changed** for a shape not in the pin's set."* That is a hypothetical future edit, not a state the code as written produces. I traced both implementations and they are not merely "example-agreeing" — they are the **same algorithm, character for character**:

- `pack.deriveGroups` (pkg/pack/pack.go:331): when `groupSizePtr == nil` returns `[]int{total}`; otherwise loops `size := groupSize; if size > remaining { size = remaining }; append; remaining -= size`.
- `groupSizesFor` (controllers/run_controller.go:2486): `total := expectedActiveGPUs(run); size := groupGPUsFor(run)`; if either `<= 0` returns nil; otherwise loops `n := size; if n > remaining { n = remaining }; append; remaining -= n`.

These are the identical "consecutive groups of size G, last possibly short" fold. They therefore agree on **every** total/size pair, not just the five pinned tuples `{8/0, 8/4, 9/4, 12/4/2, 64/32}`. The pin passing (I ran it: `ok controllers 0.009s`) is not a coincidence of examples — it reflects that the loop bodies are the same code. So in the current tree there is **no shape** for which a re-emitted rank gets a group index the packer never chose *via the deriveGroups-layout mechanism*. The reviewer's own class-7 concern ("two implementations always drift; the question is whether something notices") is real and is already catalogued in the playbook as class 7 CLONED OBLIGATION, but as an **active** defect the finding does not hold: the drift it describes requires someone to first edit `deriveGroups`. Severity-low, latent, documented. I did not need to speculate — the equivalence is a direct read, and the pin executes green.

**Provenance note (not a refutation):** the clone is **new in this branch** — `git log -S "func groupIndexForPodIndex"` returns only e681a96 (R28b). So this is not pre-existing; R28b introduced a second implementation of the packer's grouping rule and a five-example pin as its only guard. That is exactly the shape class 7 warns about, but it is a maintainability risk, not a live miscomputation.

## Caveat I am obligated to surface: a *concrete, current* divergence on this same clone, via a different input path

While tracing the inputs, I found that the clone and the packer **already disagree today** for a legal spec — not after any future edit. The group-**size** source differs between the two planes:

- The packer's request builders read the group size **only** from `Locality.GroupGPUs`: `planPlacement` (run_controller.go:1966) and the grow path (run_controller.go:2673) both do `if run.Spec.Locality != nil && run.Spec.Locality.GroupGPUs != nil {...}` and pass `nil` otherwise. `grep` confirms these are the only non-test group-size reads that feed `pack.Request`.
- The clone's size, `groupGPUsFor` (run_controller.go:2467), checks **`Roles[0].GroupGPUs` first**, then falls back to `Locality.GroupGPUs`.

`api/v1/run_types.go:103` documents `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` as *"optionally overrides spec.locality.groupGPUs for this role,"* and the webhook (run_types.go:391) only requires it be positive when set — it does **not** require `Locality.GroupGPUs` to also be set. So a roled run with `Roles[0].GroupGPUs = 4` and `Locality == nil` is a valid spec. For that run: the packer sees `groupSize == nil` → `deriveGroups(total, nil)` → **one** group, and `packPlacements` stamps every base pod group `"0"`; but `groupIndexForPodIndex` (via `groupGPUsFor` → 4) yields groups `0,1,2,…`. At initial emit this is masked because `emitCohortPods` overrides the spec-only group with `placements[i].Group` when a plan is present (run_controller.go:2189-ish). On the **`topUpActiveGang` re-emit path — where `placements` is empty because the plan is long gone** — the pod is stamped with the spec-only group, which can be `"1"`/`"2"`, a group the packer never created. That is precisely the "re-emitted rank joins a group nobody else is in" consequence the reviewer described, reached through the input asymmetry rather than a future `deriveGroups` edit. The five-example pin cannot catch it because `giRun` only ever sets `Locality.GroupGPUs` and never exercises the `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` branch of `groupGPUsFor`.

I did **not** execute this one: the hard rules forbid me creating a test file in the repo, and the pure-engine simulator route requires an in-package test. The claim rests entirely on the code trace above, which is why I hold it at moderate rather than high confidence. Two reachability qualifiers keep it from being a slam-dunk: (a) the packer ignoring role-level `GroupGPUs` outright suggests role-level group override may be an effectively-unwired knob, so if no one sets it the divergence never manifests; (b) it is a **different** defect (input-source asymmetry) than the one under review (future-layout drift), so it should be filed separately, not folded into this refutation.

## On the obvious "fix" and whether it is a reaper

The clean fix for the stated finding is to **delete the clone** and have `groupSizesFor`/`groupIndexForPodIndex` call `pack.deriveGroups` directly. That is *not* a reaper — it removes the second implementation entirely, which is the canonical class-7 remedy. But note it does **not** resolve the caveat divergence, because that lives in the disagreement between `groupGPUsFor` (role-first) and the packer's request builders (locality-only); collapsing the layout clone would still leave the two planes deriving the group **size** from different fields. The real repair for the caveat is to make one function the sole source of `(total, groupSize)` feeding both the `pack.Request` and the spec-only re-emit — i.e. teach `planPlacement`/the grow builder to honor `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` the same way `groupGPUsFor` does (or vice versa), so the packer and the top-up agree by construction.

Bottom line: **refute the finding as stated** (no current miscomputation via the deriveGroups-layout mechanism; groupSizesFor *is* deriveGroups), but flag the adjacent role-vs-locality input divergence as a real, separately-fileable current defect that the same inadequate pin also fails to cover.

VERDICT: CONFIRMED — refuted=false. The finding is real, and it is materially UNDERSTATED: the divergence between `groupIndexForPodIndex`/`groupSizesFor` and the packer's grouping is not a hypothetical future drift gated on someone editing `pack.deriveGroups`; it exists today, on the DEFAULT run configuration, and I reproduced it by execution on the production code path. I ran code; it reproduced; output is pasted below.

SUMMARY of what I did and concluded. I read docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md in full, then read the clone (controllers/run_controller.go: `groupGPUsFor`, `groupSizesFor`, `groupIndexForPodIndex`), the canonical implementation (pkg/pack/pack.go: `deriveGroups` and the three planner paths), the pin test (controllers/group_identity_test.go), the controller's request builder (`planPlacement`), the consumer (`emitCohortPods`/`topUpActiveGang`), and the plugin's mint gate (cmd/scheduler/plugin/plugin.go: `mintGroupIndex`). I found two structural asymmetries the pin never exercises, then confirmed both by copying the repo to my scratchpad (/tmp/claude-1000/.../scratchpad/repo — no repo files were touched), adding `controllers/clone_drift_probe_test.go` there, and running three probes. All three failed in the direction the finding predicts, including one that drives the exact production path (`emitIntentPods` → external pod loss → `topUpActiveGang`). The clone is NEW in this branch (commit e681a96, R28b): `git show $(git merge-base main HEAD):controllers/run_controller.go | grep -c groupIndexForPodIndex` returns 0. So preExisting=false. The R28b commit's stated purpose is "the packer's groups reach the ledger and the pods" — and across a gang top-up, for a default-configured run, they do not; a change that fails its stated purpose is a finding in its own right.

THE TWO LIVE DIVERGENCES (both confirmed by execution, neither requiring any future change to deriveGroups):

1. Default spread runs. `api/v1/run_types.go:256` — `AllowCrossGroupSpread` applies "the API default (true) when the field is unset" — and `planPlacement` (run_controller.go:1961,1975) passes that through, so a default run with no GroupGPUs routes to `planFillDomains` (pack.go:83), whose group layout is TOPOLOGY-DERIVED (one group per fabric domain, sized by free capacity). No spec-only function can reproduce that layout even in principle, yet the comment at run_controller.go:2188 asserts "the group is recomputed from the Run spec, which is what the packer laid out" — playbook class 2, a comment as the only enforcement, and it is false. `groupSizesFor` returns ONE group for such a run (groupGPUsFor falls through to `expectedActiveGPUs`).

2. Role-level GroupGPUs. The clone (run_controller.go:2468) prefers `run.Spec.Roles[0].GroupGPUs`; `planPlacement` (run_controller.go:1963) reads ONLY `run.Spec.Locality.GroupGPUs`. `Roles[0].GroupGPUs` is consumed by NO other non-test code in the repo (grep confirms). So a roled run declaring group size via the role override gets a one-group plan from the packer and a chunked-by-4 answer from the clone.

The pin test covers neither: it builds `pack.Request` by hand with `AllowCrossGroupSpread` at its zero value (false) and never calls `planPlacement` (grep for either string in group_identity_test.go returns nothing), so it pins the clone against `planSingleDomain` — a packer path production uses only when a run EXPLICITLY sets allowCrossGroupSpread: false.

EXECUTED REPRODUCTION (probe 2 is the end-to-end production path). From `go test ./controllers/ -run TestProbe -v` in the scratchpad copy:

- TestProbeDefaultSpreadRunDiverges: 8-GPU default run, two 4-GPU fabric islands. Packer: group 0 (island-0) + group 1 (island-1). Clone: "0" for all eight pods. "DIVERGENCE CONFIRMED: 4 of 8 pods stamped a different group by the clone."
- TestProbeTopUpReEmitsWrongGroup: `emitIntentPods` stamped train-active-4..7 group "1"; I removed train-active-7 from state.Pods (external deletion during assembly — node GC, operator delete); `topUpActiveGang` re-emitted it: "re-emitted train-active-7: group \"0\" (was \"1\")". The plugin then mints its lease with that label verbatim — `mintGroupIndex` (plugin.go:362) fails closed only on an ABSENT label; a wrong-but-present one passes, and INV-GROUP-STAMPED likewise checks presence, exactly as the finding says.
- TestProbeRoleGroupGPUsDiverges: packer 1 group, clone says pods 4-7 are group "1". 4 mismatches.

Reachability: `topUpActiveGang` is called from the two pre-Running assembly branches (run_controller.go:238 and :819), both reachable whenever `allocated > 0` and a pod object has gone missing — a node failure during assembly, Pod GC, an operator delete, a half-applied bridge write (R28 is literally the :806 branch's premise). Over months of operation this is routine, and the triggering configuration is the DEFAULT.

CONSEQUENCE (lens question 1, assuming real — it is real). No budget is mischarged and no lease goes immortal: the mint charges the correct owner the correct amount, and the lease stays tied to a live run and pod. The damage is ADDRESSING, per-run, for the run's lifetime: (a) the resolver's per-group lottery and `collectElasticGroups` see group 0 with an extra lease and group 1 short one, so an elastic shrink that cuts group 1 kills 3 of its 4 pods while the mislabeled fourth rank keeps its pod AND its open funded lease — one GPU burning power in a decapitated gang, charging its owner, until the run terminates (weeks, for a training run), with nothing red; (b) a node-failure swap pairs the dead rank with the WRONG group's spare (`findSpareLease` matches by group), or declines; (c) the re-emitted rank itself joins a gang across a fabric island boundary the packer deliberately avoided — silent collective-bandwidth degradation for the whole gang. GPUs held wrongly: up to one rank's worth per incident stranded doing no useful work, plus a whole group's worth of mis-cut healthy ranks per mis-addressed shrink.

REAPER CHECK (lens question 2 — INVERT). Split verdict, stated explicitly: (a) The tightening fix — making `mintGroupIndex` or a new INV-GROUP-CORRECT invariant verify the label AGAINST the spec-derivation — IS A REAPER: every legal `planFillDomains` run carries packer groups that are topology-derived and spec-underivable, so the check would refuse to mint (wedging legal assembling gangs) or panic on healthy state; it would join the four rejected invariants in pkg/invariant's package doc. fixIsReaper=true for that shape of fix, and I veto it. (b) Merely exporting `pack.deriveGroups` and calling it from controllers — the literal de-cloning the finding's class suggests — is NOT a reaper but is also NOT a fix: it cannot reproduce a topology-derived layout, and unless `groupGPUsFor` and `planPlacement` are also unified it leaves divergence 2 intact. (c) The correct, non-reaping fix exists because both `topUpActiveGang` call sites run only when `allocated > 0` — open leases exist — and the missing rank's own lease name is derived from the pod name (plugin.go:295, `leaseName := pod.Name + "-lease"`) and carries the packer's true group: recover the group from the LEDGER (or a surviving sibling pod of the same index range) and fall back to spec-derivation only when no evidence survives. That fix stamps a label on a pod being created; it closes no lease, deletes no pod, and destroys no legal state — including the spare-only-run and mid-swap states the invariant doc protects. fixIsReaper=false for that fix.

ANSWER TO THE ASSIGNED QUESTION (refute or confirm the finding): CONFIRMED, refuted=false, preExisting=false (introduced by e681a96 in this branch), severity is understated at "low" — I assess medium: the trigger configuration is the API default, the stimulus (a pod lost pre-Running) is routine over months, the consequence mis-addresses the resolver/shrink/swap machinery R28b exists to make addressable, and every rail (pin test, mint gate, INV-GROUP-STAMPED) checks the wrong property (presence or the wrong packer path) so nothing turns red. The finding's specific failure mechanism ("if deriveGroups is ever changed") is the WEAKEST form of the defect; the strong form needs no change at all. fixIsReaper depends on the fix: correctness-verification at mint/invariant = reaper (vetoed); ledger-evidence recovery in topUpActiveGang = not a reaper (recommended).

**VERDICT: NOT REFUTED. The finding is confirmed by the code trace, and I reproduced its core premise by executing the real sole-committer mint. This is a genuine instance of playbook class 5 (identity coarsening) — the very class the R22 fix claims to have closed — and this branch measurably worsens its unfunded arm.**

**The core premise is true: the `#ordinal` is a per-lease local index, not a physical GPU identity.** The sole committer is `plugin.go:299`, which calls `admission.PodLeaseWithRole`. That function mints slots with a loop that resets to zero for every lease:

`pkg/admission/admission.go:234-237`:
```
	slots := make([]string, gpusPerPod)
	for i := range slots {
		slots[i] = fmt.Sprintf("%s#%d", node, i)
	}
```
`i` runs `0..gpusPerPod-1` and bears no relation to which physical GPUs on the node were bound. Every lease on a node therefore begins at `node#0`. The binder's other path is no better — `pkg/binder/binder.go:250` does `slots[i] = nodeSlot{node: chunk.Node, ordinal: offset + i}` with `offset := 0` reset per chunk (per node) at line 241. Neither committer path encodes a globally-unique physical ordinal. And the ledger's capacity model is count-based, not identity-based: `pkg/topology/snapshot.go:28` `FreeGPUs()` returns `n.Capacity - n.Used` — a scalar. There is no physical-GPU set anywhere for a slot ordinal to name, so "the same physical GPU" is not even a representable concept in this ledger. The R22 comment's stated goal — "a run merely sharing the node on different GPUs is not [a conflict]" (`run_controller.go:1274-1276`) — is therefore not achievable with the ordinals the engine actually mints. A change that fails to achieve its stated purpose is a finding in its own right.

**I executed it (did not speculate).** I built a scratch program that calls the real `admission.PodLeaseWithRole` for two unrelated runs, each a 1-GPU lease on `nodeS`:
```
A spare slots : [nodeS#0]
B active slots: [nodeS#0]
leaseOccupiesSlots-equivalent overlap : true
```
Two runs that share no physical GPU produce byte-identical slot strings, and the slot-set overlap test (`buildSlotSet`/`leaseOccupiesSlots`, `run_controller.go:2973-2989`) returns true. The collision is not exotic — it is the deterministic result of the format string, for any node holding ≥2 GPUs where two runs each hold ≥1. Bin-packing multiple runs onto one 8-GPU node is the scheduler's normal mode, so the scenario is routine, not rare.

**The trace through HandleNodeFailure pass 2 holds end to end.** Nothing filters run B out of the inner loop at `run_controller.go:1286-1291`: it checks only `j != spareIdx`, `j != i`, `!other.Status.Closed`, and `leaseOccupiesSlots(other, spareSlots)` — no node filter, no role filter — so B (open, on nodeS, holding `nodeS#0`) qualifies. `otherKey != runKey` (line 1295) routes to the cross-run branch. Both arms behave exactly as the finding claims:
- **Funded B**: `class != ClassUnfunded`, so `crossRunConflict = true` and `failGroupWithoutSpare(run A, …, spareLease)` fires (line 1331-1341). Run A declines a swap it did not need — A's swap re-places onto A's *own* already-held spare slot — and its spare lease is released, discarding paid-for fault tolerance for a physically nonexistent conflict.
- **Unfunded B**: `reclaimSquatter(other, …)` (line 1322) runs `nodes := nodesOfSlots(lease.Spec.Slice.Nodes)` = `{nodeS}` (line 1516), passes the fail-closed check (line 1533-1536, B unfunded), closes B's leases (line 1539-1541), and calls `c.removeRunPodsOnNodes(victimKey, nodes)` at line 1549 — a real two-plane container eviction of an innocent co-tenant.

**This branch worsens the unfunded arm — confirmed against the merge base, not asserted.** At merge base `f243eb8`, the same unfunded arm was ledger-only: `closeLease(other, "ReclaimedBySpare", now)` with no pod removal. On HEAD it is `reclaimSquatter` → `removeRunPodsOnNodes`. Making the eviction two-plane is the *correct* fix for playbook class 9 when the conflict is real; but because the conflict detection is a false positive, that fix raises the blast radius of every false positive from "ledger lies / immortal container" to "a legitimately-running, physically-non-conflicting unfunded container is deleted." So this is not pure pre-existing debt: `preExisting` is true for the coarse-key comparison, but the branch both (a) enlarges the consequence of the spurious trigger via the new two-plane eviction, and (b) ships an R22 comment asserting a discrimination the code cannot perform. Reachability is unchanged-and-routine; consequence is worsened. It does not refute.

**Why the oracle and the 800-seed generator miss it (accurate as stated).** Both outcomes are *legal* states: a run that declines a swap parks Pending; a demoted unfunded run parks Pending with both planes dropped consistently. `pkg/invariant` detects illegal states (immortal lease, terminal-with-pods, width-not-assembled, closed-monotone), not incorrect-but-legal *decisions*. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD and INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS are likewise satisfied — reclaimSquatter drops both planes together. There is no invariant that says "you only evict work that physically conflicts," because the ledger cannot express physical conflict.

**Is the obvious fix a reaper?** The finding's first suggestion — a true physical-GPU ordinal in the ledger — is not a small change: it would require the sole committer to learn which physical GPUs are free at PreBind and would redefine every slot string across the codebase; the count-based capacity model has no such information today, so it is not merely a granularity tweak. The second suggestion — replace the cross-run slot test with a real free-capacity check on the spare's node — is the sounder direction and is *not* a reaper: note that the swap lands on run A's own already-held spare lease, so A already owns that GPU count and needs no capacity from B at all, which means the cross-run conflict path is arguably spurious in its entirety for a count-based ledger. A careless "fix" that simply reverts to node-granularity would reintroduce R22, and one that keeps the two-plane eviction while widening the trigger would reap innocent containers faster — so the fix needs a ruling in `docs/project/quota-semantics.md`, but a correct fix exists and does not reap. That does not lower the finding: the defect as traced is real and worsened on this branch.

## Finding: `removeSparePodOnNodes` ignores R28b placement-group identity — CONFIRMED, NOT REFUTED

**I read the code, traced reachability through the packer, and reproduced the exact failure with a compiled, running test against the actual `HandleNodeFailure` entry point on a production-shaped two-group fixture.** The finding is real. I could not refute it.

### What the code does

`removeSparePodOnNodes` (controllers/run_controller.go:2127) selects the spare pod to delete using only namespace, run name, role, and node membership:

```go
func (c *RunController) removeSparePodOnNodes(run *v1.Run, nodes []string) {
	want := buildNodeSet(nodes)
	for i := range c.State.Pods {
		p := &c.State.Pods[i]
		if p.Namespace != run.Namespace || p.Labels[binder.LabelRunName] != run.Name ||
			p.Labels[binder.LabelRunRole] != binder.RoleSpare {
			continue
		}
		if p.NodeName == "" || want[p.NodeName] == 0 {
			continue
		}
		c.State.Pods = append(c.State.Pods[:i], c.State.Pods[i+1:]...)
		return
	}
}
```

It never reads `binder.LabelGroupIndex`, even though that label is stamped onto real spare pods in production by `emitSparePods` (`binder.LabelGroupIndex: group` at controllers/run_controller.go:2098) — the very label R28b (commit e681a96) introduced so consumers could stop defaulting group to `"0"`. I checked that commit's diff (`git show e681a96 -- controllers/run_controller.go`): it edited `emitSparePods` right next to this function (stamping the real group instead of `"0"`, deleting the now-dead `flattenSpareNodes` helper) but left `removeSparePodOnNodes` itself completely untouched. R28b's stated purpose — group identity reaching the pods — does not reach this call site.

### Reachability: can two groups' spares actually land on one node?

I read `pkg/pack/pack.go`'s `assignSpares` (line 194 on). It loops over `placements` (one per group) **independently**, and for each group first tries `dom, ok := snapshot.DomainByKey(placements[i].Domain)` (pack.go:203) — i.e., the domain that group's own active work landed in — then falls back via `chooseDomainForSpare`. There is no cross-group bookkeeping that excludes a node already used for another group's spare; `allocateInDomain` just greedily fills free capacity node-by-node within the domain. Two groups of the same run whose active work shares a fabric domain (a small run, or a small island) will commonly have their spare requests satisfied from the same pool of nodes, and if a node has enough spare free capacity for both, both groups' spares land there. This is exactly the "richer than reality" scenario the reviewer describes, and nothing in the packer prevents it — I confirmed this by reading the allocation loop, not by assumption.

### Reproduction

I copied the repo to a scratch directory and wrote a test that drives `HandleNodeFailure` (the actual production entry point, not the helper directly) on a fixture with a two-group run: group 0's active lease on `node-a` (which fails), group 0's spare on `node-b#0,1`, group 1's active lease on healthy `node-c`, and group 1's spare on `node-b#2,3` — i.e., both groups' spares co-located on `node-b`, group 1's spare *pod* placed first in `state.Pods` (pod order reflects mint order, not group order, so this is realistic). Running it:

```
spare0 closed=true reason="Swap"; spare1 closed=false reason=""
after swap: spare0-pod present=true, spare1-pod present=false
HALF-PLANE BUG: spare0's lease is closed (freed in the ledger) but its pod SURVIVED --
the GPU the ledger just freed is still occupied by a running pod, so the swap pod for
group 0 cannot bind there
IDENTITY-COARSENING BUG: group 1's spare pod was deleted even though group 1's spare
LEASE (spare1) is still open (closed=false) -- an immortal lease with no pod, and
silent loss of group 1's fault tolerance
```

Both consequences the reviewer predicted materialized exactly: the lease that was actually closed (`spare0`, `Swap`) kept its pod running (a half-plane action — the ledger says the GPU is free, the workload plane says it is occupied), while the *other* group's still-open lease (`spare1`, still open) lost its pod (an immortal lease with zero fault-tolerance coverage, silently). `HandleNodeFailure` did not panic — the invariant oracle is wired in but did not fire, which I checked is expected: `pkg/invariant`'s `INV-LEASE-HAS-POD` (invariant.go:138, "LeaseHasPod") is explicitly documented as the coarse per-**run** form — "it asks whether the run has any pod at all, not whether each lease has its own" (invariant.go:133) — and the run in question still has three other pods, so the coarse check is satisfied. The doc comment itself says the exact per-lease correspondence "belongs in R26's ledger auditor," and I grepped the repo for any implemented auditor function (`grep -rln "func.*[Aa]uditor"`) and found none — so no rail in this branch currently catches this class of bug.

### Verdict

I set `ranCode=true` and `reproduced=true`: I compiled and ran a Go test against the real `HandleNodeFailure` path with a production-shaped fixture and saw the bad state (wrong pod deleted, wrong lease left open, half-plane mismatch on the lease the swap actually consumed) directly in the test output. The proposed fix — keying `removeSparePodOnNodes` on `LabelGroupIndex` of the consumed `spareLease` rather than node+role alone — is not a reaper: the label already exists on every real spare pod (`emitSparePods` stamps it), so the fix only needs to add one more label comparison to an existing filter; it does not need to invent new state or change what's legal. I default to `refuted=false` (the finding stands) because I have direct, reproduced evidence rather than an unresolved argument.

All verification is complete. Here is my report.

## Verdict: NOT refuted — CONFIRMED, executed end-to-end against the real engine. The claimed class (5, identity-coarsening) is correct, the mechanism is exactly as stated, and the funded arm's consequence is *worse* than the finding claims. Severity split: the funded-decline arm is pre-existing at merge-base; the unfunded arm's two-plane container eviction is new in this branch. The finding's "free-capacity check" fix is not a reaper; its "physical-GPU ordinal" fix is architecturally unavailable at PreBind.

### What I ran, and what it showed

I did not rely on the reviewer's scratch program (which is in the scratchpad and only exercised the slot-overlap primitive). I wrote a full-engine reproduction at `/tmp/claude-1000/-workspaces-jobtree/b3df7d06-fa76-4793-98b7-31b44b11906e/scratchpad/slotrepro/main.go` (external module, `replace`-directive against the repo; no repo files touched, no git mutation). Every lease is built by the **real production mint** — `admission.PodLeaseWithRole`, the exact function `cmd/scheduler/plugin`'s PreBind calls — and the engine is driven through the real entry point `controllers.RunController.HandleNodeFailure`. Fixture: run A active on node-a, hot spare on node-b; co-tenant run B with one 1-GPU lease; every node has 4 GPUs, so node-b has 2 GPUs physically free and there is **no physical conflict** in any case. It compiled and ran; output reproduced the finding in all three cases:

- **CASE 1 (B funded, on node-b):** the mint produced `a-spare=[node-b#0]` and `b-active=[node-b#0]` — colliding slot strings for different physical GPUs. `HandleNodeFailure("node-a")` emitted `SwapSlotConflict: spare slots for group 0 are held by funded run default/run-b; declining the swap`, closed the spare `SwapDeclined`, and drove run-a to `phase=Failed msg="node node-a failed without spare coverage"` — **terminal**, with its remaining leases released and pods dropped. This is worse than the finding's "spurious swap-decline": absent checkpoint configuration the covered run is not parked, it is dead. B untouched.
- **CASE 2 (B unfunded, on node-b):** `b-active` closed `ReclaimedBySpare`, **`b-active-pod` deleted from the pod plane**, run-b demoted `Pending ("reclaimed by a node-failure swap; will re-admit when capacity allows")`, then the swap proceeded. B's container was killed for a conflict that does not physically exist — 2 of node-b's 4 GPUs were free.
- **CASE 3 (control, B funded on node-c):** identical fixture, different machine — swap proceeds, run-a `Running`. This isolates node-sharing as the sole trigger.

### Why the coarsening is structural, not a fixture artifact

The sole committer's mint is `pkg/admission/admission.go:236`: `slots[i] = fmt.Sprintf("%s#%d", node, i)` — every production lease's ordinals start at 0, per pod, unconditionally. So **any** two co-located open leases collide at `#0`, and `leaseOccupiesSlots(other, spareSlots)` (`controllers/run_controller.go:1284`, `spareSlots := buildSlotSet(spareLease.Spec.Slice.Nodes)`) is exactly equivalent to `leaseContainsNode` for production-minted leases. The R22 comment's claim at run_controller.go:1277 — "the correct common case this sweep now closes nothing" — is false in production: the conflict fires for every co-tenant of the spare's machine. Note `binder.assign` (`pkg/binder/binder.go:250`, `slots[i] = nodeSlot{node: chunk.Node, ordinal: offset + i}`) is no better: `offset` restarts at 0 per allocation chunk, so it also collides across runs; and binder is not the production committer anyway (PLUGIN-2). The exact-GPU premise of R22 ("buildSlotSet keys on the FULL node#ordinal slot… a swap reclaims only the exact GPUs it re-places onto", run_controller.go:2971) is encoded nowhere in the data.

The test fixtures conceal this: `controllers/two_plane_test.go` hand-crafts ordinals production cannot mint (`prodLease("paid", …, []string{"node-b#2"}, …)` at line 208; `"sibling"` on `"node-b#2"` at line 90; `nfLease("squatter", …, []string{"node-b#0","node-b#1"}, …)`). Playbook class 8 applies to the fixtures' stimulus: the ordinal-distinguishing states these tests exercise do not exist in production. The quiescence generator's `mintPending` **does** use the real `admission.PodLeaseWithRole`, so the 800 seeds did produce colliding ordinals — and stayed green, because the finding is right about oracle blindness: a spurious decline (Failed/Pending, both planes consistent) and a spurious two-plane eviction (Pending, both planes dropped) are both LEGAL states; `pkg/invariant` judges legality, not decision quality. No invariant can say "this run should have swapped."

### Consequence, quantified (my lens)

- **Funded arm (Case 1):** no budget is charged afterward (`releaseRun` closes everything — no immortal lease) and no GPUs are held. The loss is availability: a run that paid for a hot spare loses its entire gang, terminally, on the first node failure whose spare shares a machine with any funded co-tenant. For sub-node (bin-packed) workloads, sharing a machine is the common case, so this largely defeats the spare feature for exactly those workloads; full-node gangs are unaffected. Frequency: per node failure, which this system treats as routine.
- **Unfunded arm (Case 2):** B loses its containers on that machine and is demoted Pending; legal and self-correcting under R14 (unfunded work is reclaimable by definition), so the cost is the discarded work of a preemptible run — real but bounded.

### Pre-existing split (classification, not refutation)

At the merge-base, the funded-decline arm was byte-for-byte the same logic (verified via `git show $(git merge-base main HEAD):controllers/run_controller.go`, lines ~1240-1290): Case 1 is **pre-existing**. The unfunded arm at merge-base was `closeLease(other, "ReclaimedBySpare", now)` — ledger-only (itself the playbook's class-9 specimen). This branch's `reclaimSquatter` (run_controller.go:1549, `c.removeRunPodsOnNodes(victimKey, nodes)`) correctly made the reclaim two-plane — but because the trigger predicate is spuriously node-granular, the branch converted a spurious bookkeeping error into a **spurious physical container eviction**, and its new fail-closed arm (a funded sibling anywhere on the machine forces decline) **widens the funded-decline arm's reachability**. Under the playbook's own rule, making the consequence physical and the decline more reachable is worsening; "pre-existing" does not refute the branch-specific delta, and the R22 comment's stated purpose ("in the correct common case this sweep now closes nothing") is not achieved by the code as it runs in production — a finding in its own right.

### Reaper check — I confirm the finding AND vet its fixes separately

- **Fix A ("physical-GPU ordinal in the ledger"): unavailable, do not pursue as stated.** PreBind runs before kubelet device assignment; the plugin knows only the node name, so a physically-true ordinal cannot exist in the mint. Making ordinals unique-per-node (allocate against open leases at mint) is a race across concurrent PreBinds and a redesign of the sole committer. Also `binder.assign`'s `offset+i` is not physical either, contrary to the finding's aside.
- **Fix B (free-capacity check on the spare's node): not a reaper.** I inverted it against every legal state the current path protects: (1) tolerated ledger oversubscription (`pkg/invariant/invariant.go:29`, the rejected invariant `"no two open leases hold the same node#ordinal slot"`) — a GPU-count check (open-lease GPUs on the node − spare's GPUs + swap demand vs. capacity) still declines on genuine shortfall, preserving the decline-not-evict ruling; (2) the fail-closed funded-sibling guard in `reclaimSquatter` remains intact for the shortfall case, since eviction stays node-granular; (3) the checkpoint-grace half-plane window is untouched. Residual risk is a wedge, not a reaper: if the pod plane holds GPUs the ledger doesn't see, the swap pod parks Pending on its hard affinity — no healthy work destroyed, and the current code shares that blindness. One warning for the fixer: **deleting the conflict test outright WOULD regress** the tolerated-oversubscription state (swap wedges, squatters never reclaimed) — the predicate must be replaced with the capacity check, not removed. **VERDICT: NOT REFUTED. The finding is confirmed by code trace and reproduced by a running engine test.** This is a genuine class-5 identity-coarsening defect that cascades into a class-9 half-plane action, and it is newly reachable in *this* branch.

**The trace, function by function.**

The swap path in `HandleNodeFailure`'s pass 2 (`controllers/run_controller.go`) selects the spare *for the failing group specifically*:

- `spareLease, spareIdx := findSpareLease(c.State.Leases, runKey, groupIndex)` — and `findSpareLease` (line ~2943) genuinely keys on group: `if leaseGroupIndex(lease) != group { continue }`. So `spareLease` is unambiguously group 0's spare.
- On the clean-swap branch (lines ~1345-1350) the code does:
  ```
  spareNodes := leaseNodeNames(spareLease)
  CloseLease(spareLease, "Swap", now)
  CloseLease(lease, "NodeFailure", now)
  c.removeSparePodOnNodes(run, spareNodes)
  ```
  `leaseNodeNames` (line ~2963) maps each slot through `nodeFromSlot`, i.e. it discards the `#ordinal` and yields **machine names**. So `spareNodes` is `["node-s"]`, the machine group 0's spare sits on.

- `removeSparePodOnNodes(run, nodes)` (line ~2127) then matches **only** run name + `RoleSpare` + machine:
  ```
  if p.Namespace != run.Namespace || p.Labels[binder.LabelRunName] != run.Name ||
      p.Labels[binder.LabelRunRole] != binder.RoleSpare {
      continue
  }
  if p.NodeName == "" || want[p.NodeName] == 0 {
      continue
  }
  c.State.Pods = append(c.State.Pods[:i], c.State.Pods[i+1:]...)
  return
  ```
  It **never reads `binder.LabelGroupIndex`**, and it returns after the *first* match. So when two groups' spares share a machine it deletes whichever spare pod the API list happened to place first in `state.Pods`.

The group label is present on the pods and therefore available — it is simply ignored: `emitSparePods` (line ~2098) stamps every spare pod with `binder.LabelGroupIndex: group`, and that labeling was added by **this branch** (commit `e681a96`, R28b — confirmed via `git log -S "LabelGroupIndex: group"`). Before R28b every spare defaulted to group `"0"`, so a node+role match was harmless; R28b gave spares real per-group identity in both planes but left `removeSparePodOnNodes` matching the coarse key. This is exactly the playbook's "reached by a new door."

**I did not speculate — I executed it.** I wrote a standalone driver against the pure engine (`controllers.NewRunController` + `HandleNodeFailure`), scratch dir `/tmp/claude-1000/-workspaces-jobtree/b3df7d06-fa76-4793-98b7-31b44b11906e/scratchpad/repro/main.go`. State: run with group-0 active on `node-a` (fails), group-0 spare on `node-s#0`, group-1 spare on `node-s#1` (same machine `node-s`), both spare pods bound to `node-s`, group-1's pod listed first. Driving `HandleNodeFailure("node-a")` produced, verbatim from the run:

```
group0 spare LEASE closed=true reason="Swap" ; group0 spare POD still present=true
group1 spare LEASE closed=false (open==immortal) ; group1 spare POD still present=false
swap pod emitted=true
HALF-PLANE: group0 spare lease closed but its pod survives -> occupies node-s#0 the swap pod targets -> WEDGE
IMMORTAL: group1 spare lease OPEN but its pod was deleted -> charges budget forever + lost fault tolerance
```

Both harms in the finding land exactly as described: (a) group 0's spare lease is closed `"Swap"` but its pod survives on the very slot `node-s#0` that the emitted swap pod hard-targets — a closed-lease/live-pod half-plane that wedges the swap; (b) group 1's spare lease stays **open** while its pod is deleted — an immortal lease plus silent loss of group 1's fault tolerance. Flipping the pod order so group 0's pod is first makes the removal correct (group0 pod gone, group1 pod kept) — proving the outcome is decided purely by `state.Pods` ordering, which in production is unspecified API-list order. So on any co-located swap it is roughly a coin flip which group loses.

**Nothing downstream heals it.** `SettleLeases` closes leases only for terminal or absent runs; here the run stays `Running` and present, so group 1's orphaned lease is not swept. And `INV-LEASE-HAS-POD` is blind to it by construction: its check (`pkg/invariant/invariant.go` ~line 273) fires only when `len(names) > 0 && r.Pods == 0` — i.e. the run holds open leases and **zero** pods. Our run still holds the swap pod and group 0's surviving spare pod, so `r.Pods > 0` and the invariant is silent. It is a per-run count, not per-lease/per-group, so a per-group orphaned lease slips through — consistent with the playbook's own warning that the oracle counts leases, not pods per group.

**Reachability.** Requires the packer to co-locate two groups' spares on one machine. `assignSpares` (`pkg/pack/pack.go` ~194) assigns each group's spares independently, preferring the group's own domain and falling back across domains via `chooseDomainForSpare`; nothing forbids two groups' spare allocations landing on the same machine when capacity is fragmented. "It needs an unusual packing" is not a valid refutation on a scheduler that runs for months with fragmented, failing capacity — and the branch's own multi-group support (R28b) is what makes the state expressible at all.

**The fix is not a reaper.** `removeSparePodOnNodes` is already called for one specific group (the `groupIndex` that `findSpareLease` matched). Threading that `groupIndex` in and adding `p.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex] != groupIndex { continue }` targets exactly group 0's spare pod. It strictly narrows the match, cannot over-delete, and cannot leave the correct pod alive (emitSparePods always stamps the group). So the obvious fix is safe and correct, not an over-broad reaper.

**Severity note:** the finding claims medium; the reproduced consequences are an immortal lease plus a wedged gang — the two highest-severity classes in this repo — so I would rate it at least high, gated only by the packer-colocation precondition.

## Verdict: CONFIRMED (refuted=false) — mechanism reproduced by running code; the claimed immediate consequence is overstated, the durable consequence is real; the proposed fix is NOT a reaper.

### Summary

I read the playbook in full, read `removeSparePodOnNodes`, its sole caller in `HandleNodeFailure`'s swap path, `findSpareLease`, `emitSparePods`, `sparePlacements`, `assignSpares` (pkg/pack), the plugin's `buildPod`/`buildLease` (pkg/binder), and the production pod mirror in `controllers/kube/bridge.go`. I then built an external Go test module in the scratchpad (no repo files touched) that constructs the exact claimed state — run "train", group 0 active on node-a, group 1 active on node-b, both groups' spare leases and spare pods co-located on node-s — and called `c.HandleNodeFailure("node-b", now)` through the pure engine, with the pkg/invariant oracle live (the binary is `*.test`, so `invariant.UnderTest()` was true). **ranCode=true, reproduced=true.** Output: `lease spare-g1: closed=true reason="Swap"` (group 1's spare lease correctly consumed) but `train-spare-0 (group 0) was deleted` while `train-spare-1` (group 1) survived on node-s with its lease closed, and `spare-g0`'s lease stayed open with no pod behind it. The oracle stayed silent — INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is per-run, and the run still holds pods. The crossing is exactly as the finding describes: the deletion key at run_controller.go:2131 is namespace+run+role+node, while the lease consumed one line earlier was selected by `findSpareLease(..., groupIndex)` at group granularity. This is textbook class 5 (identity coarsening) decaying into class 9 (half-plane divergence). Repro test: `/tmp/claude-1000/-workspaces-jobtree/b3df7d06-fa76-4793-98b7-31b44b11906e/scratchpad/sparerepro/spare_repro_test.go`.

### Answer 1 — is the finding real, and is it refutable?

Real, and I found no valid refutation. Every refutation route closes:

- **"Two groups' spares never share a node."** False twice over. In the plan, `assignSpares`'s `tried` set is per-group, and `chooseDomainForSpare` returns the first sorted free domain — two groups readily plan spares into the same domain. And the plan is not binding anyway: emitSparePods' comment says `NodeName:  node, // advisory only; the bridge turns this into soft affinity` (run_controller.go:2093), and bridge.go:448 says `spec.NodeName = "" // the plugin/scheduler places the pod; never pin here`. The kube scheduler may co-locate regardless of the plan. The lease slots record the *actual* bind, and production `State.Pods` mirrors `pod.Spec.NodeName` (bridge.go:253), so the engine sees the co-location.
- **"The loop order saves you."** Production pod order is API-list order (name-sorted: `train-spare-0` < `train-spare-1`), so whenever the consumed lease's group is not the first-sorted co-located spare's group — roughly half of such swaps — the wrong pod dies. My repro hit it deterministically.
- **"Pre-existing."** preExisting=true for the function body: `removeSparePodOnNodes` is byte-identical at the merge-base (introduced in 64d8f6c, CASCADE-3b). **But the refutation fails on reachability, which this branch created.** Before R28b (e681a96, ON this branch), every pod and lease was stamped group "0" — the commit message itself says "every pod was stamped LabelGroupIndex '0', and the sole committer stamped no group at all... Nothing was corrupted, because everything was uniformly wrong." R28b made group identity real in `pkg/resolver`, `collectElasticGroups`, `findSpareLease`, and `removePodsForGroups` — and its stated purpose was that "the packer's groups reach the ledger and the pods". `removeSparePodOnNodes` is the one group-addressed pod operation that commit did not migrate. Making the fine key exist everywhere except the deletion site is what makes the crossing possible; the branch made a dead path reachable, and a change that fails to achieve its stated purpose at one of its own sites is a finding in its own right.

### Answer 2 — consequence, assuming real (the lens's first question)

The finding's *immediate* failure scenario is overstated in both of its claims, and I state this explicitly because the severity rests on it:

- **"The swap pod cannot bind — the gang wedges": wrong at bind time.** A run's pod shape is uniform (`intentPodShape` uses `Roles[0]` only), so both spares are the same GPU count. Deleting the *wrong* spare still frees the *same number* of GPUs on the *same node*, and Kubernetes allocates GPUs by count, not by jobtree's `node#ordinal` — the ordinals are ledger bookkeeping. In my repro the swap pod was emitted normally and node-s's count-accounting balances in both planes (physical: g1-spare-pod + swap = 4/4; ledger: g0-spare-lease + swap lease = 4/4).
- **"Immortal lease / silent loss of fault tolerance": inverted.** Group 1's lease is the one *closed* (correctly — it was consumed). Group 0's lease stays open, but it is a live standby reservation the run still wants and intends to pay for, and a standby-sized physical hold still exists on that node (the mislabeled pod). `findSpareLease("0")` still finds it; a later group-0 swap physically works and — because `removeSparePodOnNodes` is symmetrically coarse — deletes the remaining spare pod and self-corrects. No budget is mischarged in this window.

The **durable** consequence is real and arrives through the group-keyed pod operations R28b just made live. After the crossing, the planes disagree about *which* group's standby exists (lease says g0, pod says g1). The first group-addressed cut of group 0 breaks it open: `applyResolution` drops pods by `group := pod.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex]` keyed on `closedGroups` (run_controller.go:~1823), and `removePodsForGroups` (elastic shrink, called at :2754) does the same. Resolver preemption after a budget tightening and elastic shrink are routine — the quiescence generator itself treats "budget tightened" as a legal external event. When group 0 is cut: its leases close, its pod key `run::0` matches nothing (the g0 pod is already gone), and the g1-labeled zombie survives with **no open lease**. Named precisely: **gpusPerPod GPUs (2 in the repro, typically 1–8) held by a leaseless pod for the remaining lifetime of the run — weeks to months, since only run terminality (`releaseRun`/`SettleLeases`) drops the run's whole pod set — charged to nobody, invisible to `pkg/funding`, while the engine plans gangs onto node capacity the ledger says is free and kube can never bind, wedging new admissions on that node.** That is the playbook's class-9 second-direction lie verbatim, and no invariant sees it: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is per-run, INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED counts leases. Severity **medium** is right — I'd have refuted "high" (no immediate wedge, no billing error), but the two-step zombie is squarely reachable in a months-long deployment.

### Answer 3 — reaper check on the proposed fix (the lens's second question)

**fixIsReaper=false.** The fix — pass the consumed `spareLease` (or its group) into `removeSparePodOnNodes` and additionally require `p.Labels[binder.LabelGroupIndex] == leaseGroupIndex(spareLease)` — destroys no legal state, because the fine key exists on both sides *by construction on this branch*: `emitSparePods` stamps `binder.LabelGroupIndex: group` on every spare pod (run_controller.go:2098), the plugin copies the group off the pod when minting the lease (`buildPod`/`buildLease` in pkg/binder/binder.go both stamp `LabelGroupIndex: fmt.Sprintf("%d", groupIndex)`; cutover_test.go:97 pins "the plugin copies it off the pod (R28b)"), and an unstamped lease is now a bug the build rejects (leaseGroupIndex's doc: "The default is gone... INV-GROUP-STAMPED fails the build rather than papering over it"). So lease-group == pod-group for every plugin-minted spare pair, and the group-keyed match finds exactly the right pod; where a group holds two spares on one node they are interchangeable within the group, so first-match remains correct. I checked the four rejected-invariant legal states specifically: a spare-only run, a mid-swap Running run, oversubscribed slots, and a cordoned node's leases are all untouched by a *narrower deletion key* — narrowing which pod dies cannot reap anything the current code preserves; its only failure direction is deleting *nothing* (e.g., a hypothetical unlabeled legacy pod), which leaves capacity held rather than evicting funded work — the conservative direction, and this repo's clean-break policy (R13) rejects the legacy-pod concern anyway. One caution for the fixer: the `emitSparePods` top-up wrinkle at run_controller.go:2075–2080 (`group := "0"` when `i >= len(placements)`) means pod-side groups can default to "0" — harmless today because the lease inherits the pod's label, but it is the same defaulting smell R28a killed in `leaseGroupIndex`, and worth cleaning in the same change. Separately: do NOT reflexively add a per-group lease↔pod pairing invariant as part of this fix — a kubelet-evicted spare pod legally leaves its lease open until the next top-up pass, and the plugin legally mints a spare pod before its lease; an unreviewed pairing invariant here would be a reaper of exactly the kind the branch's own package doc warns about.

### Evidence (verbatim)

1. `/workspaces/jobtree/controllers/run_controller.go:2131` — `if p.Namespace != run.Namespace || p.Labels[binder.LabelRunName] != run.Name ||` followed at :2132 by `p.Labels[binder.LabelRunRole] != binder.RoleSpare {` — the deletion key: run + role + (at :2135) node; no group.
2. `/workspaces/jobtree/controllers/run_controller.go:2955` — `if leaseGroupIndex(lease) != group {` — `findSpareLease` selects the consumed lease at group granularity; the asymmetry with (1) is the whole defect.
3. `/workspaces/jobtree/controllers/run_controller.go:2093` — `NodeName:  node, // advisory only; the bridge turns this into soft affinity` — co-location of two groups' spares on one node is reachable regardless of the packer's plan.
4. `/workspaces/jobtree/controllers/run_controller.go:2098` — `binder.LabelGroupIndex: group,` — the spare pod carries the fine key, so the group-keyed fix has something to match.
5. `/workspaces/jobtree/controllers/kube/bridge.go:253` — `NodeName:    pod.Spec.NodeName,` — production State.Pods mirrors the real bind node and real labels, so the engine sees the co-located reality.
6. `/workspaces/jobtree/docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md` (class 5) — "Sharing a machine is not sharing a slot." — the class predicted this shape; here it is "sharing a node is not sharing a group".

### Findings

One finding, confirmed: **removeSparePodOnNodes deletes a spare pod keyed on run+role+node while the swap consumes a spare lease keyed on group; with two groups' spares co-located on one node the wrong group's pod is deleted (executed and reproduced), leaving lease/pod group identity crossed across the planes; a later group-keyed cut (resolver preemption, elastic shrink) then strands a leaseless zombie pod holding GPUs the ledger reports free for the rest of the run's lifetime.** CONFIRMED, ranCode=true, reproduced=true, refuted=false, preExisting=true (function body predates the branch) but reachability is new to this branch (R28b made group identity real everywhere except this site — worsening, per the playbook's own rule), severity=medium (the claimed immediate wedge and immortal-lease effects are overstated and I refute those two sub-claims specifically; the durable half-plane zombie stands), fixIsReaper=false (key the deletion on `leaseGroupIndex(spareLease)`; safe by construction under INV-GROUP-STAMPED; do not bundle an unreviewed pairing invariant with it).

## Verdict: NOT REFUTED — the finding is CONFIRMED by direct execution. refuted=false, reproduced=true, ranCode=true.

**What I did.** I read `docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md` in full, then read `controllers/run_controller.go`'s `failGroupWithoutSpare` (the decline-the-swap branch at the finding's cited location), its sibling accepted-swap branch, `releaseRun`, `SettleLeases` (`controllers/settle.go`), the two new invariants in `pkg/invariant/invariant.go`, and the test the finding names (`controllers/node_failure_test.go:331`, `TestDecliningTheSwapReleasesTheSpareEvenWhenTheRunParksInCheckpointGrace`). I then copied the whole repo to `/tmp/claude-1000/-workspaces-jobtree/b3df7d06-fa76-4793-98b7-31b44b11906e/scratchpad/jobtree_probe` (never touched the real repo — no edits, no git mutations) and wrote a compiled, running Go test (`controllers/probe_swap_declined_spare_pod_test.go` in that copy) that builds a production-shaped fixture: a run with `spec.runtime.checkpoint` set, an active lease on the failing node, a spare lease whose exact slots (`node-b#0`, `node-b#1`) are squatted by a *funded* run in a different budget owner — exactly the condition that forces the swap to decline. `mirrorPods` (the branch's own fixture helper, `controllers/ledger_mirror_test.go`) gives the spare lease its real pod, matching how the sole-committer actually produces state.

**What I observed.** After `HandleNodeFailure`, the run parked `Pending` with `SwapDeclined` on the spare lease exactly as the existing test expects — but `state.Pods` still contained the spare's pod, unchanged, on `node-b`, holding 2 GPUs. I then re-admitted the run *inside* its 30-minute checkpoint grace (at +5 minutes, before the deadline), which correctly drove it to `Running` and cleared `CheckpointDeadline` via the adoption branch in `Reconcile` (`run.Status.CheckpointDeadline = nil`, guarded by "adoption already clears it"). I then reconciled 20 more simulated hours. At every single hour the spare pod was still present — `hour 20: phase=Running spare-pod=spare-pod pods=4`. Directly computing the engine's own `computeUsage` (lease-derived) for `node-b` at t+20h gave 2 GPUs, while the real pod occupancy on `node-b` was 4 GPUs (the funded squatter's 2 plus the stranded spare's 2) — the engine's ledger view of `node-b` is short by exactly the phantom 2 GPUs the finding describes, which is the precise "pod running, lease closed" lie the playbook's class 9 table names: the ledger calls a GPU free while a container still holds it, so the engine would plan new work there and kube-scheduler could never bind it. No `go test` invariant panic fired anywhere in this run (the oracle is armed under `.test` binaries and would have panicked on a caught violation) — confirming the finding's specific claim that the oracle is blind here.

**Why the oracle misses it, read from the source, not asserted.** `pkg/invariant/invariant.go:273` fires `LeaseHasPod` only `if names := openByRun[r.Key]; len(names) > 0 && r.Pods == 0` — i.e. only when a run's *entire* pod count is zero; a run still holding its 2 active pods plus the orphan spare (my repro: `pods=4`) never trips it. `TerminalNoPods` only inspects `r.Terminal` runs (Failed/Complete); the run in this scenario is legitimately `Running`, so that check never engages either. I confirmed both by reading the exact `if` conditions, not by inference.

**Why nothing else cleans it up.** `removeSparePodOnNodes` (`controllers/run_controller.go:1350`) — the only function in the file that deletes a spare pod by node — is called solely from the *accepted*-swap branch, immediately before `emitSwapPod`. The decline branch, `failGroupWithoutSpare` (`controllers/run_controller.go:1427-1441`), calls `CloseLease(spareLease, "SwapDeclined", now)` at line 1437 and nothing else touching `state.Pods`; its own comment ("Nothing else closes it: failRun only edited status, completeRun runs on success, and adoption skips terminal runs") talks exclusively about the *lease* plane and never claims anything about pods — it is silent on the exact bug that exists. `emitSparePods` (`controllers/run_controller.go:2050`) only tops spares *up* to a target count, never down, and its `consumedSpareCount` only recognizes leases closed with reason `"Swap"`, not `"SwapDeclined"`, so the stray pod is never even recognized as excess. `SettleLeases` (`controllers/settle.go:127-189`, this branch's new production sweep) only acts on runs that are terminal or whose `Run` object is gone (`controllers/settle.go:45-52` explicitly documents and refuses a per-lease/per-pod orphan rule as "REFUTED... an invariant that is wrong is not a weaker net; it is a reaper"); a `Running` run with a legitimately re-admitted gang plus one orphan spare pod is invisible to it by design.

**preExisting=true, but that does not refute it.** `git log -L1427,1466:controllers/run_controller.go` shows `closeLease(spareLease, "SwapDeclined", now)` with no pod cleanup already present at the branch's merge-base (`f243eb8`), so the underlying half-plane gap is not new. But per the playbook's own refutation rules, pre-existing is a classification, not a refutation, unless the change leaves both reachability and consequences unchanged — and this branch does not: it added `TestDecliningTheSwapReleasesTheSpareEvenWhenTheRunParksInCheckpointGrace` (new in commit `7a681c0`, this branch) whose own comment claims to close "the same leak, on the path where nothing else can catch it," yet its assertions (`controllers/node_failure_test.go:360-369`, verified by direct read: `closed, reason := closureOf(state, "spare")` ... `if closed, _ := closureOf(state, "squatter")`) touch only `state.Leases`, never `state.Pods`. The branch also shipped two new invariants and an 800-seed quiescence generator (which does include checkpoint-grace runs, per `controllers/quiescence_test.go:151-156`) specifically to catch exactly this class of two-plane disagreement, and both structurally miss this instance for the reasons above. A change whose stated purpose is "close this exact leak class" but which leaves the mirror-image of the leak wholly unaddressed and undetected by its own new oracle is a finding in its own right, independent of the pre-existing classification.

**On the obvious fix.** The correct repair is not a reaper: it is symmetric with the accepted-swap branch that already exists two lines away — call something like `c.removeSparePodOnNodes(run, leaseNodeNames(spareLease))` (or the coarser `removeRunPodsOnNodes` used by `reclaimSquatter`) inside the `if spareLease != nil` block of `failGroupWithoutSpare`, before or after `CloseLease(spareLease, "SwapDeclined", now)`. This does not touch the deliberately-exempted checkpoint-grace half-plane state for the run's *surviving active* pods (which must keep running to checkpoint, per `releaseRun`'s own doc comment) — it only removes the pod belonging to the specific spare lease that was just closed, which held no further purpose the instant the swap was declined.

An invariant that is wrong is not a\n// weaker net; it is a reaper. Ship rule 1 only.` (confirms no run-external sweep exists for this kind of orphan pod, and that the team deliberately declined to add one).

**Probe artifact.** The reproduction test lived at `/tmp/claude-1000/-workspaces-jobtree/b3df7d06-fa76-4793-98b7-31b44b11906e/scratchpad/jobtree_probe/controllers/probe_swap_declined_spare_pod_test.go` (a copy of the repo under scratchpad, not the real repo) and was executed with `go test ./controllers/... -run TestProbe_SwapDeclinedLeavesSparePodRunning -v`; it passed, and its log lines are the "CONFIRMED" quotes above. # VERDICT: NOT REFUTED — the finding is CONFIRMED by code trace and by execution.

## What I did

I traced the entire decline-the-swap path in `controllers/run_controller.go` and then, because the engine is pure, I turned the hypothesis into running Go. I copied the repo to a scratch dir and wrote four probe tests in `package controllers` (I did not touch the real repo). I ran the exact fixture the finding names, drove `Reconcile` forward twenty simulated hours, and measured both planes (`state.Leases` and `state.Pods`) plus the engine's own `computeUsage`. The claimed state reproduced.

## The trace, quoting each step

The decline path is reached from `HandleNodeFailure`'s pass 2: when the spare's slots are held by another run's funded lease (or by an unfunded lease that `reclaimSquatter` refuses to evict because a funded sibling shares the node), `crossRunConflict` is set and the code calls `c.failGroupWithoutSpare(run, runKey, lease, spareLease, nodeName, now, phases)` with a non-nil `spareLease`. Inside `failGroupWithoutSpare`, when `spareLease != nil`, the ledger plane is retired but nothing touches the workload plane:

- `controllers/run_controller.go:1437` — `CloseLease(spareLease, "SwapDeclined", now)`. That is the *only* action taken on the spare. There is no paired pod removal.

Contrast this with the **successful** swap path a few lines up, which the same authors wrote and which proves they knew the spare pod must be dropped when its lease is retired:

- `controllers/run_controller.go:1350` — `c.removeSparePodOnNodes(run, spareNodes)`, under the comment "Free the held spare's pod on the reclaimed node so the bridge deletes it." The decline path omits exactly this call. That is the class-9 half-plane action: the success path moves both planes, the decline path moves only the ledger.

After the decline, `failGroupWithoutSpare` parks the run `Pending` with a `CheckpointDeadline` (when `checkpointGrace > 0`) and returns. The only post-loop sweep in `HandleNodeFailure` is guarded to terminal runs:

- `controllers/run_controller.go:1368-1369` — `for runKey, phase := range phases {` then `if phase != RunPhaseFailed {` / `continue`. A run parked `Pending`, or one that later re-admits to `Running`, is never swept, so `releaseRun` never runs on it. The finding's claim that "the post-loop releaseRun never runs (run is not terminal)" is exactly right.

## Execution — it reproduced

Using the finding's own fixture (checkpoint grace 30m, run active on node-a, spare on node-b#0,#1, a funded squatter on the spare's exact slots), I confirmed:

- Right after `HandleNodeFailure("node-a")`: spare lease `Closed=true reason="SwapDeclined"`, squatter open, run `Pending`, and the **spare POD (2 GPUs on node-b) is still in `state.Pods`**.
- When the run recovers **inside** grace (I minted a replacement active lease + pod, the way the plugin would; `Reconcile` adopts, flips the run `Running`, and clears `CheckpointDeadline`), the orphan spare pod **survives all 20 simulated hours** — `present=true`, phase `Running`. Nothing on the Running path removes it (a non-malleable run just returns; a malleable one runs the elastic loop, neither culls a closed-lease spare pod).
- I then admitted a fresh 4-GPU run. The engine, reading `computeUsage` (node-b ledger occupancy dropped from 4 to 2 the instant the spare lease closed, while the pod kept running), planned two of the fresh run's intent pods **onto node-b** — the node where the orphan spare pod still physically sits. That is the class-9 consequence verbatim: "the ledger says a GPU is free; the engine plans new work onto an occupied GPU."

## The oracle is blind, as claimed

- `pkg/invariant/invariant.go:273` — `if names := openByRun[r.Key]; len(names) > 0 && r.Pods == 0 {` — INV-LEASE-HAS-POD fires only when a run holds an open lease and **zero** pods. Here the run holds pods, so it is silent. It also counts pods per-run in aggregate, so it can never see "a *closed* lease's pod is still running."
- `pkg/invariant/invariant.go:261` — INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS (`if r.Pods > 0`) fires only for **terminal** runs; the recovered run is `Running`, so it too is blind. No invariant catches this.

## The test asserts only one plane (playbook class 8)

`TestDecliningTheSwapReleasesTheSpareEvenWhenTheRunParksInCheckpointGrace` (`controllers/node_failure_test.go:331`) checks `closureOf(state, "spare")` is `SwapDeclined` and that the squatter is not evicted — `node_failure_test.go:364-365`: `if reason != "SwapDeclined"`. It never asserts on `state.Pods`. It is the class-9 tell: an eviction test that walks the ledger and not the workload, standing next to the bug.

## Is the obvious fix a reaper? No.

The fix is to mirror the success path on the decline branch: after `CloseLease(spareLease, "SwapDeclined", now)`, also `c.removeSparePodOnNodes(run, leaseNodeNames(spareLease))`. `removeSparePodOnNodes` filters on `LabelRunName == run.Name` and `LabelRunRole == RoleSpare` and removes at most one spare pod on the spare's own nodes — it cannot touch the squatter's containers or the active group's checkpoint-writing containers. Removing the spare pod does **not** violate the checkpoint-grace exemption: that exemption protects the surviving *active* containers so they can write a checkpoint; the spare is standby capacity with no checkpoint to write. So the fix is targeted and legal, not a reaper.

## Pre-existing? Yes, with a nuance the branch does not escape.

The `CloseLease(spareLease, "SwapDeclined")` line was introduced on `main` in commit `ae6c69e` (`git merge-base --is-ancestor` confirms it predates this branch's merge-base `f243eb8`). So the finding's stated scenario — a *funded* squatter on the spare's exact slots — is a pre-existing half-plane. Set `preExisting=true`. But two facts stop that from being a refutation: (1) this branch's whole thesis is a new oracle that catches immortal-lease / half-plane states, and both invariants it adds (INV-LEASE-HAS-POD, INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS) are structurally blind to *this* half-plane, and the new `SettleLeases` sweep only touches terminal/orphan runs so it never reaches a `Running` recovered run — the branch fails to catch a defect squarely in the class it claims to close. (2) `reclaimSquatter` does not exist at the merge-base at all (confirmed: no such function in `git show $MB:controllers/run_controller.go`); it is new on this branch, and its fail-closed `return false` opens a *second* door into the very same `failGroupWithoutSpare(..., spareLease, ...)` strand — a new caller reaching a pre-existing undischarged obligation, which the playbook explicitly counts as worsening.

## Bottom line

`refuted = false`. The trace shows the code produces the claimed state, and I reproduced it: the decline path retires the spare in the ledger plane only, the spare pod survives indefinitely on the checkpoint-grace/recovery path, no invariant catches it, the test asserts only the lease plane, and the engine over-counts free capacity and plans onto the stranded GPUs. Severity as claimed (critical half-plane, class 9) is warranted; the single-line asymmetry against `run_controller.go:1350` is the smoking gun.

Both probes pass and the classification is now settled: the `SwapDeclined` closure itself pre-dates the branch (present at the merge-base, introduced by ae6c69e "release the declined spare"), while everything around it — `releaseRun`, the pod-plane half of `reclaimSquatter`, the settle sweep, `INV-LEASE-HAS-POD` — is this branch's work on exactly this defect class.
