result.summary: The investigator examined the changed engine code under the LAST-WRITER-WINS (playbook class 3) lens, hunting for outcomes that depend on the order of state.Leases or on Go's randomized map iteration. It identified every run.Status.* write inside a loop in the diff and classified each as one of three provably order-independent shapes: a commutative severity-lattice fold in runPhaseTracker.apply used by the HandleNodeFailure pass-2 loop, failGroupWithoutSpare, and reclaimSquatter; a once-per-run map visit in applyResolution's affectedRuns loop and the post-loop phases sweep; and a build-then-sort pattern in the lender-share loop. It reviewed every `for k := range someMap` loop in the changed code and found none produces an order-dependent observable, citing settle.go's mutually-exclusive terminal/orphan rules and the sort.Slice-by-Owner determinism for the lender loop. It ran the repo's existing exhaustive 24-ordering permutation test and 64-repetition map-iteration test, both passing 20 consecutive times, and additionally performed a mutation experiment: it archived the tree to scratch, mutated reclaimSquatter's phases.apply fold into a bare direct assignment to victim.Status.Phase, and confirmed the test went RED with the run reporting different phases (Pending vs Failed) across different lease orderings, matching the class-3 divergence pattern. Based on this, the investigator concluded that reclaimSquatter's use of the phases tracker (described as the fix for a previously-open task #50) is a genuine fix, that the branch already uses the correct commutative-fold shape rather than a coincidental deterministic sort, and reported no class-3 (last-writer-wins) defect found in the branch. No findings were reported.

The investigator reviewed the whole branch (git diff main...HEAD) through the SIGNAL != REALITY (class 4), IDENTITY COARSENING (class 5), and GUARD BEFORE PREDICATE (class 6) lenses, reading run_controller.go (HandleNodeFailure pass 1/2, reclaimSquatter, openRunLeasesOnNodes, removeRunPodsOnNodes, nodesOfSlots, leaseGroupIndex), kube/reconcilers.go (fenced/nodeFailed/nodeReady, the double fenced call, the ErrNoLeaseOnNode swallow), kube/bridge.go (WithWorld + SettleLeases + load ordering), settle.go, admission.go, resolver.go, plugin.go, and the shipped RBAC. Because the engine is pure the investigator did not speculate: they copied the repo to a scratch dir and ran four mutation tests, each of which reverted one load-bearing line and went RED, proving the protection is not decorative. (1) reclaimSquatter's fail-closed return false is pinned by TestSwapDeclinesWhenTheSquattersFundedWorkSharesTheMachine; (2) pass-1's close-the-spare-on-the-failed-node guard (R25) is pinned by TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode and TestSwapNeverTargetsASpareOnTheFailedNode; (3) the R22 slot-vs-node granularity in the pass-2 conflict loop is pinned by TestSwapLeavesACoLocatedRunOnOtherSlotsAlone; (4) the #36 fencing verdict re-read UNDER the bridge lock is pinned by TestStaleFencingVerdictDoesNotCloseARecreatedNodesLeases (which runs without envtest). The investigator found NO new class-4/5/6 defect: signals are read only as fencing assertions with no clock, identity comparisons use the finest key (run key + node#ordinal slot for conflicts, run key + group for spares), coarsening to machine granularity is always guarded by a same-run fail-closed funding scan, guard-before-predicate ordering sets handled in every pass, and every swallow site uses a typed sentinel via errors.Is with no error-text coupling in production control flow.

answers.question: Does the change read any Kubernetes condition, taint, timestamp, or object-absence and treat it as a fact about what is PHYSICALLY running on a machine? For each: if that inference is wrong, WHAT RUNS TWICE?
answers.conclusion: The only physical-reality inference the change makes is nodeFailed()/fenced() and it is deliberately narrowed to a FENCING assertion: the Node object is absent (IsNotFound => fenced) OR carries node.kubernetes.io/out-of-service. It reads NEITHER conditions, NotReady, cordon (Unschedulable), NOR any timestamp — both triggers force Pod GC to grade-0 delete, so the rank is provably stopped before a swap starts a second copy. NotReady/cordon explicitly return not-fenced (a cordoned pod keeps running; NotReady means the control plane cannot hear the kubelet). So nothing runs twice: the swap that would create two live training ranks only fires on a real fence, and the previously-racy verdict is re-taken under the bridge lock so a node recreated while the reconcile waited on the mutex is not treated as failed. Mutation-confirmed: removing the inside-lock re-read reds TestStaleFencingVerdictDoesNotCloseARecreatedNodesLeases. No new signal-as-reality defect.

answers.question: For each such signal: WHO can write that field, and can they lie? Check the shipped RBAC in deploy/helm/gpu-fleet/templates/rbac.yaml.
answers.conclusion: nodeFailed takes NO clock/timestamp, which removes the class's classic lie (a compromised kubelet backdating LastTransitionTime to manufacture a failure) — there is no time field to forge. The two inputs are node deletion and the out-of-service taint, applied by a human/fencing agent or the cloud-controller-manager, and both cause force-deletion so the assertion is self-validating. The shipped RBAC (rbac.yaml) grants the controller only nodes:[get,list,watch] and NO node delete/patch/update, so jobtree itself cannot manufacture a fence; it can only observe one. The controller's own reads use the UNCACHED APIReader (Bridge doc), so it reads etcd truth, not a possibly-stale informer. This is the shipped R21/#36 design, not new, and the investigator found no new trust hole introduced by the branch.

answers.question: Does the change compare a COARSER key than the one that matters? Find every equality test on an identity and name the finest key that should have been used.
answers.conclusion: Every conflict/equality test uses the finest key that matters. Cross-run swap conflict is decided at node#ordinal SLOT granularity via leaseOccupiesSlots(other, buildSlotSet(spare.Nodes)) — NOT machine — so run B sharing a machine on different GPUs is left alone (mutation to node granularity reds TestSwapLeavesACoLocatedRunOnOtherSlotsAlone). Run identity everywhere is keys.NamespacedKey(namespace,name), never bare name. Spare<->active pairing uses run key + group index (findSpareLease filters runKey then leaseGroupIndex), and group index is only ever compared within a run, never across runs. The ONE deliberate coarsening — reclaimSquatter/removeRunPodsOnNodes evict at MACHINE granularity via nodesOfSlots, because a PodManifest names a node not a slot — is fully guarded: openRunLeasesOnNodes returns ALL the victim run's open leases on those machines and the fail-closed loop declines the swap if ANY is not Unfunded, so no funded co-located lease of the same run is silently evicted (mutation to fail-open reds TestSwapDeclinesWhenTheSquattersFundedWorkSharesTheMachine). No coarser-than-needed comparison found.

answers.question: Does the change contain a loop with BOTH a type/role filter (if ... continue) AND a match predicate? Check their ORDER.
answers.conclusion: Yes, and the ordering is correct in every case — this is exactly the R25 shape and it has been fixed. HandleNodeFailure is now split into pass 1 (role==Spare, then node match, closes the spare, sets handled=true) and pass 2 (role!=Spare, then node match, sets handled=true), so a node holding ONLY a spare is handled in pass 1 and no longer falls through to a false 'no lease found'. handled is set on every path that touched a lease, so the only route to ErrNoLeaseOnNode is a genuinely lease-free node. The inner pass-2 conflict loop's index-skip guard (j==spareIdx||j==i) precedes the occupancy predicate but only excludes the two leases already being processed (the swap target and the failed active), never a same-run stale lease that must be closed (that is handled by the otherKey==runKey branch). Mutation disabling pass 1 reds TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode and TestSwapNeverTargetsASpareOnTheFailedNode. No new guard-before-predicate defect.

answers.question: Grep the changed code and its callers for strings.Contains(err.Error(), ...) or any error compared by TEXT.
answers.conclusion: No production control-flow decision in the changed code compares an error by text. The R25 swallow that used to string-match 'no active lease found' is now errors.Is(err, controllers.ErrNoLeaseOnNode) against a typed sentinel (var ErrNoLeaseOnNode = errors.New(...), returned via fmt.Errorf("%w: ...")). The new plugin path adds another typed sentinel, ErrNoPlacementGroup, wrapped with %w and turned into a fail-closed PreBind Error (never swallowed). The only strings.Contains(err.Error(),...) hits are in _test.go files (webhook_test, binder_regression_test, reservation_semantics_test) asserting messages, not swallowing; the only production .Error() uses set run.Status.Message / a status string for surfacing, not branching. So every swallow site swallows exactly one typed sentinel. Clean.

result.summary: All suites green. The investigator hunted for immortal leases (class 1) and cloned obligations (class 7) across the whole branch (git diff main...HEAD), reading the playbook, history-run-phase-writers.md, settle.go, invariants.go, the full run_controller.go diff, resolver.go, bridge.go, and the new test files. They traced every exit path of every lease-touching function the diff adds or modifies (completeRun, failRun, applyResolution, HandleNodeFailure's two passes and post-loop sweep, failGroupWithoutSpare, reclaimSquatter, releaseRun, removeAllRunPods, removeRunPodsOnNodes, openRunLeasesOnNodes, SettleLeases, CloseLease). Every terminal-phase assignment is paired with a whole-run closure (releaseRun or the post-loop sweep keyed on the runPhaseTracker), and the sole-closer rail (CloseLease + hack/antifake/soleclose.go, zero allowlist) holds: CloseLease is the ONLY non-test writer of Lease.Status.{Closed,Ended,ClosureReason} in the tree under review. They built an external test module in the scratchpad that imports the package, confirmed the invariant oracle is genuinely live there (a Completed run holding an open lease panics INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT/NO-PODS), then drove the most suspicious surviving path -- reclaimSquatter demoting an unfunded malleable victim to Pending while it retains a lease on an untouched node -- through HandleNodeFailure plus 20 Reconciles over 20 simulated hours: no lease leaked, no invariant fired, the leftover lease stayed backed by its pod. They found NO immortal lease introduced by this branch. They report one low-severity class-7 durability observation: groupIndexForPodIndex/groupSizesFor is a newly-introduced second implementation of pack.deriveGroups whose only drift detector is an example-based pin test (five tuples), not a property/exhaustive check.

answers.question: Enumerate EVERY exit path (return, continue, break, panic, early error) of every function this change adds or modifies. For each, list which leases are open at that moment and which are closed. Name any path that exits with a lease open that should have been closed.
answers.conclusion: reclaimSquatter has four exits: (a) run_controller.go:1535 `return false` fires from the fail-closed loop BEFORE the closing loop at 1539, so NO victim lease is closed -- correct, the caller then declines the swap; there is provably no partial-closure path between the scan and the closes; (b) 1547 `return true` (victim Run gone) closes the on-node doomed leases and leaves off-node leases for SettleLeases' orphan-run rule; (c) 1559 `return true` (still >= min) and (d) 1573 `return true` (demoted Pending) both close on-node doomed leases AND remove on-node pods, leaving only off-node leases that still back live containers. releaseRun (single loop, then removeAllRunPods) always exits with every run lease closed and every pod dropped. failGroupWithoutSpare closes lease (+spare) then either parks Pending in checkpoint grace (leases of surviving groups deliberately open with running pods) or writes Failed via the tracker, whose run is swept by the post-loop loop at 1368-1380. completeRun/failRun/applyResolution-default each call releaseRun. No exit leaves a lease open that should be closed.

answers.question: Does the change add a new CALLER to an existing function whose lease-closing behaviour was only correct for its old callers? failRun once carried the comment "It never holds leases at this point". Check every function the diff newly calls.
answers.conclusion: releaseRun (the renamed/expanded closeRunLeases) gains three new call sites: completeRun (534), applyResolution's Failed default (1888), and it replaces closeRunLeases in failRun (694) and the HandleNodeFailure post-loop sweep (1376). releaseRun now ALSO removes every pod (removeAllRunPods, 1668), so its correctness depends on the caller having a TERMINAL run. All five callers are terminal: completeRun sets Complete, failRun sets Failed, applyResolution-default sets Failed, the post-loop sweep only fires for tracker phase==Failed. failRun's four callers (checkpoint-grace expiry 168, follow-cycle 566/587/597) are all hard-fail transitions where dropping all pods is correct; none is the checkpoint-grace-keep path (that goes through failGroupWithoutSpare, which never calls releaseRun). No new caller inherits a stale precondition.

answers.question: Does the change set a run to a terminal phase (Failed / Completed) anywhere? For each such site, prove that every lease referencing that run is closed on the same path or by a sweep that provably runs. Cite the sweep.
answers.conclusion: Four terminal-phase sites, each with proven closure on the same call: completeRun (535 Complete) calls releaseRun at 534 first; failRun (688 Failed) calls releaseRun at 694; applyResolution default (1876 Failed) calls releaseRun at 1888; failGroupWithoutSpare (1458, Failed via phases.apply) is NOT closed inline -- deliberately -- but the run is closed by HandleNodeFailure's post-loop sweep `for runKey, phase := range phases { if phase != RunPhaseFailed { continue } ... c.releaseRun(run,...) }` (1368-1380), which runs unconditionally after the lease loop; every Failed writer in HandleNodeFailure goes through phases.apply so every Failed run is in the tracker. releaseRun scans ALL of c.State.Leases for the run key (not a scoped subset), so out-of-scope spares are caught. The deferred checkInvariants at each entry point asserts INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT on return; the positive-control test confirmed it panics on a terminal run with an open lease. Production adds a second backstop: Bridge.WithWorld calls SettleLeases (settle.go:127) which closes any open lease whose run is terminal or gone.

answers.question: Does the change write Lease.Status.Closed anywhere other than closeLease()? Every clone of the closing obligation drifts. Name each site and say what closeLease does that the clone does not (events, metrics, ClosureReason).
answers.conclusion: No. A repo-wide grep for `Status.Closed =`/`Status.Ended =`/`Status.ClosureReason =` outside _test files finds exactly one write site in the tree under review: the body of CloseLease itself (run_controller.go:3032-3035). The previous clone -- applyResolution's inline `lease.Status.Closed = true; lease.Status.Ended = &ended; lease.Status.ClosureReason = action.Reason` -- was migrated to `CloseLease(lease, action.Reason, now)` at 1795 in this diff. hack/antifake/soleclose.go enforces this with a zero allowlist ("The permitted count is zero, forever"). CloseLease also carries the idempotency guard (`if lease.Status.Closed { return }`) so first-closure wins; settle.go and applyResolution reach it, so ClosureReason/Ended stamping is uniform. The only other `Status.Closed`/`ClosureReason` occurrences in controllers are READS at invariants.go:106 and run_controller.go:2115, not writes. Stale copies under .claude/worktrees/ are other worktrees, not part of this module's sources.

answers.question: Take the single most suspicious path you found. Write a Go test that reaches it, drive Reconcile ~20 times over ~20 simulated hours, and assert the lease is closed. Report the actual test output. If it stays open you have a critical finding; if it closes, say what closed it and cite that code.
answers.conclusion: Most suspicious path: reclaimSquatter demoting an unfunded malleable victim to Pending (run_controller.go:1563) while it keeps an open lease on a node the swap never touched -- does that leftover lease leak across reconciles? The investigator built an external module (scratchpad/leasehunt) so os.Args[0] ends in `.test` and the oracle is live (confirmed: a Completed run holding an open lease PANICS INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT/NO-PODS). Fixture: run `filler` (unfunded, Malleable MinTotalGPUs=4) holds `squat` on the spare's slots (node-b) and `keep` on node-c. After HandleNodeFailure(node-a): output `filler.phase="Pending" squat=true(ReclaimedBySpare) keep=false open=[keep] pods=[filler-keep]` -- squat closed in both planes, keep still backed by its pod. After 20 Reconciles over 20h: `filler.phase="Pending" open=[keep] pods=[filler-keep,filler-active-0..3]` -- NO panic, no leak; keep stays open because it backs a live container, and the extra pods are the legal awaiting-mint window (pods ahead of leases, which INV-LEASE-HAS-POD exempts). Nothing closed keep because nothing should: it is not an immortal lease. Conclusion: the path is sound. reclaimSquatter closes exactly the on-node doomed leases (1539-1541) and removes exactly their pods (1549), keeping the two planes consistent.

answers.question: Are the two new invariants (INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS, INV-LEASE-HAS-POD) reapers, and is the cloned-obligation surface guarded? (self-posed, within assigned lens)
answers.conclusion: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS is safe: it only fires for terminal runs, a state the swap window and checkpoint-grace never leave a run in. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is deliberately COARSE (open lease + zero pods for the whole run) and self-documents that per-lease correspondence is deferred to R26's auditor; it is exempted only where a run has any pod, so it is not a reaper but has a known blind spot (a run holding several leases where only one loses its container). The class-7 surface: CloseLease is unified (sole-closer rail); leaseGroupIndex exists in both controllers and pkg/resolver and both now return "" (no "0" default), kept in sync manually and backstopped by INV-GROUP-STAMPED; groupIndexForPodIndex/groupSizesFor is a NEW clone of pack.deriveGroups whose only drift detector is the five-tuple pin test -- adequate today, thin against a future bucketing change.

findings.title: groupIndexForPodIndex/groupSizesFor is a second implementation of pack.deriveGroups guarded only by an example-based pin test
findings.failure_scenario: If pack.deriveGroups' group layout is ever changed for a shape not in the pin's set {8/0, 8/4, 9/4, 12/4/2, 64/32} -- e.g. a change affecting total sizes divisible in a way none of those tuples exercise -- the spec-only clone used by topUpActiveGang re-emits a lost rank with a group index the packer never chose. INV-GROUP-STAMPED still passes (the label is present, just wrong), so the resolver's per-group lottery, the elastic shrink loop, and the node-failure spare pairing all silently address the wrong ranks, with nothing red.
findings.taxonomyClass: 7 CLONED OBLIGATION

result.summary: The investigator reviewed a controllers-package branch diff against docs/project/quota-semantics.md and docs/concepts/leases.md, read all new/modified tests, and ran six deliberate mutations (each reverting one load-bearing line the change adds) in a scratch copy of the working tree to confirm the tests actually fail without the fix. All six mutations went red. The investigator also verified that a suspicious fixture change (TotalGPUs from 4 to 2 in TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode) was a correction rather than a narrowing-to-hide-a-bug, by showing that restoring the old value of 4 causes the invariant oracle to panic on INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED. The investigator confirmed the quiescence generator is non-vacuous across 40 short seeds, and confirmed all test stimuli (node failures via deletion/NotFound, never cordon) are legitimate per the binding docs, avoiding the R21 trap. The single finding raised is a low-severity, class-8 coverage gap: unit tests calling controllers/run_controller.go's applyResolution directly bypass the invariant oracle because applyResolution is not one of the three entry points (Reconcile, ActivateReservations, HandleNodeFailure) that install the deferred checkInvariants check, so those tests rely solely on their own assertions rather than oracle backstop — though the investigator notes this is not a code defect, since the shipped code is correct and the assertions are load-bearing (confirmed via mutation F), and the production path plus quiescence integration do cover applyResolution via ActivateReservations.

answers.question: For each test the change adds or modifies: what is the STIMULUS and is it legitimate?
answers.conclusion: Every stimulus is legitimate and correctly interpreted. Node-failure tests provoke via a genuinely fenced node: node_recreated_test uses apierrors.NewNotFound (deleted) / an absent Node object; the pure engine tests call HandleNodeFailure(name) after the node is gone; the quiescence driver's deleteNode removes the Node from state.Nodes and explicitly notes 'A cordon is NOT a failure and cannot be modelled here.' This is exactly the R21-correct signal — deletion/out-of-service, never cordon or NotReady. Reclaim tests provoke a squatter by minting an Unfunded lease on the spare's exact node#ordinal slots (funding derived, not stored). Sweep tests provoke by setting a run terminal or deleting its Run object. R28b tests provoke via emitIntentPods/mintGroupIndex, the real committer path. Budget tightening in quiescence models an admin shrinking an envelope. No R21-style invalid-stimulus (cordon-as-failure) test exists.

answers.question: Is each new assertion's asserted state actually DESIRABLE per the binding docs?
answers.conclusion: The asserted end-states are desirable per the binding docs. TestReclaimedSquatterIsEvictedInBothPlanes asserts filler→Pending (demote-not-kill, quota-semantics R14). TestReclaimingASquatterNeverLeavesARunningRunWithNoContainers asserts a malleable run above its MinTotalGPUs stays Running. TestSwapDeclines... asserts a FUNDED sibling is never evicted by a swap and the uncovered covered-run Fails — matching leases.md's SwapDeclined and playbook class 9's legal-exception rules. The closure reasons asserted all match leases.md section 1a. TestSwapDeclinesRatherThanEvictAFundedRunOnTheSpareSlots inverts the canonical class-8 specimen: it asserts the declined spare must be RELEASED, quoting that an earlier version asserted the opposite ('the spare must not be consumed') — the corruption is now the negative control, not the spec.

answers.question: MUTATE THE FIX: does deleting each load-bearing line cause covering tests to fail?
answers.conclusion: Six mutations in a scratch tree (a cp of the working tree; the repo was never edited), each reverting one load-bearing line, and running the covering tests. ALL went red. (A) removed c.removeRunPodsOnNodes(victimKey, nodes) in reclaimSquatter → 4 fails (two_plane_test.go:126/:180, resolver_settlement_test.go:345/:397). (B) removed c.removeAllRunPods(run) in releaseRun → PANIC INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS at HandleNodeFailure. (C) replaced reclaimSquatter's phases.apply(victim,victimKey,RunPhasePending,msg) with a direct victim.Status.Phase=RunPhasePending → TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder failed showing filler=Failed for one ordering and Pending for another. (D) neutered SettleLeases' pod drop → settle_test.go:52/:78 failed ('the container outlived the lease'). (E) removed reclaimSquatter's fail-closed guard `if !classified || class != funding.ClassUnfunded { return false }` → two_plane_test.go:233 failed ('the swap evicted FUNDED work... 0 pods survive'). (F) removed applyResolution's terminal c.releaseRun(run,"RunFailed",now) → resolver_settlement_test.go:111 failed ('the spare of a run the resolver ENDED is still open'). No test passed against a reverted fix, so none is decorative on the lines tested.

answers.question: Does any test assert on a state the invariant oracle would reject as illegal?
answers.conclusion: No test asserts an illegal end-state. The full controllers package passes with -count=1 while the oracle is in PANIC mode and installed on Reconcile/ActivateReservations/HandleNodeFailure, so any illegal state at those entry-point returns would have panicked. The pkg/invariant unit tests deliberately build illegal Worlds, but only to confirm CheckSteady/CheckTransition catch them. settle_test builds terminal-run-with-open-lease fixtures and calls SettleLeases directly, but the assertion is that SettleLeases REPAIRS them, and the post-sweep state is legal. The caveat: tests that call c.applyResolution directly bypass the oracle because applyResolution is not an installed entry point (see finding). Separately, restoring TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode to TotalGPUs=4 panics on INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED ('holding 2 of 4 minimum runnable GPUs'), proving the old fixture encoded an illegal state.

answers.question: Did the change modify or delete an existing test, and were the modifications corrections or corruptions?
answers.conclusion: The modifications are corrections, not corruptions. node_failure_test.go: TestNodeFailureClosesASpareOnlyNode changed Runs from nfRun(...,4,...) to nfRun(...,2,...) and TestNodeFailureReturnsTypedSentinel... from 4 to 1; the investigator proved the OLD width was an illegal state (INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED panics), and the R25 spare-only-node-closes behaviour is unchanged, so no legitimate coverage was dropped. mirrorPods was inserted into 12 lease-only fixtures across gang_width_test/run_controller_test/node_failure_test; mutation B proved this makes the node-failure tests STRONGER, and mirrorPods models the sole committer correctly. The R28b label additions and the closeLease→CloseLease rename conform to the sole-closer refactor. The two golden JSONs changed only group ('0'→'1', the R28b correctness fix) and nodeName; both were regenerated via UPDATE_GOLDEN from commit e681a96 (R28b), a re-derivation after an intended behaviour change.

findings.title: Unit tests calling applyResolution directly bypass the invariant oracle backstop
findings.failure_scenario: In mutation F the investigator removed applyResolution's terminal c.releaseRun(run,"RunFailed",now); the resulting state (a Failed run holding an open out-of-scope spare) is exactly INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT, yet TestResolverEndingARunReleasesTheLeasesItStillHolds did NOT panic inside the call — it was caught only by its own line-111 assertion. If a future edit both reintroduces an illegal-state bug in applyResolution AND weakens one of these isolated assertions, the unit test would stay green; only the ActivateReservations entry point and the quiescence integration (both of which DO run the oracle) would catch it. This is a partial hole in the 'green means asserted-and-legal' guarantee for precisely the resolver/Scope path where class-1's 'third door' lived. Not a code defect — the shipped code is correct and the assertions are load-bearing — but a review-worthy gap in the safety net.

unsupported: taxonomyClass note field from investigator output (not a real answer/finding, just meta-commentary) was omitted from answers array

result.summary: Investigator's task: audit two invariants added on this branch (INV-LEASE-HAS-POD, INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS). Investigator states they read the playbook, AGENTS.md, and history-run-phase-writers.md in full, then audited the invariants by reading pkg/invariant/invariant.go, controllers/invariants.go, settle.go, run_controller.go, kube/bridge.go, kube/reconcilers.go, and the new test files, and by building a compiled reproduction module in the scratchpad (replace-directive module importing the repo; no repo files touched) that drives the real engine entry points. Conclusion stated: BOTH INVARIANTS ARE REFUTED BY LEGAL STATES, confirmed by running tests. INV-LEASE-HAS-POD: a routine kubectl drain (or pod GC of a Succeeded gang) removes a pod object while its lease legally stays open, and the oracle panics at the return of every Reconcile forever (reproduced: 20/20 simulated hours). INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS: pod deletion in Kubernetes is asynchronous and graceful, so the pass after every ordinary completion is branded illegal (reproduced, including an innocent bystander run's reconcile panicking about its neighbour). The 800-seed quiescence driver stays green only because its world model forbids pod deletion while modelling node deletion by the same actor. Secondary results reported: every terminal-phase writer discharges both planes in-state via releaseRun; the KnownToLedger change is semantically identical to the old anyPod; counting spare pods weakens LeaseHasPod (executed); mirrorPods leaves AwaitingMint false exactly as zero pods did (executed both directions). The correct fix shape proposed is to move both rules to the transition tier.

answers.question: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD says: a run holding >=1 open lease holds >=1 pod. Find a LEGAL engine state that refutes it. Consider at least: a pod evicted or deleted out from under an open lease; the moment between emitSwapPod deleting a consumed spare pod and the plugin minting the swap lease; removeSparePodOnNodes; a Run whose pods all reached Succeeded but whose leases Reconcile has not yet closed; growRun and shrinkRun; adoption after a controller restart. For each, say whether state.Pods is empty for that run at the RETURN of an engine entry point. Build the state in a Go test and run it.
answers.conclusion: REFUTED, executed. (a) Pod deleted out from under an open lease: kubectl delete pod, a drain (eviction API), or pod GC removes the OBJECT (kubelet node-pressure eviction does NOT). After object removal, state.Pods is empty for the run at the return of EVERY entry point, forever: the pod watch drops deletes (reconcilers.go:153), Reconcile of a Running run returns at the phase check, R21 forbids closing the lease without a fencing assertion, and SettleLeases only sweeps terminal/orphan runs. Reproduced: TestLeaseHasPodFiresOnADrainedNode panics INV-LEASE-HAS-POD and keeps firing 20/20 simulated hours. (b) The swap window: NOT a refutation — removeSparePodOnNodes and emitSwapPod run in the same pass, so at return the swap pod is present. (c) All pods Succeeded, leases not yet closed: objects still exist, Pods>0, no fire; but if pod GC collects the Succeeded objects BEFORE that reconcile, runGangComplete's sawActive stays false and the invariant fires forever on a wedged-by-design state — reproduced in TestLeaseHasPodFiresWhenPodGCCollectsASucceededGang. (d) growRun/shrinkRun: no fire at return. (e) Adoption after restart: empty pods requires external deletion (case a), in which case adoption flips Running on a containerless gang and the oracle fires on the adoption pass itself. Verdict: the invariant is a claim about the WORLD, not an engine postcondition, and the engine has no repair path; it fires every runningRunResync (5 min) forever in production.

answers.question: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS says: a terminal (Failed/Completed) run has zero pods. Enumerate EVERY writer of run.Status.Phase = Failed or Completed in the tree. For each, prove the pods are dropped on the same path, or find one that is not. Pay attention to failGroupWithoutSpare, the checkpoint-grace expiry in Reconcile, applyResolution's terminal branch, and anything in controllers/kube.
answers.conclusion: All terminal writers discharge both planes in-state; the refutation is between passes, not in any writer. Live-tree terminal writers: (1) completeRun run_controller.go:535 — releaseRun(:534) closes leases and removeAllRunPods drops every pod before the phase write. (2) failRun :688 — releaseRun at :694, covering the checkpoint-grace expiry (:168), follow cycle (:566), upstream-failed (:587), follow-grace-expired (:597). (3) failGroupWithoutSpare via phases.apply :1458 — pods dropped by HandleNodeFailure's post-loop sweep (:1368-1380) calling releaseRun for every tracker entry that ended Failed, even when apply() returned false. (4) applyResolution's default branch :1876 — releaseRun :1888. Nothing in controllers/kube writes run.Status.Phase. No writer shirks — and the invariant is STILL refuted: pod deletion is asynchronous (graceful, default 30s), the lease closures re-trigger the run reconciler via the Lease watch (reconcilers.go:177), and Bridge.load reloads the terminating pod unfiltered (bridge.go:190; PodManifest has no DeletionTimestamp field). Reproduced: TestTerminalNoPodsFiresDuringOrdinaryGracefulDeletion — pass N completes legally, pass N+1 panics. Every ordinary completion and terminal failure in a real cluster produces this window; envtest hides it only because unbound pods delete non-gracefully.

answers.question: The oracle's projection lives in controllers/invariants.go:snapshotWorld(). It now computes Pods per run by counting EVERY pod of EVERY role, and KnownToLedger changed from anyLease || anyPod to anyLease || podsOfRun[key] > 0. Are those the same? Does counting SPARE pods toward Run.Pods make INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS or INV-LEASE-HAS-POD fire on a legal state that the previous projection tolerated?
answers.conclusion: The KnownToLedger change is EXACTLY the same predicate: the branch diff (7a681c0..HEAD) shows old anyPod was built by the identical loop, so anyPod[key]==true iff podsOfRun[key]>0. The Pods field itself is genuinely new; both invariants that read it were added in the same commit (4e832a7), so there is no 'previous projection' that tolerated a state these now reject. Counting spare pods cuts both ways: for INV-LEASE-HAS-POD it can only SATISFY the check, never trigger it — masking the exact defect class the invariant was written for (executed: TestASurvivingSparePodMasksAWholeActivePodSetDeletion, no panic on an open active lease with zero active pods). For INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS, spare pods widen the trigger, but the only legal state found where that fires is the same async-deletion window any role's pod produces. One footnote-grade half-plane: HandleNodeFailure pass 1 (run_controller.go:1194-1212) closes a spare lease without removing its spare pod from state.Pods — no invariant fires, but the quiescence driver's model retains such pods forever. Also, the projection counts Succeeded/Failed pods as 'holding a GPU', which is factually wrong since a terminal-phase pod's resources are freed.

answers.question: AwaitingMint = activePods[key] > openActiveLeases[key] gates INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED. mirrorPods() adds exactly one pod per open lease. Does that make AwaitingMint FALSE in fixtures where it was previously true — and if so, has mirrorPods silently ENABLED a width check that those twelve tests were never written to satisfy, or silently DISABLED one? Which twelve tests, and what changed for each?
answers.conclusion: Neither enabled nor disabled — AwaitingMint was FALSE before mirrorPods and stays FALSE after. With zero pods, 0 > N is false for any N; with one pod per open lease, mirrored pods carry the lease's ROLE so spare-lease mirrors are excluded from activePods exactly as spare leases are from openActiveLeases, leaving the two counts equal — still false. The width check was armed on all twelve fixtures from the moment the oracle landed (7a681c0), via KnownToLedger=anyLease. Confirmed both directions (TestOnePodPerLeaseKeepsTheWidthInvariantArmed): one pod per lease keeps INV-WIDTH-ASSEMBLED firing on an under-width Running run; a second unminted pod disarms it. The twelve tests: gang_width_test.go:181, :214, :239, :267; run_controller_test.go:974, :1049; node_failure_test.go:92, :123, :154, :190, :297, :351. For each: the fixture became INV-LEASE-HAS-POD-legal, and the pod-deleting halves of the paths under test now run against non-empty slices, though most of the twelve still assert only on leases. Residual weakness: mirrorPods places the pod on Slice.Nodes[0] only, which would under-represent a future multi-node-lease fixture's pod set.

answers.question: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is checked for terminal runs too (the loop runs it before the if r.Terminal { continue }? read the code and say exactly). Is a terminal run with open leases and no pods reported once or twice, and does the ordering of the checks in CheckSteady change which violation an author sees first?
answers.conclusion: It is NOT checked for terminal runs. In CheckSteady (pkg/invariant/invariant.go:248-283) the terminal block runs first — TerminalPresent (:250), then TerminalNoPods (:261), then continue (:271) — and the LeaseHasPod check sits after it (:273), so a terminal run never reaches it. Executed: a Failed run with one open lease and zero pods yields exactly [INV-TERMINAL-PRESENT] — reported ONCE (TerminalNoPods cannot also fire because Pods==0 is its pass condition). Within one run the append order is fixed: TerminalPresent before TerminalNoPods before (never) LeaseHasPod. Across runs, snapshotWorld ranges the c.State.Runs MAP (controllers/invariants.go:87), so the order of Runs in the World — and hence which run's violation an author sees FIRST — is randomized per process; cosmetic rather than semantic, but worth knowing when diffing failure output.

answers.question: Take the single best refutation you found. Write the Go test, run it, and paste the real output. If you cannot refute either invariant, say so plainly and name the strongest legal state you tried.
answers.conclusion: Best refutation: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD versus a drained node (TestLeaseHasPodFiresOnADrainedNode, scratchpad module invrefute, run against the real engine via a replace directive). Real output quoted: 'INVARIANT VIOLATION ... site: RunController.Reconcile | INV-LEASE-HAS-POD: run default/train: holds 1 open lease(s) (train-active-0-lease) and not one pod — the ledger is billing a budget and reserving GPUs for containers that do not exist | This state is illegal. If a test asserts it, the TEST is wrong: see docs/project/adversarial-review-playbook.md, class 8.' followed by 'after 20 simulated hours: the violation fired on 20 of 20 reconciles; the lease is still open, the run still Running' — PASS: TestLeaseHasPodFiresOnADrainedNode. The companion refutation for INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS also ran red as predicted, with an unrelated healthy run's reconcile also turning red (TestTerminalNoPodsCondemnsAnUnrelatedRunsReconcile).

findings.title: INV-LEASE-HAS-POD is refuted: a drained (or GC'd) pod under a legally-open lease violates it forever on a correct engine
findings.failure_scenario: kubectl drain / delete pod / pod GC of a Succeeded gang removes the pod object; per R21 no fencing assertion exists so the engine correctly closes nothing; pod delete events are filtered (reconcilers.go:153); SettleLeases only touches terminal/orphan runs. The violation fires at the return of every entry point forever (reproduced 20/20 simulated hours) — in CI it brands any faithful drain fixture illegal, in production it fires every 5-minute Running resync against an alert whose contract is 'any nonzero value is a bug'. Fix shape: move to the transition tier — fire only when the ENGINE's own pass removed the run's last pod while leaving a lease open, which still catches the reclaimSquatter whole-pod-set deletion it was built for.
findings.taxonomyClass: 4 SIGNAL != REALITY (absence of a pod OBJECT read as an engine-shirked obligation); the 'wrong invariant is a reaper' failure mode pkg/invariant's own package doc names

findings.title: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS is refuted: Kubernetes' asynchronous graceful pod deletion makes the pass after EVERY ordinary completion or terminal failure illegal
findings.failure_scenario: completeRun/releaseRun retire both planes correctly; Bridge.apply issues the delete; the lease-closure events deterministically re-trigger the run reconciler (Lease watch, reconcilers.go:177) inside the termination window; Bridge.load reloads the terminating pod with no DeletionTimestamp/phase filter and binder.PodManifest cannot carry one. Reproduced: pass N legal, pass N+1 panics; an unrelated healthy run's reconcile also panics about the neighbour because the oracle checks the whole world. Envtest is blind to this only because unbound pods short-circuit graceful deletion. Fix shape: fire only for runs that went terminal in THIS pass (transition tier), and/or exclude Succeeded/Failed pods from podsOfRun.
findings.taxonomyClass: 4 SIGNAL != REALITY (a terminating or Succeeded pod object counted as 'holding a GPU the ledger handed back' — false for terminal-phase pods, whose resources are freed)

findings.title: Counting every role in Run.Pods lets one surviving SPARE pod mask the whole-active-pod-set deletion INV-LEASE-HAS-POD was written to catch
findings.failure_scenario: Executed: a Running run with an open ACTIVE lease, zero active pods, and one spare pod raises nothing (TestASurvivingSparePodMasksAWholeActivePodSetDeletion). The coarse per-run form is documented as deliberate (per-lease correspondence deferred to R26's ledger auditor), but the masking direction is not documented; worth a line in the package doc or a role-aware refinement when R26 lands.
findings.taxonomyClass: 8-adjacent weakness (the rail passes states containing the defect class it exists to detect)

findings.title: The quiescence driver's 'legal world' model excludes pod deletion entirely — the only reason 800 seeds stay green under these two invariants
findings.failure_scenario: quiescence_test.go:53 declares 'Nothing in this file ... deletes a pod' as fidelity, yet the same file models kube deleting NODES (deleteNode), and real kube deletes pods routinely (drain, eviction API, pod GC — which force-deletes the pods of the very nodes deleteNode kills, another divergence: the driver retains a dead node's pods forever). Adding either legal event to the driver panics it immediately via the LeaseHasPod/TerminalNoPods refutations. Whatever fix the invariants get, the driver should gain a pod-deletion event so the fixed forms are exercised against it.
findings.taxonomyClass: 8 THE TEST ASSERTS THE BUG, generator form: the rail's legality model is exactly the invariants' blind spot

result.summary: Adversarial review of branch fix/r27-invariant-oracle (f243eb8..HEAD, 47 files) under the half-plane/reaper lens. The investigator read the playbook, AGENTS.md, history-run-phase-writers.md, the full diff of controllers/run_controller.go, settle.go, invariants.go, pkg/invariant, kube/bridge.go, kube/reconcilers.go, and the new test files, enumerated every CloseLease site and every state.Pods mutation and paired them plane-by-plane, then compiled and ran three reproductions in a scratchpad module against the real tree (never editing the repo). Five findings resulted: F1 an externally-deleted pod leaves its Running run's lease immortal with no closer anywhere and INV-LEASE-HAS-POD alarms forever without healing; F2 the same-run stale-lease branch closes a lease ReclaimedBySpare while leaving its live pod on the node a swap pod then hard-targets; F3 a terminal run's Terminating pod (graceful deletion, unfiltered by bridge load()) panics INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS when a different healthy run is reconciled; F4 the checkpoint-grace declined-swap branch strands the spare holder pod so it is never re-provisioned after re-admission; F5 a mint-after-terminal race is swept and reported as Shirked though nothing actually shirked a duty. The investigator also mutation-tested the branch's two defenses (releaseRun's pod cull and reclaimSquatter's fail-closed funding guard), confirming both load-bearing, and verified checkpoint grace is safe from the sweep and both new invariants (keyed positively on terminal phase, pinned by TestSweepLeavesARunInCheckpointGraceAlone). Several scout leads were examined and refuted, including reclaimSquatter partial-closure exits, phases-map post-loop sweep commutativity, outside-lock fenced() authority, leaseGroupIndex migration completeness, and the node_failure_test width narrowing.

answers.question: For every site the change closes a Lease: whose container is still running? For every site it deletes a Pod: whose lease is still open? Name each pairing. Bridge.apply deletes exactly the pods absent from state.Pods, which is why forgetting is silent.
answers.conclusion: Closure sites and their pod half: settle.go:172 (sweep) — pods of the same doomed run dropped in the same function, same bridge pass, PAIRED. run_controller.go:1202 (pass-1 spare on failed node) and 1428 (dead group's active) — the container is on a FENCED node (deleted or out-of-service), provably stopped and force-deleted by Pod GC; paired by fencing semantics. 1261 (run==nil) — pods swept by cleanupDeletedRun and, since this branch, by SettleLeases' orphan-run rule in the same WithWorld pass, PAIRED. 1297 (same-run 'stale' lease on the spare's slots) — UNPAIRED: no pod removal exists on this branch (removeSparePodOnNodes filters RoleSpare only); compiled repro shows the stale lease closed, its active pod alive on node-b, and a swap pod hard-targeted at the same node (finding F2). 1346/1347 (swap) — spare pod removed via removeSparePodOnNodes, failed active's pod on the fenced node, PAIRED. 1437 (SwapDeclined spare): Failed branch paired via the post-loop releaseRun; checkpoint-GRACE branch UNPAIRED — the spare holder pod survives its closed lease indefinitely (finding F4). 1540 (reclaimSquatter) — removeRunPodsOnNodes over the same node set, guarded fail-closed; mutation-verified PAIRED. 1664 (releaseRun) — removeAllRunPods inside the same function; mutation-verified PAIRED. 1795 (applyResolution actions) — pods dropped via closedGroups keyed run::group; paired given INV-GROUP-STAMPED (an unstamped legacy lease would yield GroupIndex "" and skip the pod drop, but the clean-break upgrade policy excludes live unstamped leases). 2740/2743 (shrink) — removePodsForGroups(removed), PAIRED. reconcilers.go:105 (cleanupDeletedRun) — drops the run's pods below, PAIRED. Pod-deletion sites in the reverse direction all close their leases first or in the same call (removeSparePodOnNodes/removeRunPodsOnNodes/removeAllRunPods/removePodsForGroups/SettleLeases). Pod-creates-before-lease (emitSwapPod, emitCohortPods, emitSparePods) are the legal mint window and the oracle's AwaitingMint gate models it. The one pairing NOBODY owns: a pod deleted by the WORLD (drain, eviction, kubectl delete) leaves its lease open with no closer anywhere — Reconcile's Running branch returns without looking at pods, the pod watch DeleteFunc returns false, and SettleLeases skips non-terminal existing runs (finding F1, compiled repro: open after 20 simulated hours, swept=0).

answers.question: The checkpoint-grace window is a DELIBERATE, bounded half-plane state: the run parks Pending with a deadline and its containers keep running so they can write a checkpoint. Does the change reach it? Any sweep or cull that does is a reaper. Check the exemption keys on the deadline or on non-terminality, not on a phase name that could change.
answers.conclusion: The change does not reach the grace window, and the keying is in the safe direction. SettleLeases builds its doomed set POSITIVELY from run.Status.Phase == RunPhaseFailed || RunPhaseComplete (settle.go:131) plus Run-object absence — it enumerates the states it destroys rather than exempting the ones it spares, so a grace run (Pending + CheckpointDeadline) is untouched whatever the grace phase is named, and a hypothetical new terminal phase would be MISSED (fail-safe: sweeps nothing) rather than reaped. TestSweepLeavesARunInCheckpointGraceAlone pins it. Both new invariants gate on the same positive terminal pair (invariant projection: Terminal := phase==Failed||Complete), and INV-LEASE-HAS-POD cannot fire during grace because the grace containers ARE the pods (fires only on Pods==0). Grace termination is deadline-keyed, not phase-keyed: Reconcile line 167 tests run.Status.CheckpointDeadline against the clock before failRun→releaseRun culls the containers. The quiescence driver deliberately walks through grace (a third of generated runs carry spec.runtime.checkpoint). One adjacent defect: the grace branch of failGroupWithoutSpare itself creates an UNMANAGED half-plane remnant — it closes the spare's lease (SwapDeclined) and removes no spare pod; the Failed branch's releaseRun would cull it, but the grace branch culls nothing, and on re-admission emitSparePods counts the stale holder pod as an existing spare (run_controller.go:2062-2069) so the spare is never re-minted (finding F4, pre-existing, half-fixed by this branch).

answers.question: Take each fix in this change. State, concretely, the legal state its repair would destroy if applied one step more aggressively. (Example: "close all the victim's leases" evicts funded work, because funding class is per-LEASE and one run can hold an Owned and an Unfunded lease at once.) If you cannot name one, say so — but look first at pkg/invariant's four rejected invariants.
answers.conclusion: Per fix: (1) SettleLeases — one step further is Fable's rule 2 ('open Spare with no open Active of its group is an orphan'), which reaps healthy standby during every ordinary admission because the plugin mints per pod and a spare legally precedes its actives; kept refuted-executable in TestSweepLeavesASpareOnlyRunAlone. Keying doomed on 'not Running' instead of the terminal pair would reap the checkpoint-grace run. (2) releaseRun (F4/F5/F7) — calling it on the nearest non-terminal state, the grace-parked Pending run, deletes the containers mid-checkpoint-write; that is exactly why its precondition is terminality, currently enforced only by the doc comment ('CALL IT ONLY ON A TERMINAL RUN'), and an in-function terminality assert is blocked by completeRun calling releaseRun BEFORE setting Phase=Complete — reorder completeRun first if hardening. (3) reclaimSquatter — closing all the victim's leases or its whole pod set evicts the victim's Owned lease, because class is per-LEASE; my mutation removing the fail-closed guard turned TestSwapDeclinesWhenTheSquattersFundedWorkSharesTheMachine red ('the swap evicted FUNDED work'). Killing (Failed) instead of demoting (Pending) destroys legally reclaimable work (R14). (4) the ev-taken-once choice (#54) — 'fixing' it by re-evaluating per iteration reintroduces class-3 order dependence, and a judge's mutation showed sparing the artifact-funded squatter fails a genuinely funded victim instead. (5) #36 refix — one step further is treating NotReady/cordon as the verdict, which is R21: two live copies of one rank. (6) R28b no-default leaseGroupIndex — one step further is closing or refusing unstamped leases at load, which reaps every pre-upgrade lease; the shipped shape only refuses to invent '0'. (7) The oracle itself — the four documented rejected invariants each reap a named legal state (tolerated slot oversubscription; cordoned-node leases = R21 rebuilt; spare-before-active mint; the swap window's zero-open-active Running run), and WidthAssembled measured on base-gang width was a shipped reaper this branch itself had to fix (R27g/h): the resolver legally cuts a malleable run's base group while grow ranks cover the minimum.

answers.question: Does the change add or strengthen an INVARIANT? If so, find a legal state this engine really produces that would trip it. An invariant that is wrong is not a weaker safety net; it is a reaper that panics CI on healthy code and argues for a fix that rebuilds a shipped corruption.
answers.conclusion: Yes — INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS, INV-LEASE-HAS-POD, and the sweep's Shirked-panics-under-test rail. All three are trippable by states a real cluster routinely produces. (a) INV-LEASE-HAS-POD: any external pod deletion (node drain, eviction, kubectl delete) of a Running run's pod. Compiled repro: the lease stays open forever (no engine handler; the pod watch's DeleteFunc returns false; the Running branch of Reconcile returns without reading pods; SettleLeases sweeps 0), and the invariant fires on EVERY subsequent reconcile. In production that is a permanently-nonzero jobtree_invariant_violations_total for a state the engine cannot repair; under test it panics any future test that models eviction — and the quiescence generator has NO pod-delete event ('Nothing in this file … deletes a pod'), so 800 green seeds never challenge it. The violation is arguably a true detection of a real pre-existing gap (the R9 9A-3 failure edge), so the right fix is an engine path that closes leases for externally-lost workload, not deleting the invariant — but as shipped the invariant asserts a property nothing maintains. (b) INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS: the graceful-termination window. Bridge.apply deletes pods with default grace; load() lists pods with no DeletionTimestamp filter; for up to terminationGracePeriodSeconds after every failure-path releaseRun (forever in envtest, which has no kubelet), the world contains a terminal run with a Terminating pod. Compiled repro: reconciling a DIFFERENT healthy run panics on it. Production: violation counter increments after every failed run; envtest: a latent panic on healthy code the first time someone writes the failure-path envtest. (c) Shirked: a lease the plugin mints at PreBind after releaseRun's snapshot was loaded lands on an already-terminal run; the next pass sweeps it as 'terminal-run' and, under test, panics with 'some path shirked a duty' — the identical two-process race shape the orphan-run rule was explicitly excused for, but not this rule.

answers.question: Assume every finding you raised is real. For each: which budget is charged, how many GPUs are held, and for how long? An immortal lease is invisible — nothing crashes, nothing is logged, the cluster quietly gets smaller and someone quietly gets poorer. Quantify it.
answers.conclusion: F1 (external pod loss, lease immortal): the lease's PaidByBudget/PaidByEnvelope is charged for len(Slice.Nodes) GPUs — 1 GPU in the repro, all 8 of an 8-GPU node in a routine drain — for UNBOUNDED time: 20 simulated hours confirmed no self-heal, and the only exits are run completion (impossible: runGangComplete needs the pods that are gone), run deletion by a human, or node fencing. The owner's envelope concurrency is consumed, blocking their future admissions, and the ledger marks the slots occupied, shrinking the schedulable cluster. Post-branch it is at least VISIBLE (violations counter) but never healed. F2 (same-run ledger-only eviction): the budget is UNDER-charged — billing stops while the container computes free — and 2 GPUs (repro) are physically held on the node the swap pod is hard-pinned to; if they exhaust the node the swap pod never binds and the gang wedges: a 96-GPU run then charges ~94 GPUs' worth of open leases for zero progress until a human intervenes. F3 (TerminalNoPods on Terminating pods): zero GPUs and zero dollars; the cost is alarm integrity — jobtree_invariant_violations_total, positioned as evidence of illegal state, becomes routinely nonzero for ≥30s after every failed run (minutes for checkpoint-on-SIGTERM grace periods), plus a latent envtest panic. F4 (stranded spare holder pod in grace): groupGPUs GPUs (typically 2) held UNBILLED (lease closed) for the run's remaining lifetime — days to weeks for training jobs — and the run silently loses the swap coverage it paid for, so the next node failure parks or kills a run that owned a spare. F5 (mint-after-terminal race swept as Shirked): seconds-to-minutes of one lease's charge, self-healed by the sweep; the cost is jobtree_swept_leases_total{rule="terminal-run"} — documented alert-on-any-nonzero — firing on a benign race, and a flaky panic in any test running the plugin concurrently.

findings.title: External pod loss (drain/eviction) leaves a Running run's lease immortal
findings.failure_scenario: A Running run's pod deleted by the world (drain/eviction/kubectl) leaves an open lease with no closer anywhere: Reconcile's Running branch returns without reading pods, the pod watch DeleteFunc returns false, SettleLeases skips non-terminal existing runs, and INV-LEASE-HAS-POD fires on every reconcile forever. Compiled repro (scratchpad module): 20 reconciles over 20 simulated hours — lease closed=false, phase=Running, INV-LEASE-HAS-POD panic on every call, SettleLeases swept=0.
findings.taxonomyClass: 1 immortal-lease / 9 half-plane (plus a new-invariant false-fire)

findings.title: Same-run stale-lease branch closes lease but leaves active pod on the swap-targeted node
findings.failure_scenario: Pass 2's same-run 'stale lease on the spare's slots' branch closes the lease ReclaimedBySpare and removes no pod (removeSparePodOnNodes filters RoleSpare only), then emitSwapPod hard-targets the same node — a ledger-only eviction of exactly the shape the branch's cross-run fix exists to prevent. Compiled repro: stale lease closed=true reason=ReclaimedBySpare, its active pod still present on node-b, 1 swap pod emitted with required affinity to node-b.
findings.taxonomyClass: 9 half-plane

findings.title: INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS fires on routine graceful pod termination
findings.failure_scenario: load() does not filter pods with a DeletionTimestamp, so for the graceful-termination window after every failure-path releaseRun (forever in envtest, which has no kubelet) the world contains a terminal run with a Terminating pod; INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS then fires from any engine entry point — production alert noise on routine failures, latent panic for any future failure-path envtest. Compiled repro: reconciling a different, healthy run panics INV-TERMINAL-NO-PODS on the dead run's lingering pod.
findings.taxonomyClass: reaper-invariant (new class candidate: invariant blind to API deletion latency)

findings.title: Declined-swap checkpoint-grace path strands the spare holder pod, blocking future spare re-mint
findings.failure_scenario: On the declined-swap checkpoint-grace path the spare's lease is closed (SwapDeclined) but the spare HOLDER pod (sleep-forever, GPU-holding) is never removed; on re-admission emitSparePods counts it as an existing spare (lines 2062-2069) and a bound pod is never re-scheduled, so no spare lease is ever minted again — one GPU-group held unbilled for the run's lifetime plus silently lost swap coverage. TestDecliningTheSwapReleasesTheSpareEvenWhenTheRunParksInCheckpointGrace asserts only the lease plane for the spare.
findings.taxonomyClass: 9 half-plane

findings.title: Mint-after-terminal race is swept and reported as Shirked though nothing shirked a duty
findings.failure_scenario: A lease the plugin mints at PreBind after releaseRun's world snapshot was loaded lands on an already-terminal run and is swept next pass as 'terminal-run' — Shirked panics under test and trips the alert-on-nonzero terminal-run metric, though no code path shirked anything; the identical two-process race shape was explicitly excused for the orphan-run rule and not for this one. Not reproduced (spans two processes); reasoning basis: bridge lists leases once per pass, releaseRun closes only listed leases, PreBind mints concurrently, and SweepTerminalRun's doc claims every such closure 'is a bug in whichever path made the run terminal.'
findings.taxonomyClass: signal-not-reality (a race read as a shirked duty)

unsupported: repro_artifacts field (scratchpad file paths) has no equivalent required field in schema and was omitted
adjudicated_leads array (refuted scout leads) has no equivalent field in schema and was omitted
id fields on findings (F1-F5) have no equivalent field in schema and were omitted

result.summary: I reviewed controllers/settle.go (SettleLeases) and its wiring through Bridge.WithWorld/reportSweep, tracing Bridge.load, the NodeReconciler fencing verdict, cleanupDeletedRun, releaseRun/completeRun, the RunReference type, the plugin's PreBind mint (admission.PodLeaseWithRole), and the invariant oracle. I did not speculate: I built a scratch module with a replace directive against /workspaces/jobtree and exercised both the pure engine (controllers.SettleLeases over a ClusterState) and the real controllers/kube.Bridge over a controller-runtime fake client. The core sweep logic is sound against sole-committer states and against the cross-process plugin race: because a Run causally precedes its pods and its leases by many reconcile cycles, because each APIReader List is individually consistent, because terminal is a stable phase, and because a lease-create event always re-triggers a WithWorld pass whose sweep re-closes it, I could NOT construct a state where the sweep destroys a live, sole-committer-funded run's work or leaves an immortal lease via the plugin race — it heals those races rather than causing them. I DID confirm one concrete way it destroys something it should not: an empty-valued run-name label (which client.HasLabels admits into the world) collapses to the doomed key "namespace/" and makes the sweep silently delete that pod through the API and close any empty-RunRef lease, accusing nobody. Its blast radius is confined to malformed, non-sole-committer objects. I also confirmed the terminal-run panic path is effectively redundant with the engine's deferred oracle (which fires first on the identical predicate), so Q3's envtest-flake fear is unfounded, and I flag a pre-existing name-only-RunRef immortal-lease that the orphan rule advertises but structurally cannot cover.

answers.question: 1. SettleLeases derives `doomed` from state.Runs (terminal) and from leases+pods whose run key is ABSENT from state.Runs. Bridge.load lists Runs with APIReader (uncached). Enumerate every way state.Runs can be missing a Run that actually exists...
answers.conclusion: Traced Bridge.load (bridge.go:168-262). It issues six SEPARATE uncached APIReader.List calls (Runs first at :170, Leases at :178, Pods at :190) with NO namespace/field selector, so they are not one transaction. List errors are NOT swallowed: every List returns fmt.Errorf(...) up through WithWorld, aborting the pass before any sweep. Pagination is not a gap: the Lists set no Limit, so the apiserver returns the full collection with no continue token. The direct APIReader is cluster-scoped (it bypasses the cache), so no manager namespace filter applies; a namespace/RBAC restriction would surface as a 403 error (returned), not a silent subset. The one theoretical miss — a Run listed-before-it-existed while its lease/pod is listed-after — is causally unreachable: a lease exists only after the bridge created the pod and the plugin minted at PreBind, which is seconds-to-minutes after the Run was created and reconciled, so any Run whose lease is visible was created well before List Runs. Conclusion: the orphan rule fires only for GENUINELY deleted Runs (correct cleanup); it cannot orphan a live Run.

answers.question: 2. The plugin mints a Lease at PreBind, concurrently with the controller process. Construct the interleaving... Is that correct, or does it kill a pod the plugin has just funded? Now the reverse... What closes THAT lease? Is it immortal?
answers.conclusion: Forward case: Run R goes Failed while the plugin binds a straggler pod P and mints lease L for it after the failRun pass loaded its snapshot; the next WithWorld loads R=Failed + open L + P, and the terminal-run sweep closes L and deletes P. This is CORRECT — R is genuinely dead (gang co-terminates), so L would otherwise be an immortal lease and P a zombie container; settle heals it. It is flagged Shirked (a terminal-run closure) which panics under test but only logs+heals in production. Reverse case is NOT immortal: after the sweep closes L1/drops P, if the plugin mints L2 for P, the L2 create fires leaseToRun -> RunReconciler -> WithWorld, and because R stays terminal (or absent), that pass's sweep closes L2; every subsequent mint re-triggers the same closing pass, and once P is deleted the scheduler stops binding and the plugin stops minting, so it converges. Settle is the mechanism that PREVENTS the immortal lease here, not one that creates it.

answers.question: 3. reportSweep calls invariant.Report — which PANICS under go test — for rule=terminal-run... Note that Reconcile's own deferred oracle would have panicked first — would it? Prove the two checks cannot disagree.
answers.conclusion: In WithWorld the deferred oracle runs at fn's return (bridge.go:113) BEFORE SettleLeases (bridge.go:118); nothing mutates state between them, so both read the identical post-fn world. invariant.CheckSteady's TerminalPresent (invariant.go:250) fires iff a terminal run holds an open lease — the exact predicate settle's Shirked uses. So in any pass whose fn is an engine entry point (Reconcile/ActivateReservations/HandleNodeFailure all defer c.checkInvariants), the oracle panics first and settle's Report is unreachable; the two cannot disagree. Settle's Report is reachable only in a NON-engine WithWorld pass (the cleanupDeletedRun branch, or a bare test fn). In envtest there is no scheduler plugin minting leases onto a terminal run, so a terminal-run-with-open-lease can only arise from a test that hand-builds that illegal state — which the oracle exists to catch — not from a legitimate reconcile sequence. So it is a genuine finding when it fires, not a flake.

answers.question: 4. Sweep.Pods drops pods by keys.NamespacedKey(pod.Namespace, pod.Labels[LabelRunName]). What happens to a pod whose run-name label is empty or missing?... does HasLabels match a label present but EMPTY?
answers.conclusion: CONFIRMED by execution. A pod with the label MISSING is filtered out at load by client.HasLabels{LabelRunName} (bridge.go:190) and never enters state.Pods — safe. But HasLabels compiles to an EXISTS requirement, which matches a key whose value is EMPTY: my Bridge-over-fake-client repro proved the stray pod stray-empty-label (label value "") was loaded, then DELETED through the API. Its key is keys.NamespacedKey(ns, "") = "default/", which is never a real Run key, so doomed["default/"]=orphan-run and both the empty-label pod (settle.go:180) and any lease with empty RunRef.Name (settle.go:143) are reaped, with shirked=0 (accuses nobody). A healthy Running run in the same namespace was untouched, so blast radius is confined to empty-run-name objects, which the sole committer never emits — a robustness gap, not a sole-committer data-loss.

answers.question: 5. The sweep runs on EVERY WithWorld... If fn returns an error mid-admission, the engine's half-applied state is applied AND then swept. Can a half-applied admission look terminal-or-orphan to the sweep? Build it.
answers.conclusion: No. The sweep acts only on runs that are terminal (settle.go:131) or whose Run object is absent. A half-applied admission (Reconcile/ActivateReservations erroring mid-way) leaves the run Pending/Running/Reserved and PRESENT in state.Runs — never terminal and never absent — so the sweep ignores it; its newly-created-in-state leases belong to a present, non-terminal run. The only terminal transitions inside those entry points are completeRun and failRun, and both close leases + drop pods (via releaseRun) BEFORE any subsequent error return, so if the pass errors after them the run is terminal with already-closed leases and no pods, and the sweep finds nothing. The engine does not create Run objects, so a half-applied pass cannot manufacture a new orphan key either. I could not build a half-applied admission that the sweep misreads.

answers.question: 6. cleanupDeletedRun already closes a deleted run's leases and sweeps its pods. SettleLeases' orphan-run rule now does the same thing... Do they agree on the ClosureReason, on the pods, and on the events? Which one wins when both run, and does the second one re-close?
answers.conclusion: They target the identical lease/pod set for a deleted run (leases with matching RunRef, pods with matching namespace+LabelRunName) but DISAGREE on ClosureReason: cleanupDeletedRun stamps "RunDeleted" (reconcilers.go:105) while settle stamps "SweptOrphanRun" (settle.go:closureReasonFor). This is a genuine class-7 cloned obligation, but harmless: when both run in one RunReconciler pass, fn=cleanupDeletedRun runs FIRST and closes the leases; settle then SKIPS them because every settle loop guards if lease.Status.Closed { continue }, so it does NOT re-close, does NOT re-stamp, and INV-CLOSED-MONOTONE is preserved. cleanupDeletedRun wins in that pass; settle wins only in a non-Run-reconciler pass (e.g. a node event) that races ahead of cleanup. The reason divergence is informational only; the two never double-close and never disagree on which pods die.

answers.question: 7. Write the test for the single most dangerous case you found. Run it. Paste the output.
answers.conclusion: Done — I exercised the empty-run-name reaping (Q4) two ways against the real code. Pure engine: SettleLeases over a ClusterState with a live Running run plus a stray empty-label pod printed 'closed leases: 1, dropped pods: 1 / closed mystery-lease runKey=default/ rule=orphan-run / surviving pods: train-active-0 / train-lease closed? false ; mystery-lease closed? true / shirked: 0'. Real Bridge over a controller-runtime fake client (same harness as settle_bridge_test.go): after one no-op WithWorld, 'pods remaining after sweep: 1 (survivor: train-active-0), stray-empty-label still exists? false (err=pods "stray-empty-label" not found), healthy train-lease closed? false'. This proves HasLabels admits the empty-valued label, the sweep deletes that pod all the way to the API, and the healthy run is untouched. Scratch program at /tmp/claude-1000/-workspaces-jobtree/b3df7d06-fa76-4793-98b7-31b44b11906e/scratchpad/repro/pure_test_repro.go.
