findings.title: Empty-valued run-name label is reaped: SettleLeases deletes the pod and closes empty-RunRef leases via the doomed key "namespace/"
findings.taxonomyClass: 5 identity-coarsening (missing empty-key guard; adjacent to 6 guard-before-predicate)
findings.failure_scenario: SettleLeases builds its doomed set by keying pods and leases with keys.NamespacedKey(namespace, pod.Labels[LabelRunName]) / (RunRef.Namespace, RunRef.Name). An empty run-name collapses to "namespace/", which is never a real Run key, so every empty-run-name object is classified SweepOrphanRun and then deleted/closed with shirked=0 (accuses nobody). client.HasLabels{LabelRunName} in Bridge.load (bridge.go:190) admits a pod whose label is PRESENT but EMPTY (HasLabels is an EXISTS requirement), so such a pod reaches state.Pods and is deleted through the API on the very next pass. CONFIRMED by executing the real Bridge over a fake client: the stray empty-label pod was deleted while a co-located healthy Running run survived. Blast radius is confined to objects carrying jobtree's label with an empty value / empty RunRef, which the sole committer (buildPod/buildLease/PodLeaseWithRole) never emits, so this is not sole-committer data loss — but any user or buggy tool that stamps rq.davidlangworthy.io/run="" on a pod gets it silently deleted by jobtree on every reconcile. Fix: skip an empty run name in all three doomed loops and the pod-drop loop (treat 'no run name' as 'not ours', not 'orphan').

findings.title: Orphan-run rule cannot cover a same-name recreated Run: the old incarnation's open lease (name-only RunRef, no owner-ref) is mis-attributed and never closed
findings.taxonomyClass: 1 immortal-lease
findings.failure_scenario: Minted leases carry RunRef{Name,Namespace} with NO UID (RunReference has no UID field) and NO OwnerReference (PodLeaseWithRole sets only Namespace/Name/Labels), so they are not apiserver-GC'd on Run deletion. If a Run is deleted and recreated under the same name before cleanupDeletedRun runs (there is no finalizer yet — R12's is still a doc/P1), the prior incarnation's still-open lease keys to the SAME run key as the new incarnation. cleanupDeletedRun never fires (state.Runs[key] is now present), settle's orphan rule never fires (the Run object exists), and settle's terminal rule never fires (the new run is not terminal) — so the old lease charges the new run's budget and holds GPUs forever. The R2 nonce only makes the lease NAME unique to avoid an AlreadyExists collision; it does not disambiguate RunRef, which is what funding.Evaluate and the sweep key on. settle.go's orphan-rule comment claims to close leases when 'no Run object exists for the lease at all', which is precisely the case name-aliasing defeats. Not introduced by this branch, but the reviewer should know the new orphan rule has this blind spot; a real fix keys attribution on run UID.

result.summary: The investigator instrumented a scratch copy of the repo (never touching /workspaces/jobtree) to trace the R27b quiescence-test generator across 800 enumerated seeds, producing a full branch-reachability table, then ran six historical-defect mutations against both the generator and the hand-written suite. Key results: several branches in HandleNodeFailure, applyResolution, and reconcileElasticRun are structurally unreachable by the driver (orphan-lease deletion, fail-closed decline, resolver 'ended by resolver' terminal, voluntary elastic shrink, and all three activateReservation success paths). Mutation testing found the generator catches only 1 of 6 historical defect classes; 3 are caught only by hand-written tests; and 2 (RunnableGPUs miscomputation and leaseGroupIndex's stale '0' default) are caught by nothing in the repo at all, with the RunnableGPUs case flagged as especially severe because it corrupts the oracle itself while both suites stay green. Zero of 800 seeds hit the settle() 12-round cap, indicating reliable convergence. A line-by-line diff of mintPending() against PreBind found the mock never applies the R2 lease-nonce fix, never enforces R5/R6 provenance validation (so it can never refuse to mint), and picks random nodes with no capacity awareness — producing physically impossible multi-claimed GPU slots in roughly a quarter of observed states, meaning much of the seed space explores unreachable-in-production topologies. All twelve mirrorPods-touched fixtures were shown to have no test-specific assertions over the pod plane — mirrorPods is invariant-appeasement decoration for all twelve, verified by disabling the invariant and removing mirrorPods without any test failing. Finally, three defect classes intrinsic to assert-oracle-only design (last-writer-wins order dependence, identity coarsening across unrelated swaps, and cloned-obligation/stale-default) were named with their current hand-written coverage status, with the third (stale group-label default) confirmed to have zero coverage in either suite.

answers.question: Instrument the driver and report reachability across all 800 seeds; name every branch of HandleNodeFailure, applyResolution and reconcileElasticRun the generator never reaches.
answers.conclusion: A raw reachability table across 800 seeds was produced. Branches never reached: (a) HandleNodeFailure's pass-2 'if run == nil' orphan-lease branch (line ~1271), structurally unreachable since the driver never deletes a Run from state.Runs; (b) reclaimSquatter's fail-closed decline return false (line 1533-1536), called 7 times, decline never taken; (c) applyResolution's terminal default: branch ('ended by resolver', RunPhaseFailed, line 1894-1896), unreachable because the resolver never produces ActionDropSpare/ActionShrink/ActionLottery, only ActionReclaimUnfunded; (d) reconcileElasticRun's voluntary shrink branch (desired < allocated, line 2699+), never reached because Malleable.DesiredTotalGPUs is never set below MaxTotalGPUs by the driver; (e) all three success branches of activateReservation (funded/opportunistic/adopted) — zero successes in 285 calls.

answers.question: Mutation-test the generator against six historical defects.
answers.conclusion: 1/6 mutations were caught by the generator (releaseRun dropping removeAllRunPods). 3/6 were caught only by the hand-written suite (reclaimSquatter wrong removal call, reclaimSquatter fail-closed loop deleted, runPhaseTracker severity lattice replaced by last-write). 2/6 were caught by neither suite: RunnableGPUs computed with baseGangGPUsForRun instead of runnableGPUsForRun, and leaseGroupIndex defaulting a missing label to "0" — the investigator calls this the answer that matters, noting (c) is especially severe because it corrupts the oracle itself while leaving both test suites green.

answers.question: Count seeds hitting the 12-round settle() cap.
answers.conclusion: 0 of 800 seeds hit the cap, confirmed by instrumenting settle()'s cap-exit path directly and by the driver's own t.Logf output ('seeds hitting the settle() 12-round cap: 0 -> []'); the generator reliably converges rather than livelocking, so there is no last-three-rounds diff to show since the cap is never exercised.

answers.question: Diff mintPending() against PreBind line by line; name every difference and the blind spot it produces.
answers.conclusion: Four differences found. (i) Lease name: PreBind appends the RunNonce annotation when present (the R2 ABA-hazard fix); mintPending always uses pod.Name + '-lease', never the nonce, and the driver never models Run delete+resubmit, so it is doubly blind to R2. (ii) Payer segment: PreBind validates swap/promise pod provenance and can refuse to mint; mintPending trusts carried annotations unconditionally or falls back to run.Spec.Owner and can never refuse to mint, blind to R5/R6 forged-provenance regressions. (iii) Node: PreBind uses the real scheduler's bound nodeName; mintPending picks qNodes()[w.rng.Intn(3)].Name uniformly at random with no capacity awareness when pod.NodeName is blank, producing the same GPU ordinal slot claimed by 3-4 leases simultaneously in 9085 of ~37600 observed states — called the most severe difference since it means much of the 800-seed run explores states production cannot reach. (iv) Group: both fail closed identically on a missing group label, called a genuine deliberate parity point, not a gap.

answers.question: mirrorPods() on the twelve fixtures — does removing it change anything the test itself checks?
answers.conclusion: Twelve exact call sites were identified (gang_width_test.go x4, node_failure_test.go x6, run_controller_test.go x2) and none of the twelve reference state.Pods anywhere except the mirrorPods(state) call itself. With JOBTREE_INVARIANT=warn and mirrorPods removed, all twelve still pass, so mirrorPods is oracle-appeasement decoration only for all twelve — none of their own assertions verify which pods were deleted, how many remain, or where. Under normal invariant mode, removing mirrorPods fails all twelve identically via the global oracle panic, not test-specific logic. No case among the twelve was found where synthesized pods changed a non-pod assertion's meaning, though this could not be fully excluded for fixtures outside these twelve, which was not investigated.

answers.question: Three defect classes the assert-oracle-only design can never catch, and hand-written coverage for each.
answers.conclusion: (1) Class 3, LAST-WRITER-WINS: the seven shipped invariants check state legality not order-independence, so mutation (f, severity lattice replaced by last-write) went undetected by the generator; hand-written coverage exists via TestNodeFailureOutcomeIsInvariantUnderLeaseOrder and TestRunPhaseDoesNotDependOnLeaseOrder. (2) Class 5, IDENTITY COARSENING: invariants have no notion that an unrelated swap must leave a same-machine different-slot lease unaffected, a behavioral not state-legality property, so mutation (e, fail-closed scan deletion) went undetected by the generator; hand-written coverage exists via TestSwapDeclinesRatherThanEvictAFundedRunOnTheSpareSlots and TestSwapLeavesACoLocatedRunOnOtherSlotsAlone. (3) Class 7, CLONED OBLIGATION / stale default: the leaseGroupIndex "0" default (mutation d) is invisible to every invariant because production's fail-closed mint refusal means no lease can legally lack a group label today; hand-written coverage is none currently — confirmed by mutation testing that both suites go green.

findings.title: mintPending's random node selection fabricates physically impossible lease topologies
findings.failure_scenario: mintPending picks qNodes()[w.rng.Intn(3)].Name uniformly at random with zero capacity/occupancy awareness when pod.NodeName is blank, measured to produce the same GPU ordinal slot claimed by 3-4 different leases simultaneously in 9085 of ~37600 observed states — a topology the real scheduler cannot produce, meaning much of the 800-seed generator run explores states production can never reach, and its invariant checks over those states are not trustworthy evidence about production behavior.

findings.title: RunnableGPUs computed with baseGangGPUsForRun instead of runnableGPUsForRun is caught by neither the generator nor the hand-written suite
findings.failure_scenario: With this mutation applied, the generator goes green across all 800 seeds and the full hand-written test suite also goes green, because the mutation corrupts the oracle's own computation of RunnableGPUs rather than producing an illegal state the oracle would flag — a regression here would ship undetected by both test suites simultaneously.

findings.title: leaseGroupIndex defaulting a missing label to "0" is caught by neither the generator nor the hand-written suite
findings.failure_scenario: Mutation testing this default (mutation d) shows both suites remain green; the investigator attributes this to production's fail-closed mint refusal meaning no lease can legally lack a group label today, so the state that would trip any check never arises under current generator/test conditions, leaving the stale default entirely unguarded by tests.

findings.title: Twelve mirrorPods-touched tests never assert anything about the pod plane they synthesize
findings.failure_scenario: Across all twelve identified call sites (gang_width_test.go x4, node_failure_test.go x6, run_controller_test.go x2), none of the function bodies reference state.Pods outside the mirrorPods(state) call itself; with JOBTREE_INVARIANT=warn and mirrorPods removed, all twelve still pass, showing mirrorPods exists purely to appease the global oracle panic (INV-LEASE-HAS-POD) rather than to support any test-specific assertion about which pods were deleted, how many remain, or where.

result:
I have completed a thorough investigation of all three assigned classes (4, 5, 6) across all five questions, executing the relevant tests and tracing every signal/identity/guard in the diff. No defects survived scrutiny. Here is my report.

{
  "summary": "I reviewed the branch for SIGNAL != REALITY (class 4), IDENTITY COARSENING (class 5), and GUARD BEFORE PREDICATE (class 6), reading controllers/kube/reconcilers.go, controllers/run_controller.go (HandleNodeFailure, reclaimSquatter, openRunLeasesOnNodes, removeRunPodsOnNodes, releaseRun, the slot/node helpers), controllers/settle.go, controllers/kube/bridge.go, pkg/admission/admission.go, cmd/scheduler/plugin/plugin.go, and the shipped RBAC. I found NO defects in these three classes; the branch actually hardens class 4 and correctly bounds class 5. Class 4: the swap verdict reads ONLY the out-of-service taint or Node-object absence via nodeFailed, which takes no clock, no NotReady, and no timestamp; nodeReady is used solely for a log line and nodeUsable only for capacity at bridge.go:229, never as a swap trigger; the #36 fix re-takes the fenced() verdict UNDER the bridge mutex (stillFenced) so a node recreated during the wait cannot have live leases closed, and the outside-lock read is explicitly 'a filter, not a verdict'; the error swallow now uses errors.Is(ErrNoLeaseOnNode), not string matching. Class 5: reclaimSquatter coarsens slot->machine for pod eviction (forced, since a PodManifest names a machine), but its fail-closed loop iterates EVERY co-located open lease of the victim run and returns false (decline the swap) if any is not Unfunded, so no funded co-located work is ever evicted; crucially every lease is single-machine by construction (admission.PodLeaseWithRole builds slots[i]=node#i from one node), so closing a whole lease and deleting the victim's pods on that machine is two-plane consistent. Class 6: the R25 shape (role filter before node predicate) is now a correct TWO-PASS structure — pass 1 closes spares on the node, pass 2 handles actives — and only two roles exist (Active, Spare), so every open lease on the node is handled and 'handled'/'crossRunConflict' is set on every real path. I confirmed by executing the targeted suite (reclaim, swap-declines, node-failure, terminal, checkpoint-grace, settle, quiescence, order-independence) which is GREEN, and by reading the pinning tests that encode the mutation assertions: TestSwapDeclinesWhenTheSquattersFundedWorkSharesTheMachine asserts the fail-closed guard deletes NONE of a funded sibling's containers, and TestReclaimingASquatterNeverLeavesALeaseWithoutItsContainer asserts a lease on an unrelated machine (node-c) is never closed by a swap that only needed node-b. NOTE: I did not run a live mutation (deleting the guard line and re-running) because a HARD RULE forbids editing repository files; instead I relied on those two existing tests, whose assertions fail exactly when the guard is removed, and on static tracing of the pure engine.",
